name: Home
title: Home
description: Homepage that loads an overview of the metal products and services, provides a global product search, and routes users quickly into product categories, specific products, and key services such as laser cutting and emergency repair.

core_features: Load home overview data (hero text, featured product categories, featured services, featured products) via getHomeOverview and expose it to other components.
Load the main active product categories for navigation via getMainProductCategories.
Load the list of main services for navigation tiles via getServicesOverview.
Provide a global product search form that routes to products.html with the search term as a URL parameter.
Display featured product category tiles that route to products.html with the selected category slug as a URL parameter.
Display featured services and service navigation tiles with direct navigation to service_detail.html using the serviceId URL parameter, and to services.html for the complete services list.
Display a featured products grid from the overview data, with each product card linking to product.html using the product id URL parameter.
Provide an introductory overview header using hero content and a link to the About page.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Browse products by category from Home
1) On page load, the home-data-loader component calls getHomeOverview and getMainProductCategories.
2) When data is returned, the main-category-nav component displays the primary categories and the featured-category-tiles component displays highlighted categories.
3) The user clicks a category item (from either list).
4) The click handler reads the category slug from the clicked item and navigates the browser to products.html?category={category.slug}.
5) The products page then uses the category URL parameter to filter its list (handled on that page).

Workflow: Search for a specific product from Home
1) On page load, the home-data-loader initializes the global-search-form (no pre-filled query).
2) The user types a search term into the global search input.
3) The user submits the form (e.g., pressing Enter or clicking the search button).
4) The global-search-form onSubmit handler URL-encodes the search term and navigates to products.html?query={encodedSearchTerm}.
5) The products page then reads the query parameter and performs the search (handled on that page).

Workflow: Open a featured product from Home
1) On page load, home-data-loader calls getHomeOverview and passes featured_products data to the featured-products-grid component.
2) The featured-products-grid renders product cards bound to Product fields from the response.
3) The user clicks a product card.
4) The product card onClick handler reads the product_id and navigates to product.html?id={product_id}.
5) The product detail page uses the id URL parameter to load and display the selected Product (handled on that page).

Workflow: Access laser cutting service from Home
1) On page load, home-data-loader calls getHomeOverview and getServicesOverview.
2) The featured-services-strip component receives featured_services from getHomeOverview and matches them with the full Service list from getServicesOverview by service_id.
3) The user clicks the specific action for laser cutting (e.g., a button associated with service_id 'laser_cutting').
4) The click handler navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId=laser_cutting.
5) The service detail page uses the serviceId URL parameter to display the laser cutting service and its quote flow (handled on that page).

Workflow: Access emergency repair service from Home
1) On page load, home-data-loader calls getHomeOverview and getServicesOverview.
2) The featured-services-strip displays any service whose service_id corresponds to emergency repair data in the responses.
3) The user clicks the emergency repair tile or action.
4) The click handler navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={service.service_id} for that emergency repair service.
5) The service detail page uses the serviceId URL parameter to show emergency repair information and request flow (handled on that page).

Workflow: Navigate to About page from Home
1) On page load, home-data-loader calls getHomeOverview and passes hero data to the home-overview-header component.
2) The home-overview-header component displays hero_title, hero_subtitle, and a generic link to learn more about the company.
3) The user clicks the link.
4) The click handler navigates to about.html without additional URL parameters.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Window/document load event for index.html.
- Behavior: home-data-loader sequentially or concurrently calls getHomeOverview(), getMainProductCategories(), and getServicesOverview(). It manages loading and error states until data is available, then distributes the results as props/state to dependent components.

Interaction: Global search submit
- Trigger: global-search-form onSubmit.
- Behavior: Prevent default form submission, read the search input value, trim it, and if non-empty, navigate to products.html?query={encodeURIComponent(searchTerm)}. If empty, optionally keep the user on the same page or show a validation message (client-side only).

Interaction: Click on featured category tile
- Trigger: featured-category-tiles onClick for a category item.
- Behavior: Read the associated category.slug from the bound data and navigate to products.html?category={encodeURIComponent(category.slug)}.

Interaction: Click on main navigation category item
- Trigger: main-category-nav onClick for a category list item.
- Behavior: Read category.slug from the selected ProductCategory and navigate to products.html?category={encodeURIComponent(category.slug)}.

Interaction: Click on featured product card
- Trigger: featured-products-grid onClick for a product card.
- Behavior: Read the product_id from the clicked card's data and navigate to product.html?id={encodeURIComponent(product_id)}.

Interaction: Click on 'View all services'
- Trigger: featured-services-strip onClick on a 'View all services' navigation element.
- Behavior: Navigate to services.html without additional URL parameters.

Interaction: Click on a service tile or service CTA
- Trigger: featured-services-strip onClick for a service tile or CTA button.
- Behavior: Read service.service_id from the bound Service data and navigate to service_detail.html?serviceId={encodeURIComponent(service.service_id)}.

Interaction: Click on About link
- Trigger: home-overview-header onClick on the About-related link.
- Behavior: Navigate to about.html without additional URL parameters.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Home page does not read or interpret incoming URL parameters; its behavior is the same for any direct access to index.html. All dynamic content is obtained from the assigned interfaces on load: getHomeOverview, getMainProductCategories, and getServicesOverview. Transient UI state (such as loading states, error states, or which tab is active in a services strip) is managed in-memory within the page and is not persisted to the URL. URL parameters are used only when the user leaves the Home page: (1) Navigating to products.html with query={searchTerm} when submitting the global search; (2) Navigating to products.html with category={category.slug} when selecting a category; (3) Navigating to product.html with id={product_id} when selecting a product; (4) Navigating to service_detail.html with serviceId={service.service_id} for service CTAs. These outgoing URLs are constructed solely from the live data returned by the interfaces, without hardcoded parameter values.

components.functionality: On page initialization, fetches all required data for the Home page by calling getHomeOverview, getMainProductCategories, and getServicesOverview. Aggregates the results into a shared state object and exposes loading and error states for child components.
event_handlers: onSuccess: store results in page-level state and notify dependent components
onError: store error state and expose it so children can render fallback messaging

components.functionality: Displays the primary overview text for the Home page using hero_title and hero_subtitle from getHomeOverview, and provides a simple navigation link to the About page.
Provides a single input field for users to search across all products. On submit, routes the user to the products listing page with the entered search term encoded in the query URL parameter.
data_binding: Product (search is performed on products.html using the query parameter; Home only forwards the query value)
event_handlers: onSubmit: prevent default, read searchTerm from input, if non-empty navigate to 'products.html?query=' + encodeURIComponent(searchTerm)

components.functionality: Renders a subset of featured product categories from getHomeOverview for quick access. Each tile is clickable and routes to the Products page filtered by that category.
event_handlers: onClick.categoryTile: read category.slug from clicked item and navigate to 'products.html?category=' + encodeURIComponent(category.slug)

components.functionality: Displays the main active product categories from getMainProductCategories as a navigational list or strip, giving users broad entry points into different product families.
event_handlers: onClick.categoryItem: read category.slug and navigate to 'products.html?category=' + encodeURIComponent(category.slug)

components.functionality: Shows key services (e.g., laser cutting, emergency repair, and others) using a combination of featured_services from getHomeOverview and detailed Service data from getServicesOverview. Each service item allows navigation to its detail page, and the component includes a generic link to view all services.
event_handlers: onClick.serviceItem: read service.service_id and navigate to 'service_detail.html?serviceId=' + encodeURIComponent(service.service_id)

components.functionality: Provides a direct, prominent action for requesting or viewing laser cutting service details from the Home page. It leverages the services data to confirm that laser cutting is available.

Displays a grid or list of featured products from getHomeOverview.featured_products, including basic details like name, category_name, size_label, material, profile_type, unit_price, currency, free_shipping flag, stock_status, and thumbnail_image_url. Each product card links to the product detail page.
event_handlers: onClick.productCard: read product_id from clicked card and navigate to 'product.html?id=' + encodeURIComponent(product_id)

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero at the very top containing the industrial photo, the main site/home title, and the global search and key quick action. Immediately below, a strong horizontal divider separates the hero from the body. The body is a two-column band inside a centered content container: a narrow left sidebar for the main category navigation, and a wider right column stacking the home-specific content in order: category tiles at the top, then a featured services strip, then the featured products grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero at the very top containing the industrial photo, the main site/home title, and the global search and key quick action. Immediately below, a strong horizontal divider separates the hero from the body. The body is a two-column band inside a centered content container: a narrow left sidebar for the main category navigation, and a wider right column stacking the home-specific content in order: category tiles at the top, then a featured services strip, then the featured products grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero spans 100% of the viewport width and occupies roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height. Below it, the central content container runs across about 80–90% of the page width, centered, with subtle left/right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar takes approximately 20–25% of the width while the right main content takes 75–80%. Vertical spacing between major blocks (hero → body, sections within the right column) follows a 32–48px rhythm based on an 8px grid, with internal padding for cards and interactive elements around 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is visually open: large background image, a prominent centered title, and a single band of controls (search + quick action) with comfortable breathing room. The two-column body is moderately dense: the left nav items are fairly close vertically with 8–12px gaps, while the right column stacks content sections with clear 32–40px separations but relatively tight internal spacing (16–24px padding, 16–24px gutters in grids), giving a functional, slightly compact feel suitable for a utilitarian, industrial site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow follows an F/Z pattern: the eye lands first on the centered hero title, then moves slightly down to the subtitle and global search, and to the high-contrast quick action. From there, the eye drops to the horizontal divider and then enters the two-column area, where attention is drawn first to the top of the right column (featured category tiles and their heading). As users scan downward through categories, the gaze travels through the services strip and finally into the featured products grid. The left sidebar nav provides a stable vertical anchor that is easy to pick up when the user wants to jump between product areas.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a full-bleed industrial metal fabrication photo that fills the top third to two-fifths of the viewport. Centered over this image is a bold, utilitarian home title and short descriptor, with a wide global search bar and a prominent laser-cutting quick-action button aligned along the lower portion of the hero content, forming a functional control band. A thick, gray horizontal divider spans the full width below the hero, clearly separating it from the main body. Beneath this, a centered content container splits into two columns: on the left, a narrow vertical navigation listing main product categories; on the right, a broader content column where a grid of featured category tiles appears near the top, followed by a horizontal strip of key services, and then a more densely packed grid of featured products extending downward. The overall impression is a straightforward, corporate, image-driven layout where the hero sets the industrial tone, the sidebar anchors orientation, and the right column progressively drills down from broad categories to specific services and products.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This non-visual component underpins the entire page and effectively wraps the full layout. It loads just before the hero section, influencing content state but not occupying any visible space. In the document flow, it sits logically at the top of the page structure, with all other components rendered within or after it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual footprint; behaves as an invisible wrapper component that initializes data for all subsequent sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The home-overview-header forms the core of the hero at the very top of the page. It spans the full browser width and sits on top of a large industrial background image that covers roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height. Within this hero, its content is centered horizontally inside a max-width container (around 960–1120px) with generous 32–48px padding from the top and sides. The main home title appears centered both horizontally and roughly in the upper-middle of the hero area, set in large, bold type. A short descriptive line sits directly beneath the title with about 16–24px vertical spacing, still centered. The lower portion of this component’s content area aligns the search form and quick action (from their own components) so that together they read as a control band beneath the text, maintaining generous vertical breathing room above and a 24–32px cushion before the bottom of the hero image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, occupying the full-width hero region; does not scroll with the viewport once passed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The global-search-form sits within the hero, positioned in the lower half of the home-overview-header’s content area. It is horizontally centered within the same max-width container as the title and spans approximately 60–70% of that container’s width on desktop, giving it a substantial, prominent presence. There is about 24px of vertical space between the subtitle text and the top edge of the search form, keeping it visually connected but clearly distinct. The search field itself is a single, wide bar with 16–20px internal padding and a compact search button attached or adjacent on the right. Below the form, a 16–24px gap separates it from the laser-cutting quick action, forming a tight but readable cluster of primary interactions near the bottom center of the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned in normal flow inside the hero; on narrower viewports it shrinks toward 100% of the content width while maintaining its centered placement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The laser-cutting-quick-action is placed immediately adjacent to the global-search-form within the hero, forming part of the same control band. On larger screens, it appears to the right of the search bar, aligned to the same vertical center line as the input field, occupying roughly 20–25% of the content container’s width. It reads as a high-contrast button or compact callout card with 16–24px padding, clearly labeled for laser cutting and emergency-related services. There is a small 16px horizontal gap between the search field and this quick action so that they feel grouped but distinct. On smaller screens where horizontal space is limited, the quick action stacks directly below the search bar, centered, with around 16–24px vertical spacing. In all cases, it sits near the lower third of the hero so that as users scroll, it is one of the last elements from the hero to leave the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the hero but responsive: aligns side-by-side with the search form on wide screens and stacks vertically beneath it on narrow screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The main-category-nav occupies the left sidebar beneath the hero, starting just below the full-width horizontal divider. It is contained within the central page container and takes about 20–25% of that container’s width, aligning flush to the left margin. The nav list runs vertically from near the top of the body section downward, with each nav item spanning the full sidebar width and having 12–16px vertical spacing between labels and 12–16px internal padding. The top of the sidebar has a 24–32px margin from the divider above, and there is a consistent 32–40px gap between the right edge of this sidebar and the left edge of the right main content column, creating a clear column separation. The overall height of the nav is shorter than the full page, so it reads as a compact, scroll-insensitive anchor alongside the taller right-hand content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a left column element within the two-column layout; optionally sticky so that once the user scrolls past the hero, the nav remains pinned near the top of the viewport within its column until its content ends.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-category-tiles component is the first major block in the right main column, positioned directly to the right of the main-category-nav and starting slightly lower than the nav (around 32px below the horizontal divider) to create a subtle stagger. It occupies roughly 75–80% of the central content container’s width. At its top, it includes a clear section heading such as “Browse by Category,” aligned left with 8–16px of space above and about 16–24px space below before the tiles begin. The tiles themselves form a grid with 2–3 columns on desktop (depending on available width), each tile being a card with 16–24px internal padding and 16–24px horizontal and vertical gutters between cards. The grid extends downward for one or two visible rows on initial load, with 32px of space below the last row before the next section begins, matching the moderately dense, utilitarian look of the mockup’s product imagery area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the right column, expanding vertically as more categories are shown; the grid responsively collapses to fewer columns on smaller screens while maintaining consistent 16–24px gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-services-strip sits in the right main column directly below the featured-category-tiles, separated by a 32–40px vertical gap. It spans the full width of the right column and reads as a horizontal band of service callouts, slightly differentiated with a light background or subtle top/bottom border to form a strip. Within it, 3–4 service items are arranged in a single row on desktop, each behaving like a compact card with 16–20px padding and 16–24px horizontal spacing between them. The strip has 16–24px padding on all sides, and its heading or label (e.g., “Key Services”) is aligned left at the top of the strip with about 8–16px spacing before the service items. Below the strip, there is another 32–40px vertical gap before the featured-products-grid begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section within the right column; its internal content wraps to multiple rows on smaller screens but maintains its identity as a horizontal band separating category browsing above from detailed products below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The featured-products-grid is positioned at the bottom of the main content stack in the right column, starting roughly 32–40px below the featured-services-strip. It spans the same 75–80% width of the central content container as the other right-column components. At the top of this component, a concise heading such as “Featured Products” or “In-Demand Stock” appears left-aligned, with a 16–24px gap below it before the product cards. The products are arranged in a denser grid than the category tiles: typically 3–4 columns on desktop with 16–24px gutters, and slightly smaller card padding (around 16px) to increase information density. The grid can extend significantly down the page as more items are loaded, maintaining consistent gutters and a clear left alignment with the content above. The right column’s bottom margin provides 48px or so of space before the page footer, visually closing the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content within the right column; the number of columns adapts responsively to viewport width while preserving the compact, grid-based aesthetic.

name: Products Listing
title: Products
description: Browse and search all metal products with category-based listing, filters, sorting, and quick add-to-cart, using URL parameters to control state.

core_features: Display product listings based on either a selected category or a search query using listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.
Provide filter controls for material, profile type, price range, handrail step range, and free-shipping eligibility using getProductFilterOptions.
Allow sorting of results by different criteria (including price ascending via sortBy=price_asc) and apply that sorting to listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.
Enable users to navigate between top-level product categories using getMainProductCategories.
Allow users to open product detail pages from product cards (navigation to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID).
Support navigation to a configurator for configurable cabinet products from listing items (navigation to configurator.html?baseProductId=PRODUCT_ID) when applicable.
Provide quick add-to-cart from listing items using addProductToCart, with optional cart-view navigation to cart.html.
Manage pagination of products using the pagination information returned by listProductsByCategory or searchProducts and URL parameters for paging.
Persist all listing state (category, query, filters, sorting, pagination) in URL parameters so the view can be shared and reloaded consistently.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Browse products by category
- Step 1: User opens products.html directly or via the main navigation.
- Step 2: Page-init logic reads the URL parameter `category` (if present).
- Step 3: The page calls getMainProductCategories to show available top-level categories.
- Step 4: If `category` is present and no `query` is provided, the page calls getProductFilterOptions(categorySlug=category) to load relevant filters.
- Step 5: The page then calls listProductsByCategory(categorySlug=category, filters derived from URL parameters, sortBy from `sortBy` URL param, and page/pageSize from URL or defaults).
- Step 6: Products are rendered in the product grid; the category navigation highlights the active category.
- Step 7: User changes filters in the sidebar (e.g., selects a material); the page updates the corresponding URL parameters, resets page to the first page, and re-calls listProductsByCategory with the updated filters.
- Step 8: User changes sorting (e.g., selects 'Price - Low to High'); the page updates `sortBy` in the URL and re-calls listProductsByCategory.
- Step 9: User clicks on a product card to view details; the browser navigates to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID.

Workflow 2: Search for a specific product type
- Step 1: User enters text into the search form on products.html (or arrives with a `query` URL parameter from another page).
- Step 2: On search form submit, the page updates the `query` URL parameter (and may keep or clear `category` depending on implementation) and resets pagination.
- Step 3: The page calls getProductFilterOptions with categorySlug set to the current `category` if present, otherwise with no categorySlug, to retrieve filter options appropriate for the search context.
- Step 4: The page calls searchProducts(query, filters derived from current URL parameters, sortBy from `sortBy`, and page/pageSize from URL or defaults).
- Step 5: Products matching the query and filters are displayed; the applied-filters bar reflects active filters.
- Step 6: User refines results using filters (e.g., sets min_price, max_price, free_shipping=true); the page updates those URL parameters, resets pagination, and re-calls searchProducts with updated filters.
- Step 7: User pages through results via pagination controls; each page click updates the `page` URL parameter and triggers a new searchProducts call.
- Step 8: User clicks any product card to navigate to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID.

Workflow 3: Find cheapest suitable product using filters and sorting
- Step 1: User navigates to products.html with a category (e.g., tubing) or a query (e.g., from global search).
- Step 2: User sets relevant filters in the filter sidebar (e.g., material, profile type, handrail step range, and free shipping when applicable). The page updates the corresponding URL parameters and refreshes results via listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.
- Step 3: User opens the sort dropdown and chooses the 'Price - Low to High' option; this sets sortBy=price_asc in the URL.
- Step 4: The page re-calls listProductsByCategory or searchProducts with sortBy=price_asc and current filters and displays products sorted from lowest to highest unit_price.
- Step 5: User reviews the first items in the listing and can click through to product.html for more details or use quick add-to-cart if available.

Workflow 4: Quick add-to-cart from listing
- Step 1: User finds a desired product in the listing grid or list view.
- Step 2: User opens the inline add-to-cart form on the product card (for non-configurable products) and enters a quantity if the UI allows changing it.
- Step 3: On clicking the 'Add to Cart' action, the product card component calls addProductToCart(productId, quantity, optional notes).
- Step 4: Upon a successful response, the component displays feedback (e.g., a confirmation message and updated cart summary) using the returned Cart and CartItem data.
- Step 5: User can then click a 'View Cart' control, which navigates to cart.html using standard navigation (no extra parameters required, as the active cart is maintained server-side).

Workflow 5: Configure a cabinet product from the listing
- Step 1: User browses the listing and identifies a cabinet product that is configurable (indicated by data from the backend, such as a flag in the product data returned by listProductsByCategory or searchProducts).
- Step 2: The product card shows a 'Configure' or 'Customize' action for such products.
- Step 3: When the user clicks this action, the page navigates to configurator.html?baseProductId=PRODUCT_ID using the product_id from the listing data.

Workflow 6: Use handrail step range filters
- Step 1: User wants a handrail kit suitable for a specific number of steps.
- Step 2: User navigates to products.html with category or query relevant to handrail kits.
- Step 3: The filter sidebar uses getProductFilterOptions to retrieve application_step_ranges and renders options or a step-range selector.
- Step 4: User chooses or inputs a step range; the page sets application_min_steps and application_max_steps in the URL parameters.
- Step 5: The page calls listProductsByCategory or searchProducts with filters including application_min_steps and application_max_steps.
- Step 6: The listing shows only handrail kit products whose supported step range overlaps with the selected range; user can then sort or further filter as needed.

Workflow 7: Persist and share filtered listing state via URL
- Step 1: User arrives at products.html and adjusts category, filters, sorting, and pagination to a desired view.
- Step 2: Each interaction updates the URL parameters (category, query, material, profile, min_price, max_price, application_min_steps, application_max_steps, free_shipping, sortBy, page, and optionally pageSize).
- Step 3: User copies the page URL or bookmarks the current view.
- Step 4: When the URL is opened again or shared, the page-init controller reads all URL parameters, reconstructs the filters and sorting state, and calls getProductFilterOptions plus listProductsByCategory or searchProducts accordingly, restoring the same listing state without using localStorage.
interactions: On page load or when the URL changes, a controller component reads all relevant URL parameters and decides whether to call listProductsByCategory or searchProducts based on the presence of `query` and `category` parameters.

Clicking on a category in the category navigation updates the `category` URL parameter, clears or adjusts filters that no longer apply if necessary, resets the `page` parameter, and triggers new calls to getProductFilterOptions and listProductsByCategory.

Submitting the search form updates the `query` URL parameter (and may preserve `category` to search within a category), resets pagination, and triggers a new call to searchProducts with current filters and sorting.

Changing a filter (material, profile, price range, step range, free shipping) immediately updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), resets the page to the first page, and triggers new calls to getProductFilterOptions (if the active category changed or is needed) and to listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.
Selecting a sort option (including 'Price - Low to High') updates the `sortBy` URL parameter and triggers a new call to listProductsByCategory or searchProducts using the updated sortBy value.

Clicking a pagination control (next/previous page or specific page number) updates the `page` URL parameter (and optionally `pageSize` if the user can change it) and triggers a new call to listProductsByCategory or searchProducts for that page.
Clicking on a product card's main area (image, name, or explicit 'View details' action) navigates to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID using the product_id from the listing data.
Clicking on a 'Configure' or 'Customize' button on a configurable cabinet product navigates to configurator.html?baseProductId=PRODUCT_ID.

Submitting a quick add-to-cart form on a listing item triggers addProductToCart(productId, quantity, notes). On success, the page shows confirmation feedback and optionally exposes a 'View Cart' action that navigates to cart.html.

Clicking on an applied filter chip in the applied-filters bar removes that filter by clearing the corresponding URL parameter and triggers a refresh of the listing via listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.

Clicking a 'Clear all filters' action clears all filter-related URL parameters (material, profile, min_price, max_price, application_min_steps, application_max_steps, free_shipping), resets page and sortBy as desired, and reloads the listing.

If no products are returned in the current result set, the product grid displays a functional message that no products match the current filters and suggests adjusting filters or query; the filters and state remain available for modification.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters are the single source of truth for the listing state. On each page load and whenever the URL changes, the page controller parses the following parameters and rebuilds the view:

- category (string): If present and non-empty, indicates the active ProductCategory slug. When `query` is not provided, the controller calls listProductsByCategory(categorySlug=category, filters, sortBy, page, pageSize). When `query` is provided, the controller calls searchProducts with a filters object that includes the category constraint.
- query (string): If present and non-empty, indicates a text search. When `query` is set, searchProducts is used as the primary data source. When `query` is absent or empty and `category` is also absent, the controller can treat this as a global browse state and use searchProducts with an empty query or a backend-supported equivalent to show all products.
- material (string): If present, this is mapped into the filters object passed to listProductsByCategory or searchProducts (e.g., filters.material = material). It also controls the material filter UI state.
- profile (string): If present, this is mapped into filters.profile_type (or an equivalent field) passed to the interfaces and bound to the profile-type filter UI.
- min_price (number) and max_price (number): If present, these are converted to numeric values and mapped into the filters object (e.g., filters.min_price and filters.max_price). The price-range control in the filter sidebar is initialized from these values.
- application_min_steps (number) and application_max_steps (number): If present, these are mapped into filters.application_min_steps and filters.application_max_steps, used primarily for handrail kit search, and used to initialize the step-range filter UI.
- free_shipping (boolean): If present and truthy, this is mapped into filters.free_shipping = true, used to filter products eligible for free shipping. The free-shipping checkbox in the UI is checked when this parameter is set.
- sortBy (string): Controls the sorting behavior. Its value is passed directly into listProductsByCategory or searchProducts. When sortBy='price_asc', the backend is expected to return results sorted by unit_price ascending. The sort control UI reads and sets this parameter.
- page (number): Used for pagination. If absent, a backend or application-level default is applied. This value is passed into the page argument in listProductsByCategory or searchProducts and controls which page of results to display. Changing page in the UI updates this parameter.
- pageSize (number, optional): If present, controls the number of products per page and is passed into the pageSize argument. If absent, a system default page size is used. A page-size selector (if implemented) updates this parameter.

On each URL change (including browser back/forward navigation), the page controller re-runs the logic: 1) Determine if search or category listing should be used based on `query` and `category`; 2) Call getMainProductCategories once to populate category navigation; 3) Call getProductFilterOptions with categorySlug set to the current category when available, or without it otherwise; 4) Call listProductsByCategory or searchProducts with a filter object derived solely from the current URL parameters and with sortBy/page/pageSize; 5) Update all child components by passing the new data and state. No localStorage or other client-side persistence mechanisms are used; the URL encodes all necessary state.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and reacts to URL changes. Determines whether to use category-based listing (listProductsByCategory) or search-based listing (searchProducts) based on URL parameters. Orchestrates calls to getMainProductCategories and getProductFilterOptions, passes resulting data and state down to child components, and handles loading/error states.

Displays top-level product categories for quick navigation and highlights the active category. Uses getMainProductCategories data and the `category` URL parameter. When a category is selected, updates the URL and triggers a refresh of filters and products via the controller.

Provides a text input for searching products. Initializes its value from the `query` URL parameter. On submit, updates the `query` parameter (and may keep the current `category` to scope the search), resets pagination, and asks the controller to call searchProducts with updated filters and sorting.

Displays filter controls for materials, profile types, price range, handrail application step range, and free shipping. Uses getProductFilterOptions(categorySlug or global) to populate available filter options and the current URL parameters to set active selections. When a filter changes, it updates the corresponding URL parameter(s), resets the page to the first page, and triggers the controller to reload products.

Shows a summary of currently active filters (e.g., material, profile, price range, step range, free_shipping). Reads from URL parameters and displays each filter as a removable chip. Offers a 'Clear all filters' action. Removing a chip or clearing all filters updates URL parameters accordingly and triggers a reload of filters and products.

Allows the user to choose the sorting order for the listing (including a 'Price - Low to High' option that sets sortBy=price_asc). Reads the active value from the `sortBy` URL parameter. When changed, updates `sortBy` in the URL and triggers the controller to re-call listProductsByCategory or searchProducts.

Renders the list of products returned by listProductsByCategory or searchProducts in a grid or list format. Accepts the products array and pagination data from the controller and instantiates a product-card component for each item. Handles empty-result states by showing a functional message and leaving filters available for adjustment.

Represents a single product in the listing. Displays key data fields from the listing interfaces (product_id, name, size_label, material, profile_type, unit_price, currency, free_shipping, stock_status, thumbnail_image_url, and optionally category_name or datasheet availability from searchProducts). Clicking the main card area navigates to product.html?id=PRODUCT_ID. If the product is marked as configurable (according to data returned from the backend), shows a 'Configure' action that navigates to configurator.html?baseProductId=PRODUCT_ID. For simple products, can contain a quick add-to-cart form that uses addProductToCart.

Embedded in or associated with each product-card for products that can be added directly from the listing. Accepts the current product_id and a quantity input. On submission, calls addProductToCart(productId, quantity, notes). On success, updates any cart summary UI and shows confirmation messaging. Exposes an optional 'View Cart' control that navigates to cart.html.

Displays pagination controls based on the pagination object returned from listProductsByCategory or searchProducts. Uses the current page/pageSize from URL parameters to highlight the active page. On page change, updates the `page` (and optionally `pageSize`) URL parameters and triggers the controller to fetch the corresponding page of products.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: Replicate the mockup’s structure: a full-width hero (handled elsewhere) at the top, followed by a strong horizontal divider. Below the divider, place all provided components inside a centered content band that is split into two columns: a narrow left column for filtering (filter-sidebar as the vertical anchor) and a wider right column for all interactive listing tools and results (category-navigation, product-search-form, applied-filters-bar, sort-control, product-grid, pagination-controls). Within the right column, stack controls in a linear top-to-bottom order: category-navigation at the top, then search, then applied-filters-bar, then sort-control, followed by the product-grid containing repeated product-card components with embedded quick-add-to-cart-form. Pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of this main column.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Replicate the mockup’s structure: a full-width hero (handled elsewhere) at the top, followed by a strong horizontal divider. Below the divider, place all provided components inside a centered content band that is split into two columns: a narrow left column for filtering (filter-sidebar as the vertical anchor) and a wider right column for all interactive listing tools and results (category-navigation, product-search-form, applied-filters-bar, sort-control, product-grid, pagination-controls). Within the right column, stack controls in a linear top-to-bottom order: category-navigation at the top, then search, then applied-filters-bar, then sort-control, followed by the product-grid containing repeated product-card components with embedded quick-add-to-cart-form. Pagination-controls sit at the very bottom of this main column.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Below the hero, the content band spans the full page width but is visually constrained to a central container (~80–90% of viewport width) to echo the subtle frame of the mockup, aligned to an implicit 12-column grid. The left filter-sidebar uses roughly 20–25% of this container width, forming a stable vertical strip. The right main content column takes the remaining 75–80%. Within the main column, the top tools (category-navigation, product-search-form, applied-filters-bar, sort-control) together occupy roughly the top 25–30% of the visible content area, with the product-grid filling most of the remaining vertical space. Product cards are arranged in a multi-column grid (3–4 columns on desktop) with consistent gutters aligned to the 8px spacing system.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain the mockup’s moderate density: controls and text are comfortably spaced but not airy. Vertical gaps between stacked components in the right column are in the 16–24px range, while major section breaks (e.g., between controls and the product-grid, and between the grid and pagination) use 32–40px spacing. Inside components like product-card and filter-sidebar, internal padding is typically 16–24px. The result feels functional and slightly compact, prioritizing quick scanning and efficiency over minimalism.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Follow the same F/Z scanning pattern: after the hero, the user’s eye hits the strong horizontal divider, then moves into the right main column, starting with the category-navigation (top), then scanning rightward towards search and sort. The gaze then drops vertically through applied-filters-bar and into the product-grid, where individual product-cards become the primary focal elements. The left filter-sidebar acts as a persistent vertical anchor that users notice when scanning back up or wanting to adjust results. Pagination-controls provide a clear visual stop at the bottom, signaling the end of the current result set.

layout.overall_description: Below a full-width industrial hero and a bold horizontal separator, the page resolves into a two-column band sitting inside a centered content container. On the left, a tall, narrow vertical filter-sidebar runs from just under the divider down toward the fold, echoing the mockup’s left navigation column and giving the page a solid, utilitarian spine. On the right, a wider main content column stacks listing controls in a clear hierarchy: category tabs at the top, then a search bar, a compact strip summarizing active filters, and a right-aligned sort dropdown. Beneath this control area, the layout opens into a dense but orderly product grid, with consistent card sizes and industrial imagery forming a repeating rhythm across the width. Each product-card mirrors the no-nonsense aesthetic: image on top, name and specs in the middle, and a quick add-to-cart strip anchored at the bottom. At the very bottom of the main column, pagination provides a discrete but clear endpoint. Overall, the layout feels like a structured, slightly old-school corporate catalog: straightforward, image-led, and optimized for scanning and adjusting filters rather than storytelling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and does not occupy explicit screen space, but conceptually it sits at the top of the page’s document flow, just beneath the hero section in the DOM. It initializes URL parameter state and ensures that when the two-column band is rendered, the filter-sidebar, applied-filters-bar, sort-control, and product-grid all appear in a consistent, synchronized state. Visually, it has no box, no padding, and no impact on spatial layout, but all visible components below depend on it being processed first.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; exists purely as a logical controller in the normal document flow with no rendered footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the right main content column, the category-navigation appears at the very top, directly below the horizontal divider that separates the hero from the body content and horizontally aligned with the top of the filter-sidebar on the left. It spans most of the width of the right column (approximately 90–100% of that column, with 16–24px side padding inside the content container) and is positioned about 24–32px below the divider. The tabs are arranged horizontally, left-aligned, forming a single line or two lines of categories, with 16px–24px horizontal spacing between each tab, contributing to a strong primary focal row. Vertically, there is 16–24px of space below the category-navigation before the product-search-form, keeping the density functional but not cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content column; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-search-form sits directly beneath the category-navigation in the right main column, aligned to the same left edge. It occupies roughly 70–80% of the right column’s width, creating a prominent, wide search input that echoes the utilitarian style. The search field itself is a single horizontal bar centered vertically within its container row, with 16px vertical padding above and below, and 16–24px internal padding. To maintain the compact density, the vertical gap between the bottom of the search form and the top of the applied-filters-bar is about 16–20px. The left filter-sidebar continues down alongside this search row, so the search form’s height visually aligns roughly with the top portion of the filters, reinforcing the two-column relationship.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains in the normal scroll flow just below category-navigation.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-sidebar forms the left column of the two-column band, beginning directly under the horizontal divider that follows the hero and extending downward alongside the main content. It occupies approximately 20–25% of the total content container width, starting flush with the container’s left margin and maintaining 16–24px internal padding from its edges to its filter controls. The sidebar has a columnar structure: a simple heading or label at the top, followed by vertically stacked filter groups with minimal vertical spacing (12–16px) between individual items to preserve the moderate density. In the viewport, the top of the sidebar aligns vertically with the category-navigation in the right column, and its bottom typically extends at least to the middle of the product-grid area, forming a tall, steady visual anchor. There is a 24–32px horizontal gutter between the right edge of the filter-sidebar and the left edge of the main content column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static by default but can optionally use a sticky behavior on larger screens—sticking to the top of the viewport once scrolled—while respecting a top offset that keeps it below any fixed global header; it otherwise scrolls within the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-bar is positioned in the right main column directly beneath the product-search-form, sharing the same left alignment. It stretches nearly the full width of the right column (about 90–100%), creating a horizontal strip of compact filter chips. Vertically, there is a modest 16–20px gap above it and below it, maintaining the moderate density. Each active filter appears as a small pill-shaped chip with 8–12px horizontal spacing between chips and 8px vertical spacing when they wrap to a second line, echoing the slightly compact Google Sites-like aesthetic. When multiple filters are active, the bar may grow to two lines but remains visually contained; the product-grid below is separated from this bar by approximately 24–32px to clearly distinguish control summary from results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; scrolls away with the main content, remaining part of the control stack at the top of the results area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control sits in the same horizontal band as the lower edge of the applied-filters-bar or just beneath it, aligned to the right edge of the right main column. Practically, it appears as a compact dropdown on the top-right above the product-grid. Horizontally, it occupies only about 20–25% of the right column width, with the remaining left side of the row either empty or optionally showing a small result count, keeping the layout visually light but organized. There is approximately 8–12px vertical space between the bottom of the applied-filters-bar and the top of the sort-control row (if separated), and 24–32px between the sort-control and the top of the product-grid, reinforcing that sorting is the final control before the results. This placement supports the F-pattern scan from left filters to right sort, then down into the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; remains in the normal flow above the product-grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-grid begins in the right main column immediately beneath the sort-control, separated by a clear 24–32px vertical gap to mark the transition from controls to content. It spans the full width of the right column (minus standard side padding), forming a multi-column grid of product-card components arranged in 3–4 columns on desktop (each card occupying roughly 25–30% of the right column width), with 16–24px horizontal and vertical gutters between cards, aligned to the 8px spacing scale. The grid’s top row aligns visually with the mid-section of the filter-sidebar, making the left filter column and right product grid feel visually paired. As the user scrolls, the grid extends downward, occupying the bulk of the page’s vertical space and serving as the main content area. The bottom of the grid is separated from pagination-controls by a 32–40px gap, signaling the end of available results on the current page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; fills the available vertical space and scrolls, with responsive behavior reducing to fewer columns on narrower viewports while maintaining consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each product-card is a rectangular tile within the product-grid, following a simple vertical stack: image at the top, text details in the middle, and the quick-add-to-cart-form anchored at the bottom. Cards are uniform in width within each row and have a subtle, utilitarian appearance—light border or faint shadow—with 16–24px internal padding on all sides. The product image occupies roughly the top 40–50% of the card height, spanning the full card width with a fixed aspect ratio, reinforcing the image-driven feel of the overall design. Beneath the image, there is 12–16px of vertical spacing before the product name and key details (like material, size) arranged in 2–4 lines of text, using tight line spacing consistent with the moderate density. At the bottom, separated by another 12–16px gap, the quick-add-to-cart-form occupies the full card width as a horizontal strip, ensuring all cards visually align along the bottom row of controls. The card’s outer margins are defined by the product-grid’s gutters, making them feel like tightly arranged, industrial catalog tiles rather than airy showcases.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid; cards resize responsively within their grid columns but maintain the top-image / middle-details / bottom-add-to-cart stacking order.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quick-add-to-cart-form lives inside each product-card, anchored along the card’s bottom edge to create a consistent baseline across all cards in a row. It spans nearly the full width of the card’s inner content area (with 12–16px side padding), and its height is compact, reflecting the efficient density of the overall design. The form layout is horizontal: a small quantity input field on the left and an add-to-cart button on the right, or a simple add button with optional quantity selector inline, all aligned vertically around a single baseline. There is minimal vertical padding (8–12px) within this strip to keep it visually tight and functional. The form is clearly separated from the product details above by about 12–16px, but sits only 8–12px above the card’s bottom padding, reinforcing its role as the final actionable element for each product.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each product-card; anchored to the bottom so that even when text content varies, the add-to-cart area remains consistently aligned across cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-controls are placed at the very bottom of the right main content column, directly beneath the product-grid, with a generous 32–40px vertical gap separating them from the last row of product-cards. They are horizontally centered within the right column to create a clear visual terminus for the page’s content. The controls themselves are arranged in a single horizontal row (previous/next arrows and page numbers), with 8–12px spacing between buttons, matching the moderate density and the Google Sites-like pattern. The bottom of the pagination row has 32–48px of padding or margin before the end of the page content container, giving a subtle but clear breathing space that communicates “end of section.” From the user’s perspective, reaching pagination feels like reaching the bottom of the catalog page iteration.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static; appears only once at the bottom of the main column and scrolls into view as the user reaches the end of the product-grid.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a single product (structural sections, consumables, or kits), and allows users to add it to the cart or save it to a wishlist/project list.

core_features: Load and display full product details based on the URL parameter 'id' by calling getProductDetails.
Show product specifications including dimensions, material, profile type, application notes, and shipping-related information using data from getProductDetails.
Display pricing per unit and stock-related constraints (minimum/maximum order quantities) using Product fields from getProductDetails.
Allow users to set a quantity, optionally add internal notes, and add the product to the cart via addProductToCart.
Surface technical resources by conditionally displaying a datasheet link when getProductDetails indicates datasheet availability.
Enable saving the product into an existing wishlist or a newly created named project list using getWishlists and addProductToWishlist.
Provide optional entry into the configurator flow for configurable products by exposing navigation to configurator.html when getProductDetails.can_configure is true.
Show confirmation and next-step navigation (to cart.html or wishlist.html) after successful add-to-cart or add-to-wishlist actions.
user_workflows:
View product details:
1) User navigates to product.html?id={productId} from listings, wishlist, cart, or an external deep link.
2) The page extracts the 'id' URL parameter and invokes getProductDetails with productId = id.
3) While data is loading, the page shows a loading state in place of product content.
4) On success, the page renders product name, category, images, specifications, pricing, stock information, application description, and datasheet availability.
5) If the product supports configuration (can_configure true), a control to navigate to configurator.html is displayed.

Add product to cart:
1) User reviews product details and enters a quantity in the add-to-cart form.
2) The form validates quantity against product constraints (such as stock_status and any min_order_quantity / max_order_quantity from the Product data) without hardcoding numeric limits.
3) User optionally enters notes (e.g., for internal reference) into a notes field.
4) User submits the form; the page calls addProductToCart with productId = id, quantity = user-entered quantity, and notes = user-entered notes.
5) On success, the page updates a cart notification banner using the returned Cart and CartItem data and displays any message returned by addProductToCart.
6) User may click 'View Cart' in the notification to navigate to cart.html or dismiss the notification to continue browsing on the same product page.

Save product to wishlist or project list:
1) From the product detail page, the user clicks a 'Save to wishlist/project' control.
2) If wishlists have not yet been loaded in this session, the page calls getWishlists to retrieve all available wishlists for the user.
3) A modal or panel displays the list of existing wishlists and an option to create a new list by entering a new list name.
4) User selects an existing wishlist or enters a new list name, and optionally adds notes for this saved item.
5) User submits; the page calls addProductToWishlist with productId = id, wishlistId set when an existing list is chosen (or left unset when creating a new list), newListName populated when creating a new list (or left unset when using an existing list), and notes from the form.
6) On success, the page shows a wishlist confirmation notification using the returned Wishlist and WishlistItem data, along with the message from addProductToWishlist.
7) User may click 'View Wishlist' in the notification to navigate to wishlist.html or dismiss the notification.

Access technical datasheet:
1) After product details load, the page checks datasheet_available from getProductDetails.
2) If true, a 'Technical Datasheet' link or button appears.
3) User clicks the control; the browser opens the datasheet_url from getProductDetails in a new tab or window.
4) If datasheet_available is false, no datasheet control is displayed and no additional call is made.

Start configuration for configurable product:
1) After loading product details, the page reads can_configure from getProductDetails.
2) If can_configure is true, the page shows a 'Configure this product' control.
3) User clicks the control; the page navigates to configurator.html?baseProductId={id}, using the same 'id' URL parameter from product.html.
4) The configurator page then handles configuration based on the passed baseProductId.
interactions: On initial load or when the 'id' URL parameter changes, the product detail loader component calls getProductDetails with the new productId.
If getProductDetails fails (e.g., invalid id), the page displays an error state and disables add-to-cart and wishlist actions for that session until a valid product is loaded.
Clicking quantity controls (such as increment/decrement or direct text input) updates an internal quantity state used when calling addProductToCart.
Submitting the add-to-cart form triggers validation and then calls addProductToCart; on success, a notification banner appears and remains visible until the user dismisses it or navigates away.
Clicking 'View Cart' in the cart notification navigates to cart.html without modifying the cart further.
Clicking 'Save to wishlist/project' opens the wishlist modal; if wishlists are not yet loaded, it triggers getWishlists before showing selectable options.
Selecting an existing wishlist in the modal sets an internal wishlistId state; toggling to 'create new list' clears wishlistId and enables entry of newListName.
Submitting the wishlist modal calls addProductToWishlist with parameters derived from the user’s selection and input; on success, the modal closes and a wishlist notification banner is shown.
Clicking 'View Wishlist' in the wishlist notification navigates to wishlist.html.
Clicking the datasheet control (when shown) opens the datasheet_url in a separate browser context without changing the current page state.
Clicking the 'Configure this product' control (when can_configure is true) navigates to configurator.html with baseProductId set to the current product id.

Changing the browser URL to a different id (e.g., via in-app navigation) triggers a re-load of product details via getProductDetails without requiring a full page reload if the application supports client-side routing.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state driver for this page is the URL parameter 'id'. On every page load or when the URL changes, the page extracts the 'id' parameter and uses it as productId for getProductDetails. If 'id' is missing or empty, the page enters a 'no product selected' error state and does not attempt to call getProductDetails.

The getProductDetails response becomes the canonical source of truth for the Product being displayed and for related derived information such as category_name, formatted_dimensions, application_description, datasheet_available, datasheet_url, and can_configure. All display components (header, images, specifications, pricing, configuration entry, and datasheet controls) bind to these fields and update whenever a new getProductDetails result is received.

The page maintains internal, in-memory UI state only (no localStorage or similar client persistence). Examples of internal state include: loading/error status for getProductDetails; the currently entered quantity for add-to-cart; optional notes for cart or wishlist entries; visibility of the wishlist modal; selected wishlistId or newListName; and flags indicating whether notifications are currently visible. None of these states are persisted beyond the current page context.

Whenever the user submits the add-to-cart form, the page calls addProductToCart with productId based strictly on the current 'id' URL parameter and quantity/notes from current UI state. The returned Cart and CartItem objects are not stored as long-term state on this page; they are used to populate the success notification and any immediate contextual information (such as displaying updated item count), with the authoritative cart view handled on cart.html.

When the user opens the wishlist modal for the first time in a session, the page calls getWishlists and stores the resulting array in memory only for as long as the page is alive. Subsequent openings reuse this in-memory list unless the page chooses to actively refresh it. addProductToWishlist is always called with productId derived from the current 'id' URL parameter and wishlistId/newListName/notes derived from transient UI state in the modal.

Navigation to cart.html, wishlist.html, or configurator.html is purely via URL changes that may include parameters (for example, baseProductId for configurator.html). No additional state is stored locally; downstream pages must re-fetch the necessary data using their own interfaces and URL parameters.

components.functionality: Reads the 'id' URL parameter, calls getProductDetails with this productId, and manages loading and error states for the entire product detail page.
Displays high-level product information such as name, category label, SKU, material, profile type, and a concise size label, using the Product returned by getProductDetails as a read-only source.
Shows the primary product image and any additional images returned in Product.image_urls, allowing the user to change the active image.
Displays key specifications derived from the Product and getProductDetails output, including dimensions, units, and any application-specific information.
Conditionally shows a technical datasheet access control when getProductDetails indicates availability, and opens the datasheet_url in a new browser context on click.
Displays pricing per unit and stock-related information for the product, drawing exclusively from Product fields obtained via getProductDetails and without hardcoded numeric values.
Allows the user to specify quantity and notes for this product, validates this input against Product constraints, and calls addProductToCart to add the item to the user's cart.
Shows feedback after addProductToCart completes, using the returned Cart, CartItem, and message to inform the user and offer navigation to cart.html.
Provides a single entry point for saving the current product to a wishlist or project list by opening the wishlist modal and, if required, triggering retrieval of available wishlists.
Displays available wishlists from getWishlists, allows selection of an existing list or creation of a new named list, captures optional notes, and on submission calls addProductToWishlist.
Shows confirmation after a successful addProductToWishlist call, summarizing which wishlist received the product and offering navigation to wishlist.html.
If getProductDetails.can_configure is true, enables the user to start a configuration flow for the current product by navigating to configurator.html with baseProductId set to the current product id.

layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area is relatively open with a single large image and centered title. Below the divider, the product-detail area is more compact and functional: headings are clear but not overly spaced, input fields and buttons are grouped with 8–16px gaps, and panels have 16–24px internal padding. The image gallery has slightly more breathing room to showcase the product, while the right-side purchase column is denser, stacking pricing, availability, and forms in a tight, utilitarian layout. Specs and datasheet links are arranged in a list-like, information-dense manner consistent with engineering documentation, but still separated by 24–32px section breaks for legibility.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention follows an F/Z pattern inspired directly by the mockup. First, the eye lands on the hero’s centered site or category title. It then moves down to the horizontal divider and shifts into the main content column. Within the right column, the large product title in the product header is the first focal point. From there, attention moves horizontally to the right-hand purchase area (price and add-to-cart form) within the upper interaction band, then back left into the product image gallery. After this, the gaze moves downward into the specs panel and datasheet link for detailed validation. The left sidebar nav provides a stable vertical anchor when scanning the page or considering switching sections, while transient banners (cart/wishlist notifications) momentarily intercept the flow near the top of the right column without hiding the main structure.

layout.overall_description: The page sits in a centered content frame beneath a wide industrial hero image, separated by a solid gray horizontal divider that spans the full width of the screen. Below this bar, the layout snaps into a two-column structure: a narrow left sidebar with vertically stacked navigation links and, to its right, a wider product-detail column that drives the page’s purpose. At the very top of this right column, any notification banners appear as full-width strips, followed by a bold product header that prominently displays the product name and key identifiers. Directly beneath the header, the column opens into a broad interaction band that itself is divided: the left side shows a large product image gallery, while the right side stacks pricing, stock status, add-to-cart controls, wishlist entry, and a configurator entry link in a compact, card-like block. Below this band, the layout returns to a single-column flow, presenting specifications in a dense but organized panel and a clearly separated datasheet link block. The overall effect is utilitarian and industrial: the grid is clear, spacing is regular and 8px-based, and the page encourages a top-down reading path that moves smoothly from understanding what the product is, to buying/configuring it, and then to validating details via technical specs and documents. Overlays like the loader and wishlist modal temporarily float above this structure without altering its inherently linear, no-nonsense layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-detail loader appears as a semi-opaque overlay that covers the main body area below the hero, including both the left sidebar and the right product-detail column. It starts just beneath the horizontal divider bar and extends to the bottom of the viewport, aligning with the central content container’s lateral bounds or slightly beyond to feel like it blankets the working area. At the center of the viewport, a simple spinner or loading indicator sits within a small, lightly framed box with 24px padding, clearly visible against the dimmed background. Underneath, all underlying components maintain their positions but are visually subdued, reinforcing that the loader is temporally on top without changing the underlying layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay within the viewport, positioned relative to the top of the content area beneath the hero; covers interactions and scrolls with the viewport if content is taller than the screen.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-header section sits at the top of the right product-detail column, directly below any notification banners and roughly aligned with the first line of items in the left sidebar. It spans the full width of the right column (approximately 75–80% of the total content width) and is separated from the horizontal divider by about 24–32px of vertical whitespace. Inside, the product name appears as a large, bold heading aligned to the left, with supporting identifiers (e.g., SKU, category, brief descriptor) either on the next line or in a subdued row beneath, separated by 8–12px. The header has a compact vertical footprint: roughly equivalent to 2–3 lines of text plus 16–24px of top and bottom padding, giving it clear prominence without making it overly tall.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element within the right column, anchoring the top of the product-detail content; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-image gallery is positioned immediately below the product header, forming the left portion of the key interaction band in the right column. It occupies about 60% of the width of this band, aligned to the left edge of the right column, with a 24px horizontal gap separating it from the right-hand purchase area. Vertically, its top edge is aligned with the top of the price-and-stock block on the right, creating a clear horizontal row. The gallery’s main image area is roughly square or slightly landscape-oriented and takes up the majority of its column, with smaller thumbnail images (if present) arranged in a row beneath or as a narrow strip along the left edge, separated by 8px gaps. The overall block has 16–24px internal padding, and there is about 32px of vertical space between the bottom of the gallery and the next full-width section below (typically the specs panel), giving the imagery room to breathe while respecting the page’s moderately compact density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component constrained to the left portion of the right column’s interaction band; resizes proportionally with the column width but maintains its relative 60% share.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product-specs panel is placed directly below the entire interaction band (image gallery plus right-hand purchase column) within the right product-detail column. It starts approximately 32–40px beneath the lowest element of that band and stretches the full width of the right column, maintaining a consistent left alignment with the product header and image gallery. The panel resembles a structured, table-like or sectioned block with a clear title (e.g., 'Specifications') at the top, followed by rows or grouped fields of technical data. Internal padding is 16–24px on all sides, and rows of data are separated by 8–12px, creating an information-dense but legible block. The overall height can grow as needed with content, but it is visually framed as a single section between the interaction band above and the datasheet link block below, separated from each by about 32px of vertical space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width panel within the right column; extends vertically as needed while preserving the column’s width and alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The datasheet-link block appears just below the product-specs panel in the right column, separated by roughly 24–32px of vertical space to clearly distinguish it as a resources section. It spans the full width of the right column but is relatively shallow in height, functioning like a single-row card or simple text block. The main link or button to download/view the datasheet is aligned to the left, potentially accompanied by a small document icon, with 16–24px of padding around the content. If additional resource links are present, they are stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing. The block sits slightly closer to the specs panel above than to the bottom page margin, preserving the sense that it is closely related to the technical information while still standing on its own.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block element tied to the bottom of the main informational stack in the right column; scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The price-and-stock block is positioned in the upper-right portion of the right product-detail column, forming the right side of the interaction band opposite the product-image gallery. Its top aligns horizontally with the top of the gallery, and it occupies about 40% of the band’s width, with a 24px gap between it and the gallery. The block is card-like, with 16–24px internal padding and a subtle border or background tint that sets it off from the page background. Within the block, price information is at the top in larger, bold text; immediately beneath, stock status and lead time details are presented with shorter line lengths and 8–12px vertical spacing. This block does not span the full height of the gallery; instead, it stacks compactly above the add-to-cart form and related controls such that the entire right-hand column appears as a cohesive vertical stack of purchase-related elements. There is approximately 16px vertical spacing between this block and the add-to-cart form directly below it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned column component within the interaction band; maintains its approximate 40% width as the layout scales.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart form sits directly below the price-and-stock block within the same right-hand column of the interaction band. It shares the same width as the price-and-stock block (about 40% of the right column) and aligns flush with its left and right edges, creating a clean vertical column of commerce actions. The form includes quantity inputs, possibly variant selectors, and a prominent 'Add to Cart' button, arranged in a vertical stack with 8–12px gaps. The entire form has 16–24px internal padding and likely shares a similar card treatment to the price-and-stock block so they visually read as a unified section. The 'Add to Cart' button sits near the bottom of this column but remains above the wishlist button and configurator entry link, which appear as secondary actions. There is about 16–24px of vertical spacing between the bottom of the add-to-cart form and the wishlist entry button immediately beneath it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, stacked directly under the price-and-stock block in the right-side interaction column; scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-notification banner appears as a full-width strip at the very top of the right product-detail column, directly beneath the horizontal divider bar and above the product-header section. When visible, it spans the entire width of the right column (aligning visually with the product header and main content), with 12–16px of internal vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. Its height is kept compact, typically one to two lines of text plus padding, and it pushes the product header and all subsequent content downward rather than overlapping them. The left edge of the banner is aligned with the top of the body content’s left sidebar, preserving a clear horizontal rule across the layout even though the banner is only in the right column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline banner that appears and disappears within the normal document flow; it does not float but reflows content when shown or hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-entry button is placed in the same right-hand purchase column as the price-and-stock block and add-to-cart form. It appears directly below the add-to-cart form, aligned to the left edge of that column, with about 16–24px of vertical space separating it from the bottom of the form. The button is smaller and less visually dominant than the primary 'Add to Cart' button—often styled as a secondary button or outlined control—but still clearly clickable. If there are two secondary actions (wishlist and configurator entry control), they may sit as a small vertical stack, with 8–12px between them.
