This positioning keeps wishlist actions contextually tied to purchase decisions while respecting the visual hierarchy: header → price → add-to-cart → wishlist.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control in the right-hand interaction column; it moves with that column as the page scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist modal appears as a centered overlay on top of the entire page, dimming the background including the hero, sidebar, and product-detail column. The modal panel itself is a medium-sized box occupying roughly 40–50% of the viewport width and up to 60% of the viewport height, with a minimum 48px margin from any viewport edge to prevent it from feeling cramped. Internally, it has 24–32px padding, with a title at the top, a scrollable list or selector area in the middle, and action buttons at the bottom aligned to the right. The modal is vertically centered on most screens, but if content exceeds available height, it remains centered while its internal content scrolls independently, ensuring the close controls remain visible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, independent of page scroll; background content is non-interactive while the modal is open.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-notification banner occupies the same structural position as the cart-notification banner: a full-width strip at the top of the right product-detail column beneath the horizontal divider and above the product header. When both banner types could appear, they either share this slot (one at a time) or stack with 8–12px between them, maintaining a compact top area. The banner’s height is similar to the cart banner—one or two lines of text plus 12–16px vertical padding—and it aligns left and right with the product header’s text block. It pushes the product header and all content below it down when visible, preserving the uninterrupted layout lines between the right column and the left sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline banner that appears or disappears within the normal document flow at the top of the right column; does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configurator-entry control is placed in close proximity to the wishlist-entry button within the right-hand purchase column, typically directly beneath the wishlist button or alongside it depending on width constraints. In the default stacked layout, it appears as a text link or subtle button aligned to the left, with about 8–12px of vertical spacing from the wishlist button above. This positions it as an advanced or secondary action after standard purchase and wishlist flows, but still part of the same vertical stack of commerce-related controls. The control does not span the full width of the right column; instead, it uses a compact width tied to its label length, maintaining the utilitarian, no-frills aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static navigation link within the right-hand interaction column; scrolls normally with the page and maintains its position relative to the wishlist and add-to-cart controls.

name: Shopping Cart
title: Shopping Cart
description: Page for reviewing and managing items in the cart, updating quantities, and proceeding to checkout.

core_features: Load and display the current cart with all items, quantities, unit prices, line subtotals, and cart totals using getCartDetails.
Support both standard products and configured products by showing configuration summaries when configured_product_id is present from getCartDetails.
Allow users to change the quantity of any cart item and refresh cart totals using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow users to remove individual items from the cart using removeCartItem.
Allow users to clear all items from the cart with a single action using clearCart.
Show order summary including subtotal, shipping_total, tax_total, and total from the Cart object returned by getCartDetails and subsequent update interfaces.
Provide a clear action to start or resume checkout using startCheckout, then navigate to checkout.html?step=shipping.
Provide navigation back to product browsing and product details (products.html and product.html?id={product_id}) from cart items.
user_workflows: View current cart contents: 1) User navigates to cart.html via header/cart icon or direct URL. 2) On page load, the cart-init-controller component calls getCartDetails with no parameters. 3) The returned cart and items data populate the cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel components. 4) The user sees all cart items, including configuration_summary for configured products, along with quantities and prices.

Update the quantity of a cart item: 1) User adjusts the quantity field for a specific line in the cart-item-row (e.g., via input or stepper). 2) User confirms the change (e.g., blur, enter, or update button), triggering the onQuantityChange handler. 3) onQuantityChange calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId set to the line’s cart_item_id and quantity set to the new value. 4) On successful response, the page-level cart state is replaced with the returned cart and items. 5) The cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel re-render with updated line_subtotal, subtotal, and totals.

Remove a single cart item: 1) User clicks the remove action on a cart-item-row. 2) The onRemoveItem handler calls removeCartItem with cartItemId set to the line’s cart_item_id. 3) On success, cart-init-controller updates the shared cart state from the returned cart and items. 4) The removed item disappears from cart-item-list and totals in cart-summary-panel are updated. 5) If no items remain, the empty-cart-state component is shown instead of the list.

Clear the entire cart: 1) User triggers the clear-cart-control (e.g., a clear cart button). 2) The onClearCart handler calls clearCart with no parameters. 3) On success, the cart state is updated with the returned empty items array and updated Cart totals. 4) cart-item-list hides and empty-cart-state becomes visible, while cart-summary-panel reflects an empty cart.

Proceed to checkout: 1) User reviews items and clicks the proceed-to-checkout-button in the cart-summary-panel. 2) The onProceedToCheckout handler calls startCheckout with no parameters. 3) On successful response, the page may optionally validate that checkout_session.status is compatible with continuing (e.g., in_progress or shipping_selected). 4) The browser is redirected to checkout.html with URL parameter step=shipping.

Continue shopping: 1) From the cart page, user clicks the continue-shopping-link. 2) The browser navigates to products.html with no additional parameters (or with whatever query parameters are already present in the current URL if the implementation chooses to preserve them). 3) User can add more items and later return to cart.html.

View product details from cart: 1) User clicks the product name or thumbnail in a cart-item-row. 2) The onOpenProductDetail handler reads the product_id from the row’s data. 3) Browser navigates to product.html with URL parameter id set to that product_id (product.html?id={product_id}). 4) When user returns to cart.html, the cart is reloaded via getCartDetails.
interactions: On page load of cart.html, the cart-init-controller automatically calls getCartDetails and binds the returned data to shared cart state used by all cart-related components.
Users can click on product names or thumbnails in cart-item-row elements, triggering navigation to product.html?id={product_id} via the onOpenProductDetail event.
Users can change quantities in the quantity-editor-form inside each cart-item-row, which triggers updateCartItemQuantity via the onQuantityChange handler when the change is confirmed.
Users can click a remove control inside cart-item-row to trigger removeCartItem via the onRemoveItem handler, removing the line and refreshing totals.
Users can activate the clear-cart-control to call clearCart via the onClearCart handler, removing all CartItem records associated with the current Cart and updating the UI to an empty state.
Users can click the proceed-to-checkout-button, which calls startCheckout via onProceedToCheckout and then navigates to checkout.html?step=shipping.
Users can click the continue-shopping-link to navigate back to products.html via onContinueShopping, without changing the cart state.

The UI can conditionally display stock_status information from getCartDetails for each item (e.g., in_stock, backorder), and may disable or warn on checkout for items that are out_of_stock or discontinued, all without introducing static stock rules.
page_functionality.state_logic: The cart page itself does not require any URL parameters to function; it always represents the current active Cart associated with the user session and uses getCartDetails to load that state. State transitions rely on server-side updates via the assigned interfaces and on navigation between pages using URL parameters where specified. When the user navigates from cart.html to product.html via onOpenProductDetail, the destination URL sets id={product_id} (e.g., product.html?id={product_id}) so that the product detail page can load the correct Product. When the user proceeds to checkout via onProceedToCheckout, after calling startCheckout the browser navigates to checkout.html with the URL parameter step=shipping (checkout.html?step=shipping) to indicate the current checkout step. Returning to cart.html (via navigation or browser back) always triggers a fresh getCartDetails call; no cart-related state is persisted in URL parameters or local storage. The products.html page is accessed without mandatory query parameters from the cart, and any additional query string behavior on that page is independent of cart.html and not defined here.

components.functionality: Central controller for the shopping cart page that, on load, retrieves the current Cart and related items using getCartDetails. It maintains an in-memory cart state (Cart object and list of items as returned by getCartDetails) and exposes it to child components such as cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel. It is also responsible for updating this shared state after updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, clearCart, and startCheckout are invoked by other components.

Displays all cart items returned by getCartDetails. For each item, it renders a cart-item-row showing product_name, product_sku, thumbnail_image_url, configuration_summary (for configured products), quantity, unit_price, line_subtotal, stock_status, and any shipping indicators based on applied_free_shipping. It listens to shared cart state from cart-init-controller and re-renders whenever that state changes. When the list of items is empty, it hides itself and allows empty-cart-state to render.

Represents a single CartItem with associated Product and optional ConfiguredProduct context as summarized by getCartDetails. Displays the product_name and thumbnail_image_url as clickable elements to open product.html?id={product_id}. Shows configuration_summary when configured_product_id is present so users can see cabinet or other configuration details. Exposes controls for updating quantity via quantity-editor-form and for removing the item via remove-item-button. It also displays stock_status for that line so users can see if an item is in stock, on backorder, or discontinued.

Embedded in each cart-item-row, this form allows the user to adjust the CartItem quantity. The form displays the current quantity from the item data returned by getCartDetails. When the user changes the value and confirms (e.g., by leaving the field or activating an update control), the onQuantityChange handler validates the input (e.g., ensuring it is a positive integer) and then calls updateCartItemQuantity with cartItemId set to the associated cart_item_id and quantity set to the new value. On success, the updated Cart and items list returned by updateCartItemQuantity are passed back to cart-init-controller via onCartStateUpdate, causing cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel to re-render.

Control within each cart-item-row that removes the corresponding CartItem from the cart. When activated, the onRemoveItem handler calls removeCartItem with cartItemId equal to the item’s cart_item_id. The response returns an updated Cart and items array. cart-init-controller’s onCartStateUpdate is then invoked to replace the shared cart state, which causes cart-item-list and cart-summary-panel to refresh, and may trigger empty-cart-state if the last item was removed.

Provides an option to remove all items from the current Cart. When the user activates this control, onClearCart may optionally display a confirmation step in the UI, then calls clearCart with no parameters. The returned Cart and items array (which should represent an empty cart) are applied to shared state through cart-init-controller. This hides cart-item-list, shows empty-cart-state, and updates cart-summary-panel to reflect the empty Cart.

Displays an appropriate empty-state message and a navigation option to continue shopping when the Cart contains no items. It is controlled by the shared cart state from cart-init-controller (e.g., when the items array returned by getCartDetails, removeCartItem, or clearCart is empty). It offers a link or button that navigates to products.html via the onContinueShopping handler.

Displays order-level totals using the Cart object returned by getCartDetails and subsequent updates. It shows subtotal, shipping_total, tax_total, total, and optionally indicates if free shipping has been applied at the order level (inferred from Cart.shipping_total or from applied_free_shipping flags on items). It also contains the proceed-to-checkout-button and optionally the clear-cart-control. When cart state changes (e.g., after updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, or clearCart), it re-binds to the latest Cart object.

Primary call-to-action for moving from the cart to checkout. When activated, the onProceedToCheckout handler calls startCheckout with no parameters. On success, the handler can use the returned checkout_session to confirm that checkout can continue (for example, by checking status in the response). After validation, the handler navigates the browser to checkout.html?step=shipping as defined in the outgoing navigation rules.

Provides a way for the user to leave cart.html and resume browsing products. When activated, onContinueShopping triggers navigation to products.html. It does not modify the Cart and does not call any cart-related interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same top-to-bottom sequence as the mockup: a full-width industrial hero at the very top (shared across the site), then a strong horizontal divider bar. Below the divider, the shopping cart content lives inside a centered content container split into two main columns: a narrow left sidebar for site-wide navigation and a wider right column for the cart. Within the right column, a large 'Shopping Cart' page title appears at the top, followed by a short explanatory paragraph. Beneath this header block, the content is divided horizontally: the cart item list occupies the left two-thirds of the right column, while a compact cart summary panel with checkout actions occupies the right third. When the cart is empty, the entire right column instead shows a centered empty-state panel, replacing both the list and summary.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same top-to-bottom sequence as the mockup: a full-width industrial hero at the very top (shared across the site), then a strong horizontal divider bar. Below the divider, the shopping cart content lives inside a centered content container split into two main columns: a narrow left sidebar for site-wide navigation and a wider right column for the cart. Within the right column, a large 'Shopping Cart' page title appears at the top, followed by a short explanatory paragraph. Beneath this header block, the content is divided horizontally: the cart item list occupies the left two-thirds of the right column, while a compact cart summary panel with checkout actions occupies the right third. When the cart is empty, the entire right column instead shows a centered empty-state panel, replacing both the list and summary.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport height at full width. Below that, the content container spans about 80–90% of the page width, centered, with subtle left and right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar uses approximately 20–25% of the width, and the right main content uses about 75–80%. Within the right main content, the cart header (title + description) takes the full width of the right column across the top. Below it, the cart-item-list uses roughly 65–70% of the right column’s width on the left, and the cart-summary-panel uses 30–35% on the right, aligned to form a simple internal grid. Vertical spacing between major blocks (hero to divider, divider to cart header, header to list/summary, list to footer actions) follows the 32–48px rhythm, with individual components using 16–24px internal padding aligned to the 8px grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and functional. The hero remains visually open with a large background image and a bold title. In the body area, elements are more compact: cart rows are table-like with 16–20px padding, relatively tight vertical spacing (12–16px) between rows, and concise typography. The side nav links are stacked with modest gaps, and the summary panel is tightly structured to show totals and actions without excessive whitespace. Overall spacing is enough to keep items clearly separated, but the page leans toward a workmanlike, information-dense feel consistent with a corporate, utilitarian cart for an industrial supplier.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the hero title and top navigation, drops down to the horizontal divider, then moves into the right-hand 'Shopping Cart' heading, reinforcing the primary focus on the cart. From there, the user scans down through the introductory text into the left-side cart item list, reading rows from left (product info) to right (quantity and removal controls). The eye then shifts to the right-side cart summary panel to confirm totals and locate the bold 'Proceed to Checkout' button, which acts as the primary call to action. The left sidebar nav remains a vertical anchor along the left edge, available when scanning back up or choosing other sections. Secondary actions like 'Clear Cart' and 'Continue Shopping' sit below or around the primary content, easily discoverable but visually subordinate to checkout.

layout.overall_description: Below the shared full-width industrial hero and a solid horizontal divider, the shopping cart page is framed within a centered content band that feels like a simple Google Sites–style template. On the left, a narrow vertical sidebar lists site sections for quick navigation. On the right, the main cart content begins with a bold 'Shopping Cart' heading and a brief line of explanatory text spanning the full width of this column. Directly under this header, the area splits: the larger left portion shows a stacked list of cart items in a table-like layout, each row with product details, quantity controls, price, and a remove action. To the right of this list, a compact summary panel sits in a card-like box, displaying subtotal, estimated taxes, and the prominent 'Proceed to Checkout' button, with a subtle 'Continue Shopping' link nearby. At the bottom of the item list, a 'Clear Cart' control provides a secondary destructive option. When the cart is empty, the list and summary panel disappear and are replaced by a centered empty-state message within the main right column, with a prominent path back to continue shopping. The entire composition preserves the observed patterns: strong horizontal segmentation, a left-hand navigation spine, and a functional, image-light but information-heavy body that guides the user from understanding what’s in the cart to confidently proceeding to checkout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-init-controller is non-visual and conceptually sits at the very top of the right main content column, just below the global divider bar and before the 'Shopping Cart' heading in the document flow. It initializes the cart state so that either the cart-item-list plus cart-summary-panel or the empty-cart-state is rendered. It does not occupy any visible space or affect the spacing between components; it simply runs before the visible layout of the right column is composed.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; participates only in logical ordering, not in spatial layout. It is positioned logically before all cart content in the DOM but has no rendered box or spatial behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list appears in the right main content column, beginning approximately 32–40px below the 'Shopping Cart' heading and its brief descriptive paragraph. It occupies the left portion of the right column’s lower area, spanning about 65–70% of the right column width. The list forms a vertical stack of horizontally oriented rows, aligned to the same left edge as the page heading. There is a consistent 16–24px top padding inside the list container before the first row, and 16–20px padding on the left and right to keep rows away from the column edges. The list’s bottom edge stops 32–40px above the 'Clear Cart' and other footer-level actions, maintaining a clear separation from those controls. On narrower viewports, the list continues to occupy the primary width while the summary panel drops below it; in all cases, the list retains a straightforward, table-like vertical flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as a standard block-level container in the main content area, expanding vertically as rows are added. It remains aligned to the left within the right column and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each cart-item-row is a full-width horizontal strip within the cart-item-list, spanning nearly the entire width of the list container (minus its internal padding). Rows are stacked with approximately 12–16px vertical gap between them, giving a slightly compact, spreadsheet-like feel. Each row has about 16–20px padding on all sides and is optionally separated by a light bottom border aligned to the industrial, utilitarian aesthetic. Inside a row, content is divided into four horizontal zones from left to right: a narrow product thumbnail area on the far left (about 10–15% of the row width), a broader product details area next (about 45–55%) for name, SKU, and any variant/spec text, the quantity-editor-form area to its right (about 15–20%), and a rightmost area that holds the item price and the remove-item-button aligned to the far right edge. All elements are vertically centered within the row’s height, which is typically 64–88px depending on content. On smaller screens, these zones can wrap, but the default desktop view mirrors a slightly old-school, tabular cart layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard list item behavior: each row expands vertically to fit product detail text, but maintains its left-to-right zone proportions where space allows. Rows scroll with the cart-item-list and do not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The quantity-editor-form sits inside each cart-item-row, in the third horizontal zone from the left. It is positioned roughly in the center-right portion of the row, directly to the right of the product details block and just to the left of the price/remove area. It occupies about 15–20% of the row width, enough space for a small horizontal control group (minus button, numeric input, plus button) or a compact dropdown. The form is vertically centered within the row, maintaining at least 8–12px of breathing room above and below. The left edge of the quantity editor aligns with an 8px-based grid, so the distance from the product details block is a clean 16px. This keeps the editor visually distinct but tightly integrated, reflecting the slightly dense, utilitarian cart layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within each row’s flex/grid layout. It does not affect the row’s outer dimensions beyond its defined width, and it moves with the row when the list scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The remove-item-button appears in the rightmost zone of each cart-item-row, aligned with the item price. It is positioned at the far right edge of the row’s content area, typically as a text link or compact icon-button, set about 8–12px to the right of the price text. Both price and remove control are vertically centered relative to the row. The button maintains at least 8px padding around its clickable area, but visually it remains compact so the right side of the row feels functional rather than decorative. On narrow layouts where content stacks, the remove button may drop beneath the price but remains right-aligned within that block to preserve the clear, action-at-the-edge pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline action aligned to the far right within each cart-item-row, scrolling with the row. It does not stick or float; its position is determined by the internal layout (e.g., flex alignment) of the row.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The clear-cart-control sits below the cart-item-list within the right main content column, aligned to the same left edge as the list and page heading. It is placed approximately 24–32px beneath the last cart-item-row, giving a clear visual separation between item-level actions and this global destructive action. The control itself appears as a subtle text-like button or secondary-styled button, sized to its label and not spanning the full width. To its right, at a distance of 16–24px, the continue-shopping-link may also appear, forming a small horizontal cluster of secondary actions at the bottom-left of the list area. This placement keeps it visible but subordinate to the cart summary and checkout actions to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard inline/block control near the bottom of the main cart content. It remains in the regular flow of the page and scrolls away as the user moves up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the cart is empty, the empty-cart-state occupies the entire right main content column just below the horizontal divider and any global header. The usual cart-item-list, cart-summary-panel, and clear-cart-control are hidden. Instead, a centered empty-state block appears, beginning roughly 48px below the 'Shopping Cart' heading (or the heading can be integrated into the empty state). This block is about 60–70% of the right column’s width and is horizontally centered within that column. It includes an icon or simple illustration at the top, a bold 'Your cart is empty' message, explanatory text beneath, and prominently the continue-shopping-link as the main action. The empty-state content is vertically spaced with generous gaps (24–32px) between elements, giving it slightly more openness than the dense cart rows, while still fitting within the overall utilitarian framing. The left sidebar remains present, but the eye is drawn to this centered message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Replaces all other cart-content blocks within the right column based on state. It behaves as a standard centered block in the main content area and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel is positioned in the right main content column, to the right of the cart-item-list, forming a secondary column inside the main content band. Its top edge aligns vertically with the top of the first visible cart-item-row (roughly 32–40px below the 'Shopping Cart' descriptive text), so the list and summary read as parallel sections. It occupies about 30–35% of the right column width and is right-aligned against the column’s inner edge, leaving a consistent 24–32px horizontal gap between itself and the cart-item-list on its left. The panel is styled like a compact card with 16–24px internal padding on all sides, a light border or subtle background to distinguish it from the page background, and vertically stacked contents: label, subtotal/totals, any notes, the proceed-to-checkout-button, and the continue-shopping-link. The panel’s height is just enough to contain its content; it does not stretch to match the full height of the list, so visually it appears as a tight, functional summary box. On narrower viewports, it may shift below the cart-item-list, but it maintains its card-like, compact structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level panel aligned to the right within the main content. It scrolls with the page and does not use sticky positioning by default, reflecting the straightforward, template-like design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The proceed-to-checkout-button is the primary call to action within the cart-summary-panel. It is placed near the bottom of the panel’s content stack, typically following the subtotal/total lines and any applicable notes. The button spans nearly the full width of the summary panel’s inner area, with 16px horizontal padding and ample 12–16px vertical padding to appear substantial and clearly tap/clickable. There is about 16–24px vertical spacing above the button to separate it from the totals, and 8–16px below it before the continue-shopping-link or any secondary text. The button is centered horizontally within the panel’s card, and its prominence (solid color, stronger contrast) makes it the main visual focal point of the panel, clearly drawing the eye after the user reviews their totals.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the cart-summary-panel’s vertical stack. It does not float or stick; it stays within the card and moves as the summary panel scrolls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The continue-shopping-link appears in two closely related positions, maintaining a consistent visual style. In the typical non-empty cart state, its primary placement is inside the cart-summary-panel, directly beneath the proceed-to-checkout-button, separated by approximately 8–16px of vertical space. It is left-aligned with the button text, styled as a secondary link (less visual weight), and confined to the panel’s width so it reads as a supportive, alternative action. Optionally, a duplicate or relocated version can appear near the clear-cart-control under the cart-item-list, aligned to the left and spaced 16–24px from the clear-cart-control, forming a small cluster of secondary actions at the bottom-left of the main content area. In the empty-cart-state, this same component is centered within the empty-state block and treated as the primary way to move forward, positioned about 24px below the empty-state message. Across all contexts, its placement keeps it visible but subordinate to the main checkout path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline link that participates in the normal flow of either the cart-summary-panel or the empty-cart-state. It does not have any floating or sticky behavior and moves with its parent container.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Manage shipping details, select a shipping method based on delivery needs, and review the order summary before proceeding to purchase.

core_features: Initialize or resume a checkout session for the active cart using startCheckout.
Collect, validate, and persist the shipping address via setCheckoutShippingAddress.
Retrieve and display available shipping methods for the current checkout using getAvailableShippingMethodsForCheckout.
Allow the user to specify delivery time requirements and automatically highlight the cheapest shipping method that satisfies those requirements.
Persist the selected shipping method (including shipping cost and delivery estimates) via selectCheckoutShippingMethod.
Retrieve a full checkout summary (cart items, shipping address, shipping method, totals) using getCheckoutSummary for a final review step.
Surface key shipping policy information on the checkout page via getShippingPolicies and provide navigation to a detailed policy document.
Support a simple multi-step checkout flow controlled by the URL parameter step (e.g., shipping entry/selection vs. review).
user_workflows:
1) Start or resume checkout
- User arrives from cart.html via a 'Proceed to Checkout' action or by direct URL to checkout.html.
- Page reads URL parameter step; if missing or invalid, defaults logically to 'shipping'.
- checkout-session-initializer component calls startCheckout to create or resume a CheckoutSession tied to the current Cart.
- If the CheckoutSession already has a shipping address and/or a selected shipping method, those values are used to prefill the form and selection components.
- Based on CheckoutSession.status and the step parameter, the page either shows the shipping step or the review step.

2) Enter or edit shipping address
- On the 'shipping' step, the user fills in shipping_first_name, shipping_last_name, shipping_street, shipping_city, shipping_state, shipping_zip, and shipping_country in the shipping-address-form.
- On form submit, the component validates required fields on the client side (format, non-empty) and then calls setCheckoutShippingAddress with the collected shippingAddress object.
- The API response updates the local CheckoutSession state; any server-side validation errors are shown inline.
- After a successful save, the page fetches available shipping methods (if not already loaded) so the user can immediately choose a method.

3) Choose shipping method and apply delivery constraints
- Once a valid shipping address is set, the shipping-method-selector calls getAvailableShippingMethodsForCheckout.
- All returned ShippingMethod options are listed with name, description, price, and estimated delivery range derived from min_delivery_days and max_delivery_days (or other available fields).
- The delivery-requirement-filter component lets the user express a requirement such as a maximum acceptable delivery duration or a desired latest delivery date.
- Based on the user’s input and the loaded ShippingMethod data, the component computes which methods meet the requirement and identifies the cheapest eligible method by comparing their price values.
- The recommended eligible method is highlighted; the user can either accept it or manually select a different method.
- When the user selects a shipping method (including accepting the recommended one), shipping-method-selector calls selectCheckoutShippingMethod with the chosen shippingMethodId.
- The response updates CheckoutSession (including shipping_cost and estimated_min_delivery_date / estimated_max_delivery_date) and Cart totals; then the order summary is refreshed using getCheckoutSummary.

4) View shipping policy details
- The shipping-policy-snippet component calls getShippingPolicies to show concise conditions (such as which methods may be free under certain conditions) and a structured list of methods for reference.
- The user can click 'View full shipping policy' which navigates to policies.html?doc=shipping_policy without additional API calls from this page.
- Returning from policies.html to checkout.html re-triggers the normal initialization flow using startCheckout.

5) Review order before proceeding
- When the user clicks 'Continue to Review' from the shipping step, the page checks that a shipping address and selected shipping method exist in the CheckoutSession; if not, it displays validation messages and prevents navigation.
- If everything is set, the page updates the URL to checkout.html?step=review (e.g., via a link or history API) and reloads or statefully switches to the review step.
- On the review step, the order-summary-panel calls getCheckoutSummary to load Cart, CartItem list, CheckoutSession details, and the selected ShippingMethod.
- The user reviews product lines, quantities, prices, shipping address, selected shipping method, shipping cost, tax_total (if provided), and total.
- From here the user can either navigate back to checkout.html?step=shipping to make changes or proceed to the next stage of purchase (e.g., payment) via a form submission or navigation outside the scope of the given interfaces.

6) Adjust details from review step
- On the review step, the user may click an 'Edit shipping address & method' control.
- The page updates the URL to checkout.html?step=shipping.
- The shipping-address-form and shipping-method-selector are pre-populated with existing CheckoutSession data.
- The user can modify the address (calling setCheckoutShippingAddress) and/or choose a different shipping method (calling selectCheckoutShippingMethod), which in turn refreshes the summary via getCheckoutSummary.
interactions: Click 'Back to Cart' link: navigates to cart.html, discarding the current page context but leaving any server-managed CheckoutSession as-is.

On page load or when URL parameter step changes: checkout-session-initializer calls startCheckout to ensure there is a current CheckoutSession and associated Cart, then decides which step UI to render.
Shipping address form input changes (text fields, dropdowns): update local form state; no API call until the user submits the form.

Shipping address form submit: triggers client-side validation; if valid, calls setCheckoutShippingAddress(shippingAddress); on success, updates CheckoutSession state and enables shipping-method-selector; on error, shows inline validation messages.

Click 'Load/Refresh shipping options' (if provided explicitly) or automatic load on valid address: calls getAvailableShippingMethodsForCheckout; binds the returned list of ShippingMethod objects into the shipping-method-selector.

Change delivery constraint input (e.g., change maximum delivery days or select a latest acceptable delivery date): delivery-requirement-filter recalculates eligible methods using the already-loaded ShippingMethod list, identifies the cheapest eligible method, updates recommendation state in shipping-method-selector, but does not call APIs.

Select a shipping method (radio button or similar): triggers selectCheckoutShippingMethod(shippingMethodId); on success, updates CheckoutSession and Cart; then calls getCheckoutSummary to refresh the order summary section.
Click 'View full shipping policy': navigates to policies.html?doc=shipping_policy in the same or new tab, without altering the CheckoutSession from this page.
Click 'Continue to Review': checks local CheckoutSession state (or results from latest getCheckoutSummary) to verify required shipping data; if valid, changes location to checkout.html?step=review.
On entering the review step: order-summary-panel calls getCheckoutSummary; binds returned Cart, CartItems, CheckoutSession, and ShippingMethod to the summary UI.
Click 'Edit shipping' from the review step: updates URL back to checkout.html?step=shipping so the user can modify address or shipping method.
Click 'Proceed to Payment' or equivalent final action (implementation-specific): posts or navigates to the next page in the checkout flow; no additional use of the provided interfaces on this page.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state driver on this page is the URL parameter step combined with server-managed CheckoutSession state.

- URL parameter step:
  - Accepted values include at least 'shipping' and 'review'.
  - If step is missing, invalid, or empty, the page defaults to 'shipping'.
  - When step='shipping', the page shows components necessary to collect or edit the shipping address and select a shipping method (shipping-address-form, shipping-method-selector, delivery-requirement-filter, shipping-policy-snippet, and a minimal order-summary-panel if desired).
  - When step='review', the page hides editing forms and shows a read-only summary (order-summary-panel with review-step-actions), while still allowing navigation back to step='shipping'.

- CheckoutSession-driven state:
  - startCheckout is called on initial load and whenever the page needs to ensure a valid session after navigation.
  - If startCheckout returns a CheckoutSession with status 'shipping_selected' or 'completed' but the URL step is 'shipping', the page may still show the shipping step but pre-populated. It does not override the URL parameter; instead it uses status only to pre-select data and possibly suggest moving to 'review'.
  - If startCheckout returns a CheckoutSession without a shipping address and step='review', the page automatically redirects (via client-side navigation) to step='shipping' to enforce the flow.
  - setCheckoutShippingAddress updates the CheckoutSession in memory; subsequent calls to getAvailableShippingMethodsForCheckout assume that the server uses the latest shipping address for eligibility and pricing.
  - selectCheckoutShippingMethod updates CheckoutSession.selected_shipping_method_id, shipping_cost, and estimated delivery dates; the updated Cart totals are used for display via getCheckoutSummary.

- No localStorage or other client-side persistent storage is used. All persisted checkout state is managed by the backend via the CheckoutSession entity and associated Cart, accessed only through the provided interfaces. The client relies solely on URL parameters (particularly step) and the latest API responses to decide what to render.

components.functionality: Orchestrates page initialization: reads URL parameter step, calls startCheckout to create or resume a CheckoutSession for the current Cart, determines which step UI ('shipping' or 'review') to render, and ensures dependent components receive the latest CheckoutSession and Cart data.
Collects and edits the shipping address fields for the CheckoutSession. Prefills inputs from the current CheckoutSession when available and submits changes to the backend.

Allows the user to specify delivery timing requirements (for example, a maximum delivery duration or a latest acceptable delivery date). Uses these requirements to filter the available ShippingMethod list and determine which methods satisfy the constraint.

Displays the list of available shipping methods for the current checkout, including pricing and estimated delivery ranges. Integrates with the delivery-requirement-filter to highlight the cheapest eligible ShippingMethod that meets the user’s delivery requirement and persists the user’s chosen method.

Fetches structured shipping policy information to display concise, contextual notes about available shipping methods and any free-shipping conditions. Provides a link to the full shipping policy document.

Shows a complete summary of the current checkout, including cart items, quantities, pricing, shipping address, selected shipping method, shipping cost, tax total, and overall total, as returned by getCheckoutSummary.
Manages navigation between checkout steps ('shipping' and 'review') and to external pages such as cart.html, by reading and updating the step URL parameter and reacting to user navigation actions.

Provides final actions on the review step, such as confirming that the user has reviewed the order and proceeding to the next phase of the purchase flow (e.g., payment) via navigation or form submission outside the provided interfaces.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom pattern as the mockup: a full-width industrial hero at the very top with centered site title and global navigation, followed by a bold horizontal divider bar. Beneath this, the main checkout area is a two-column band centered on the page: a narrow left sidebar for vertical step navigation, and a wider right column for the active checkout content. Inside the right column, the content is stacked in a clear vertical order: page title → short explanatory text → shipping address form → delivery requirement filter → shipping method selector → shipping policy note → checkout actions, with the order summary panel visually docked to the right side of this column, parallel to the forms.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear top-to-bottom pattern as the mockup: a full-width industrial hero at the very top with centered site title and global navigation, followed by a bold horizontal divider bar. Beneath this, the main checkout area is a two-column band centered on the page: a narrow left sidebar for vertical step navigation, and a wider right column for the active checkout content. Inside the right column, the content is stacked in a clear vertical order: page title → short explanatory text → shipping address form → delivery requirement filter → shipping method selector → shipping policy note → checkout actions, with the order summary panel visually docked to the right side of this column, parallel to the forms.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport with full-bleed photography. Below that, the main checkout band spans a central content width (e.g., around 1040–1200px) with modest left/right margins. Within this band, the left sidebar takes about 20–25% of the width, while the right main content takes 75–80%. Inside the right main content, the form stack (address, filter, methods, actions) occupies roughly the left 60–65% of the right column, with the order summary panel occupying the remaining 35–40% on the right, separated by a 24–32px gutter. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows a 32–48px rhythm; internal padding for panels and cards is 16–24px, aligned to the 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate and functional. The hero is visually open with large typography over the industrial image, but the checkout area is more compact and information-dense. Sidebar steps are tightly stacked with 8–16px vertical gaps. Form fields in the shipping address and shipping method selector are arranged with 16–24px between rows and 8–16px between labels and inputs. The order summary panel is dense but clearly segmented with short dividers and 8–16px spacing between line items. Overall, whitespace is sufficient to avoid clutter while still feeling utilitarian and efficient, echoing the slightly old-school Google Sites vibe.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye lands first on the center of the hero (site title/logo over industrial imagery), then is drawn down to the global nav and the horizontal divider. From there, the eye moves into the main checkout band, first catching the left vertical step navigation as a structural anchor, then quickly shifting to the large 'Checkout' page title at the top of the right column. After reading the title and brief description, attention flows downward through the shipping address form, then to the delivery requirement filter and shipping method selector. In parallel, peripheral vision continually picks up the order summary panel fixed along the right, encouraging users to glance sideways to confirm their order. Finally, the visual flow ends at the bottom-aligned checkout action buttons, which sit on a strong horizontal axis and visually signal the next step.
layout_strategies.overall_layout_description: The page presents as a straightforward, corporate checkout in the same framework as the rest of the site. At the top, a wide industrial photograph spans the full browser width, with a bold, centered site name and simple top navigation overlaid or sitting just beneath it. A thick, muted gray horizontal rule runs across the screen below the hero, clearly separating the branding band from the transactional area. Under this divider, the main content is framed in a centered container that feels slightly inset from the viewport edges. On the left, a narrow column holds a vertical list of checkout steps, forming a tall structural rail that extends down the page. To the right, a wider column carries the 'Checkout – Shipping & Delivery' heading and a brief, one- or two-line description, followed by an efficient stack of form-based content. The shipping address form sits in the upper-left of this right column, while a contained order summary card rises along the upper-right, so that forms and summary appear side-by-side like two coordinated columns within the main area. Beneath the address, a compact row of controls asks about special delivery requirements, and below that a vertical list of shipping method options fills out the middle of the page. A slim policy snippet stretches across the lower part of the right column, grounding the content with a subtle gray background or fine rule. The page culminates in a row of checkout action buttons aligned along the bottom of the right column, creating a strong horizontal endpoint. The entire layout feels utilitarian, grid-driven, and direct, balancing a slightly compact density with clear segmentation and predictable, old-school web structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-session-initializer is a non-visual controller that sits logically at the very top of the main checkout band, just below the horizontal divider and above all visual content in the right column. It does not occupy visible space in the layout; instead, it initializes state before any other checkout components render. From a spatial standpoint, it is effectively positioned behind the scenes at the top of the right-column content stack, ensuring that when the user first sees the page, the visible top elements are the checkout-step-navigation on the left and the page title with the shipping address form section on the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, zero-footprint controller that loads before and governs the behavior of the visible checkout components; it does not affect layout flow or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-address-form occupies the upper-left portion of the right main content column, directly below the main 'Checkout – Shipping & Delivery' heading and a short descriptive paragraph. It starts roughly aligned with the top of the order summary panel to its right, forming a clear horizontal row. The form itself spans about 60–65% of the width of the right column, leaving a consistent 24–32px vertical gutter to its right before the order-summary-panel begins. Inputs are arranged as a stacked form with 16–24px vertical spacing between rows, and 8–16px between labels and fields, matching the moderately compact density of the mockup. Edge-to-edge, the form content is inset from the right column by 24px on the left and right, reinforcing the grid. The bottom of the form ends roughly mid-page, handing off to the delivery-requirement-filter which appears immediately below with about 24–32px of separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the page; anchors the left side of the right column’s upper content and defines the starting point of the main form flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The delivery-requirement-filter sits directly below the shipping-address-form in the same left-aligned sub-column of the right main content area. It begins approximately 24–32px beneath the last form field, maintaining the vertical rhythm from the mockup. Horizontally, it aligns flush with the left edge of the shipping-address-form and spans the same width, keeping the left side of the right column visually continuous. The filter appears as a compact control band: a brief label or question on the left, followed by a row of radio buttons, toggles, or pill-style options arranged horizontally. When additional controls wrap, they stack in a second row with 8–16px vertical spacing. Overall it occupies a low-profile strip—roughly 10–15% of the vertical space of the right column’s above-the-fold area—serving as a bridge between address entry and shipping method selection.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the main content, tightly coupled to the shipping-address-form above and the shipping-method-selector below, preserving the linear form flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-method-selector is positioned immediately below the delivery-requirement-filter, forming the central decision section of the right column. It aligns with the same left edge and width as the shipping-address-form, keeping the left sub-column consistent. A 24–32px gap separates it from the filter above. Within this block, shipping methods are displayed as a vertical list of selectable rows or cards—each row spanning nearly the full width of the sub-column—with 16–24px vertical spacing between options. Each method row contains the method name on the left, estimated delivery dates underneath or just beside it, and pricing on the right, creating a clear left-to-right scan inside each row. The entire selector block typically occupies 20–35% of the page height depending on the number of methods, and ends with a modest 24–32px gap before the shipping-policy-snippet. The right edge of the selector stays visually aligned with the left edge of the order-summary-panel gutter, reinforcing the internal two-column relationship within the right main content area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls normally with the content; serves as a vertically expanding block whose height depends on the number of available shipping methods.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-policy-snippet appears as a low-profile informational band directly beneath the shipping-method-selector, helping to visually close out the main selection area before the final actions. It begins about 24–32px below the last shipping method option. Unlike the form blocks above, this snippet spans the full width of the right main content column—from its left padding edge to its right padding edge—bridging across the gap that exists between the left-side forms and the right-side order summary panel. It appears as a short paragraph or two of text, optionally on a light gray or subtly tinted background to distinguish it from the interactive sections above. Internal padding of 16–24px top and bottom gives the band a slightly more open feel despite the overall moderate density. Vertically, it takes up only about 10–15% of the right column’s visible height, leaving enough space below for the review-step-actions to sit with clear separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the content; forms a full-width, non-interactive block that visually ties the left form sub-column and right summary panel together.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-summary-panel is docked to the right side of the right main content column, visually parallel to the shipping-address-form and selection blocks. Its top edge aligns roughly with or slightly below the top of the shipping-address-form, creating a strong horizontal alignment. The panel’s left edge sits 24–32px to the right of the shipping-address-form’s right edge, forming a consistent vertical gutter between the form sub-column and the summary. It occupies about 35–40% of the width of the right column and extends vertically to match or nearly match the combined height of the shipping-address-form and shipping-method-selector. Inside the panel, content is arranged in a card-like structure: a bold 'Order Summary' heading at the top, followed by a stacked list of line items, subtotals, shipping costs, taxes, and a prominent total, all separated by thin horizontal rules and 8–16px vertical gaps. The panel’s internal padding is 16–24px on all sides. On taller pages, as the user scrolls, the panel stays visually anchored on the right so that it remains visible beside the form content rather than disappearing immediately off-screen.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky card on larger screens—remaining fixed within the viewport once scrolled into position, as long as the user is within the checkout section. On smaller screens, it can drop below the form content and scroll normally to preserve usable space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-step-navigation occupies the narrow left sidebar within the main checkout band, directly under the horizontal divider and extending downward alongside the right column content. It spans about 20–25% of the total content width and is vertically aligned so that its first step aligns approximately with the top of the main 'Checkout – Shipping & Delivery' heading in the right column. The steps are arranged as a vertical list, with each step label and indicator stacked top to bottom, separated by 8–16px vertical spacing. The current step (e.g., 'Shipping & Delivery') is visually emphasized with a bold label and possibly a background highlight or indicator bar, while completed and upcoming steps are more subdued, reflecting the utilitarian, slightly old-school styling. The left sidebar has 16–24px padding from its container edges, giving the step list a neat, compact column. As the user scrolls, the step navigation remains in view, acting as a stable vertical anchor on the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the left sidebar on desktop, remaining fixed relative to the viewport once the user scrolls past the hero, so that the user always sees their current checkout step. On smaller viewports, it can move above the main content as a horizontal step indicator and scroll normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The review-step-actions block sits at the bottom of the right main content column, directly below the shipping-policy-snippet. A 24–32px vertical gap separates it from the snippet above. It spans the full width of the right column’s inner container, aligning with both the left edge of the shipping forms and the right edge of the order summary’s visual boundary. Within this block, primary and secondary actions—such as 'Continue to Payment' and 'Back to Cart'—are laid out horizontally. The primary button is aligned to the right, with the secondary action either to its left with 16–24px spacing or left-aligned as a text link, creating a strong right-weighted call-to-action in keeping with transactional patterns. Vertical padding of 16–24px ensures the action row feels significant without being oversized. On scroll, this row naturally becomes the final element users encounter, forming a clear visual and functional endpoint for the page’s flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the content and serves as the terminal section of the right column; on smaller screens, buttons may wrap to a vertical stack while remaining anchored at the bottom of the content stack.

name: Services Overview
title: Services Overview
description: Displays all available fabrication and repair services and routes users to detailed service pages using dynamic service data.

core_features: Fetch and display a dynamic list of services using getServicesOverview, showing each service’s name and short description.
Allow users to navigate from any service in the list to its detailed service page via a URL parameter (service_detail.html?serviceId={service.service_id}).
Optionally highlight a specific service based on the incoming URL parameter highlight, matching it against the service_id field from the loaded services.
Expose basic service attributes (such as supported regions, supported materials, and availability of quote/booking forms) to help users quickly assess which services fit their needs.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – View all services:
1. User opens services.html without additional URL parameters.
2. Page-init component parses the URL (no highlight found) and triggers getServicesOverview.
3. Services-data-loader calls getServicesOverview and receives an array of Service records.
4. Services-list renders one service-card per Service, using name and short_description, plus indicators based on has_quote_form/has_booking_form, supports_regions/supports_materials.
5. User reviews the list and chooses a service to explore.
6. User clicks the desired service-card (or its detail link), and the browser navigates to service_detail.html?serviceId={clickedService.service_id}.

Workflow 2 – Arrive with a highlighted service (from home page):
1. User clicks a service link on index.html that points to services.html?highlight={someServiceId}.
2. Page-init reads the highlight URL parameter and stores its value as highlightServiceId in page state.
3. Services-data-loader invokes getServicesOverview and passes the resulting Service array to Services-list.
4. Highlight-controller compares highlightServiceId with each Service.service_id.
5. The service-card whose service_id matches highlightServiceId is rendered in a highlighted state (e.g., visually distinct) and optionally scrolled into view.
6. If the user clicks another service-card, the page navigates to the corresponding service_detail.html?serviceId={thatService.service_id} without altering the Services Overview page’s URL (since the user leaves the page).

Workflow 3 – Clear or change highlighted service via URL:
1. User lands on services.html?highlight={serviceIdA}; the matching service-card is highlighted.
2. User modifies the URL (e.g., via an internal control such as a 'Clear highlight' action that updates the address bar) to remove or change the highlight parameter.
3. Page-init/onUrlParamChange re-parses the URL and updates highlightServiceId (to undefined or to a new value).
4. Highlight-controller re-evaluates all service-cards: if highlightServiceId is undefined or does not match any service_id, no cards are highlighted; if it matches a different service_id, the previously highlighted card reverts to normal and the new matching card becomes highlighted.
interactions: On page load (onInit): parse the highlight URL parameter from location.search and store it as highlightServiceId in page state; then trigger getServicesOverview.
On services data loaded (onDataLoaded): pass the returned array of Service objects to Services-list and to Highlight-controller for matching against highlightServiceId.

On URL parameter change while staying on the page (onUrlParamChange, e.g., via history API or manual URL changes where supported): re-parse the highlight parameter and re-run the highlight logic on existing Service data.
On click of a service-card container (onClickServiceCard): navigate to service_detail.html?serviceId={service.service_id} using the service_id from the clicked Service record.
On click of a dedicated 'Learn more' or equivalent detail action inside a service-card (onClickServiceDetailLink): perform the same navigation to service_detail.html?serviceId={service.service_id}.

On error from getServicesOverview (onDataError): show a functional error state (e.g., an error message region) indicating that services could not be loaded and provide a retry action that re-calls getServicesOverview.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary external state driver for this page is the highlight URL parameter and the services data returned by getServicesOverview.

URL parameter handling:
- On each page load, the page reads location.search and extracts the highlight parameter value (if present).
- The extracted value is treated purely as a candidate service identifier (highlightServiceId) and is not trusted until matched against a Service.service_id from the API response.
- If highlight is absent, highlightServiceId is left undefined and no special highlighting is applied.
- If highlight is present but does not match any Service.service_id in the loaded data, the page remains in a non-highlighted state; no errors are thrown.

Data and view state:
- The page invokes getServicesOverview once on initial load (and on explicit retry) to retrieve an array of Service entities.
- Until data is returned, the page is in a 'loading' state; Services-list is either empty or shows a loading indicator.
- On success, the list of Service records becomes the single source of truth for rendered services; there is no hardcoded list.
- Highlight-controller uses the combination of highlightServiceId and the loaded Service array to mark at most one service-card as highlighted.

Navigation behavior:
- When the user clicks any service-card or its detail link, navigation is performed via a full page change to service_detail.html with the serviceId query parameter set to the service_id value of the clicked Service.
- This mechanism works for any Service returned by getServicesOverview, including (but not limited to) ones whose service_id equals values such as 'laser_cutting', 'emergency_repair', or 'custom_cutting'. The page never hardcodes these identifiers; it always reads them from the Service entities.

Persistence and storage:
- No localStorage, sessionStorage, or cookies are used to store page state.
- All state is derived from the current URL parameters and the latest response from getServicesOverview.
- If the page is reloaded, state is recomputed from the URL and fresh service data from getServicesOverview.

components.functionality: Initializes the Services Overview page by parsing URL parameters, triggering the data load via getServicesOverview, and wiring the resulting data and highlight state into child components.
Executes getServicesOverview to retrieve the dynamic list of Service entities and exposes loading, success, and error states to the rest of the page.

Renders a collection of service-card components from the array of Service entities provided by services-data-loader, and passes highlight status to each card based on the currently active highlightServiceId.

Displays functional information for a single Service record (such as name and short_description) and provides interactive elements for navigation to the detailed service page. It can render in a highlighted state when its service_id matches the current highlightServiceId.

Manages highlight logic by comparing the URL-derived highlightServiceId with the service_id of each Service returned by getServicesOverview, marking the matching service-card as highlighted and optionally triggering scroll-into-view for that card.
Displays a functional error message and a retry control when getServicesOverview fails, allowing the user to re-trigger the service data load.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero banner at the very top (industrial imagery + site title + top navigation), directly followed by a full-width horizontal divider. Below the divider, all service-related components live within a central content container that is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar (for site/service navigation, not part of this component set) and a wider right main content column. Within the right column, the Services Overview page title and short descriptive text appear first, and immediately beneath that the dynamic services-list component renders a grid of service-card items. The services-error-state occupies the same area as the services-list when loading fails, preserving the overall structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strict top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero banner at the very top (industrial imagery + site title + top navigation), directly followed by a full-width horizontal divider. Below the divider, all service-related components live within a central content container that is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar (for site/service navigation, not part of this component set) and a wider right main content column. Within the right column, the Services Overview page title and short descriptive text appear first, and immediately beneath that the dynamic services-list component renders a grid of service-card items. The services-error-state occupies the same area as the services-list when loading fails, preserving the overall structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero uses the full viewport width and roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height, with content centered both horizontally and vertically within the image. Below, the central content container spans about 80–90% of the page width, centered, with subtle left/right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar consumes roughly 20–25% of the width, while the right main content column occupies the remaining 75–80%. The services-list grid stretches nearly the full width of the main content column, with 16–24px gaps between cards. Vertical spacing between the hero and the content band, and between headings and the services grid, is around 32–40px, aligned to the 8px system.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero section feels open: large image, large centered title, and comfortable vertical breathing room. Below the divider, density increases to a functional, slightly compact layout: sidebar links are stacked with relatively tight vertical spacing, and the main column text uses moderate line height with 24–32px gaps between heading, description, and grid. The services-list grid is visually dense (multiple cards visible above the fold) but still readable thanks to consistent card dimensions, 16–24px internal padding, and regular gutters between items. Error and loading states occupy the same area as the grid, maintaining density and structure.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual entry point is the page-top hero: eyes land on the centered title, then move downward to the supporting text and top navigation. A strong horizontal divider redirects attention straight down into the main content area. In the body, the eye is pulled first to the large 'Services Overview' heading in the right column, then to the short descriptive paragraph directly beneath. From there, the gaze flows downward into the services-list grid, scanning cards from left to right in rows. The left sidebar acts as a vertical anchor, catching the eye when users look for different sections or service categories, but the services-list remains the primary focal area. Error or loading states, when present, are centered within the same main content region, ensuring the visual flow is uninterrupted even when data changes.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a wide, industrial hero image that stretches from edge to edge across the top of the screen, occupying roughly the upper third of the viewport. Centered over the image sits the company or site title in a bold, utilitarian type treatment, with minimal supporting text and a straightforward top navigation aligned in a simple horizontal row. Beneath this, a solid horizontal divider bar runs the full width of the page, clearly separating the hero from the working content area. Below the divider, a central content container spans most of the page width and is split into two columns: on the left, a narrow vertical sidebar lists site or service navigation links in a compact stack; on the right, a wider main content column carries the Services Overview heading at the top, followed by a short block of explanatory copy. Directly under this text, the services-list fills the remainder of the main column with a grid of service-card tiles arranged in multiple columns, with consistent gaps and aligned edges that echo the old-school Google Sites grid feel. When services fail to load, an error message block appears centered in place of the grid, maintaining the same proportions and alignment so the layout remains stable. The result is a straightforward, corporate-feeling page where the hero sets context and the main content area efficiently lists and routes users into detailed service views.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is not visually represented on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the top of the page hierarchy, just beneath the full-page shell that includes the hero, divider, and two-column body container. It initializes the Services Overview route before any visible services content appears. From a spatial perspective, it governs when the right-hand main content column can show either the loading state, the services-list grid, or the services-error-state, but it does not occupy any visible space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, purely logical; participates in layout only by determining which child components are rendered within the right main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This data-loader is also non-visual by default, but it controls a visual loading experience in the main content column. While services data is being fetched, it reserves the same rectangular area that will eventually hold the services-list grid: this area begins approximately 32px below the introductory text in the right main column and spans nearly the full width of that column (about 75–80% of the page width overall). A loading indicator (such as a centered spinner or skeleton card placeholders) should appear centered horizontally within this region, with at least 48px of vertical padding above and below so it reads as a contained block. Once data is loaded, this space is seamlessly taken over by the services-list grid, preserving the vertical flow from heading → description → content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the main content area where the services-list would render, but only while loading; it is otherwise removed from the visual flow. It behaves as a standard block element within the right main column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-list component is the primary visual content in the right main column. It is positioned directly below the Services Overview page title and its descriptive paragraph, with a vertical gap of approximately 32px (aligned to the 8px base unit). It spans almost the full width of the right main column, leaving a 16–24px internal margin from the column’s left and right edges to create a clean frame. Within this area, the list renders a responsive grid of service-card items: on a typical desktop width, the grid should show 3 columns (each card occupying roughly one third of the main column width, minus 16–24px gutters); on narrower tablet widths, it collapses to 2 columns; and on mobile, it stacks in a single column. Vertical and horizontal gaps between cards are consistent at 16–24px, creating a moderately dense but organized block of content. The grid extends downward as far as needed, with around 32–40px of bottom margin separating the last row of cards from any footer or subsequent content. Visually, this grid forms the lower half of the main content area and is the main scanning surface after the heading and intro text.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right main column, expanding vertically as more service-card items are present. It reflows responsively with the page width, adjusting the number of columns but always maintaining equal card widths and consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each service-card is a rectangular tile within the services-list grid. On desktop, each card occupies roughly one third of the main content column width (minus horizontal gutters), producing 3 columns per row. The card has a slightly compact feel: internal padding of 16–24px on all sides, with content stacked vertically. The upper portion of the card (about the top 55–60% of its height) is dominated by an image or icon area representing the service—this image spans the full width of the card with a fixed aspect ratio (e.g., 4:3) and aligns flush to the card’s left and right edges. Below the image, the service title is left-aligned and uses a bold, utilitarian type size, followed by one or two lines of descriptive text or key attributes in a smaller size with moderate line spacing. There is a 8–16px gap between the image and the title, and another 8–12px gap between the title and description, maintaining alignment with the 8px spacing system. Cards have a subtle border (1px) and a light background to create a simple industrial feel rather than a highly polished, shadow-heavy aesthetic. Margins between adjacent cards are 16–24px horizontally and vertically, aligning cards into clean rows and columns that match the overall grid structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a flexible grid item, automatically wrapping to a new row when space runs out. On hover or selection, visual states managed by the highlight-controller adjust border and background styles but do not change the card’s size or position, preserving the grid’s strict alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlight-controller is not a standalone visual block; instead, it modifies the appearance of individual service-card components within the services-list grid. When a card is hovered, focused, or programmatically highlighted (for example, when matching a selected service from the sidebar), the highlight-controller applies a consistent visual emphasis: the card’s border becomes slightly thicker and uses the primary accent color, its background lightens subtly, and a modest shadow or outline appears. These changes remain constrained inside the existing card footprint, so the card’s outer dimensions and its position in the grid remain fixed and aligned with neighboring cards. The effect is easily noticeable when scanning rows left-to-right and top-to-bottom, guiding attention to the active or relevant service without disrupting the structured, utilitarian layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-spatial, state-based overlay behavior applied to service-card elements in place. It does not create new layout regions or shift nearby elements; it only alters visual styling within the existing card boundaries.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-error-state occupies the same space where the services-list grid would normally appear in the right main content column. It is positioned approximately 32px below the Services Overview heading and description, centered horizontally within the column. The error block has a maximum width of about 400–480px to keep the message readable and compact; it is centered so there is equal left and right whitespace between it and the main column edges. Inside this block, an error icon or title sits at the top, followed by one or two lines of explanatory text, and then a retry button, all stacked with 8–16px vertical spacing. There is at least 24–32px of padding around the content within the error container so it feels like a distinct card. The entire error state maintains the same vertical footprint that the top portion of the services grid would have, so the overall page balance—hero at top, divider, heading and description in the upper right, and a visually significant content area below—remains intact even when services fail to load.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block replacing the services-list region in the right main column whenever an error occurs. It does not overlay other content; instead, it is inserted into the normal document flow in the grid’s place, preserving the two-column overall structure.

name: Service Detail
title: Service Detail
