description: Displays detailed information for a selected service and provides a service-specific request/quote form, driven by URL parameters and backend interfaces.

core_features: Load and display detailed information for the selected service based on the `serviceId` URL parameter using `getServiceDetails`.
Render service-specific descriptive content (names, descriptions, supported regions/materials) from the `Service` entity.

Render service-specific controls and guidance: for example, options for laser cutting (materials, units, tolerance types) via `getLaserCuttingOptions`, or regions/priorities for emergency repair via `getEmergencyRepairOptions`.
Provide a prominent call-to-action that scrolls or focuses to the relevant embedded form section on the same page, with behavior influenced by the `action` URL parameter when present.

Provide a laser cutting quote form bound to the `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest` model and submitted via `submitLaserCuttingQuoteRequest`, including material, dimensions, tolerance, quantity, shipping location, and contact details.

Provide an emergency repair request form bound to the `EmergencyRepairRequest` model and submitted via `submitEmergencyRepairRequest`, including region, priority, issue description, site address, and contact details.
Validate required inputs for each form before submission and show submission results (success/failure) using the response from the corresponding submit interface.
Support direct deep-linking into the quote/request form sections using the `action` URL parameter without relying on any client-side storage.

user_workflows.name: View service details from Services overview
steps: User clicks a service in `services.html` which links to `service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}` (optionally also including `&action={action}`).
The Service Detail page reads `serviceId` from the URL and initializes the `service-detail-loader` component.
The page calls `getServiceDetails(serviceId)` to retrieve the associated `Service` record.
When the `Service` data is returned, the page displays the service name and descriptions in the summary and detail components.
If the service supports regions or materials (according to the `Service` fields), these are listed or explained in the detail content.
If the service has a quote or booking form (according to `Service.has_quote_form` or `Service.has_booking_form`), the relevant call-to-action button is shown in the summary section.

user_workflows.name: Request a laser cutting quote
steps: User navigates to `service_detail.html?serviceId=laser_cutting` from another page or a direct link.
The page loads service details using `getServiceDetails('laser_cutting')`.
The `service-detail-loader` identifies the service as laser cutting and initializes `laser-cutting-options-loader`, which calls `getLaserCuttingOptions()`.
When the user clicks the call-to-action (or the URL includes `action=quote`), the page scrolls to and focuses the `laser-cutting-quote-form`.
The `laser-cutting-quote-form` populates material, dimension unit, and tolerance selections from `getLaserCuttingOptions()` and renders all required fields for a `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest`.
The user enters material, thickness, dimensions, quantity, tolerance, shipping location, any additional notes, and contact information.
On form submission, the component validates required fields. If validation passes, it calls `submitLaserCuttingQuoteRequest` with the user-entered values.

On a successful response, the form displays confirmation using the returned `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest` and `message`. In case of an error (e.g., interface call fails), an error state/message is shown and the user can adjust inputs and resubmit.

user_workflows.name: Request an emergency repair service
steps: User navigates to `service_detail.html?serviceId=emergency_repair` (optionally with `&action=request`) from `services.html`, `index.html`, or a direct link.
The page calls `getServiceDetails('emergency_repair')` and displays the service description and any supported region information provided in the `Service` record.
The `service-detail-loader` identifies the service as emergency repair and initializes `emergency-repair-options-loader`, which calls `getEmergencyRepairOptions()`.
If `action=request` is in the URL or the user clicks the call-to-action button, the page scrolls to and focuses the `emergency-repair-request-form`.

The `emergency-repair-request-form` renders dropdowns for region and priority using the `regions` and `priorities` arrays from `getEmergencyRepairOptions()`, plus fields for issue description, site address, and contact details.
The user selects a region and priority, describes the issue, enters site address and contact details.
On form submission, required fields are validated. If valid, the form calls `submitEmergencyRepairRequest` with the entered values.
On success, the response `EmergencyRepairRequest` and `message` are used to show confirmation (including current status). On failure, the user sees an error state and can modify inputs and retry.

user_workflows.name: Use URL `action` parameter to deep-link into forms
steps: User arrives at `service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}&action={action}` from a marketing email or another page.

The page loads service details via `getServiceDetails`. Once the service type is known and the relevant form component is initialized, the `service-action-focus-manager` interprets the `action` parameter.
If the service is laser cutting and `action` indicates a quote, the page automatically scrolls to and focuses the laser cutting quote form.
If the service is emergency repair and `action` indicates a request, the page automatically scrolls to and focuses the emergency repair request form.
If the `action` parameter does not match the available form for the service, the page displays only the service information while keeping the default scroll position.

user_workflows.name: Navigate back to all services
steps: User clicks the 'Back to all services' link in the breadcrumb/navigation component.
The page navigates to `services.html` without relying on any stored client state.
The user can then select another service, which again routes to `service_detail.html?serviceId={serviceId}` with the new identifier.

interactions.name: Service selection via URL
interactions.details: When the page loads or the URL changes, `service-detail-loader` reads the `serviceId` parameter and triggers `getServiceDetails(serviceId)`. If `serviceId` is missing or if the service cannot be retrieved, the component shows an error state instead of the normal content.

interactions.name: Call-to-action click
interactions.details: In the `service-summary` component, the CTA button is rendered whenever the loaded `Service` indicates that a quote or booking form is available. Clicking this button calls a handler that uses in-page anchors or programmatic scrolling to bring the relevant form into view and focuses the first required input field.

interactions.name: Laser cutting form field interactions
interactions.details: In `laser-cutting-quote-form`, selecting material, units, or tolerance type updates the internal form state. Options for these fields come directly from `getLaserCuttingOptions()`. Validation runs on blur or change for required fields and again on submission, preventing submission until required fields are populated.

interactions.name: Emergency repair form field interactions
interactions.details: In `emergency-repair-request-form`, region and priority dropdowns are populated from `getEmergencyRepairOptions()`. Changing region or priority updates the internal form state and may adjust contextual helper text (e.g., priority descriptions from the API). Validation ensures mandatory fields such as region, priority, issue description, site address, and contact details are set before allowing submission.

interactions.name: Form submission and feedback
interactions.details: On submission of either form, a loading state is shown and the relevant submit interface is called. On success, the `form-submission-status` component displays the confirmation returned in the `message` field and may expose key fields from the created request (such as the current `status`). On failure, an error state is shown and the user can modify inputs and resubmit.

interactions.name: Action-based form focusing
interactions.details: The `service-action-focus-manager` monitors the `action` URL parameter and the readiness of the relevant form. Once both the service details and the required options are loaded, it triggers scrolling/focusing logic so that users arriving with an `action` parameter land directly at the appropriate form.

page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state is controlled through the URL parameters `serviceId` and `action`. On initial load and on any change to these URL parameters (e.g., via navigation), the `service-detail-loader` reads `serviceId`. If `serviceId` is present, it calls `getServiceDetails(serviceId)` and stores the returned `Service` object in component state. The type of service (`Service.service_id`) controls which additional loaders and forms are rendered: if it represents laser cutting, `laser-cutting-options-loader` is initialized and `getLaserCuttingOptions()` is called; if it represents emergency repair, `emergency-repair-options-loader` is initialized and `getEmergencyRepairOptions()` is called. The `action` parameter is read by `service-action-focus-manager` after service data and any required option data are available. If `action` indicates a quote for a service that has a quote form, the manager scrolls to and focuses the quote form; if it indicates a request for a service that has a request form, it scrolls to that form. Internal component state (such as current form field values, validation errors, loading and submission status) is kept only in memory within the relevant components and is not persisted via URL or any storage. No static data such as options lists or region names are hardcoded; all selectable options and behavioral flags are derived from the `Service` entity and from the `getLaserCuttingOptions` or `getEmergencyRepairOptions` responses, ensuring the page behavior fully reflects backend configuration.

components.functionality: Reads `serviceId` (and optionally `action`) from the URL, calls `getServiceDetails(serviceId)`, manages loading/error states, and exposes the loaded `Service` data to child components. Based on `Service.service_id`, it conditionally initializes loaders and forms specific to that service type.
event_handlers: onInit: Triggered when the page is first loaded; reads URL parameters and calls `getServiceDetails`.
onUrlParamChange: Triggered when `serviceId` changes in the URL; re-fetches service details and reinitializes dependent components.
onServiceLoaded: Fires when `getServiceDetails` successfully returns; notifies dependent components (forms, options loaders, focus manager).
onServiceLoadError: Fires when `getServiceDetails` fails; updates a shared error state to display an error message instead of service content.

components.functionality: Displays a simple breadcrumb or back-link allowing users to return to the services overview page.
event_handlers: onClickBack: Navigates the browser to `services.html` when the 'Back to all services' link is clicked.

components.functionality: Shows high-level service information such as the service name and short description retrieved from the `Service` entity, and displays a context-appropriate call-to-action button that scrolls/focuses to the embedded form if the service supports a quote or booking form.
event_handlers: onClickCTA: When clicked, determines the appropriate form section based on `Service.service_id` and signals the `service-action-focus-manager` (or uses an anchor) to scroll/focus the relevant form.

components.functionality: Renders extended descriptive content and any service metadata such as supported regions or materials, using fields from the `Service` entity. It does not define options itself but displays data as provided by the backend.

When the loaded service represents laser cutting, this component calls `getLaserCuttingOptions()` to retrieve supported materials, units, and tolerance types/units. It exposes these as option lists for the laser cutting quote form.
event_handlers: onServiceTypeDetected: Triggered by `service-detail-loader` when `Service.service_id` equals the laser cutting type; initiates the call to `getLaserCuttingOptions`.
onOptionsLoaded: Fired when option metadata is successfully retrieved; makes data available to `laser-cutting-quote-form`.
onOptionsLoadError: Fired when the options interface fails; communicates an error state to the quote form so it can disable submission and display a relevant message.

components.functionality: Renders and manages the laser cutting quote request form when the current service is laser cutting. It uses option metadata from `laser-cutting-options-loader` and binds user input to fields of the `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest` model. On submission, it calls `submitLaserCuttingQuoteRequest`.
event_handlers: onInit: Initializes form state when the service type is laser cutting and options become available.

onFieldChange: Updates internal state for each field (material, thickness, thickness_unit, length, length_unit, width, width_unit, quantity, tolerance_type, tolerance_value, tolerance_unit, shipping_zip, shipping_country, additional_notes, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone) as the user types or selects values.
onValidate: Runs validation rules for required fields based on the `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest` schema before submission; prevents submission and highlights issues if validation fails.
onSubmit: If validation passes, calls `submitLaserCuttingQuoteRequest` with the current form values and enters a loading state while awaiting the response.

onSubmitSuccess: Receives the created `LaserCuttingQuoteRequest` and `message` from the interface, passes them to `form-submission-status`, optionally resets or locks the form, and exits the loading state.

onSubmitError: Handles submission errors (e.g., network or validation errors from backend), displays an error state via `form-submission-status`, and exits the loading state so the user can adjust inputs and retry.

components.functionality: When the loaded service represents emergency repair, this component calls `getEmergencyRepairOptions()` to retrieve regions and priorities as defined by the backend. It provides these option lists to the emergency repair request form.
event_handlers: onServiceTypeDetected: Triggered by `service-detail-loader` when `Service.service_id` equals the emergency repair type; initiates the call to `getEmergencyRepairOptions`.
onOptionsLoaded: Fired when available regions and priorities are successfully retrieved; exposes them to `emergency-repair-request-form` for use in dropdowns and helper texts.
onOptionsLoadError: Fired when the options interface fails; propagates an error state so the request form can disable submission and display a relevant message.

components.functionality: Renders and manages the emergency repair request form for the emergency repair service. It uses regions and priorities from `emergency-repair-options-loader` and maps user input to the `EmergencyRepairRequest` model fields. On submission, it calls `submitEmergencyRepairRequest`.
event_handlers: onInit: Initializes form state when the service type is emergency repair and the options data is available.

onFieldChange: Updates internal state for each field (region, priority, issue_description, site_street, site_city, site_state, site_zip, site_country, contact_name, contact_email, contact_phone) as the user interacts with the form.
onValidate: Runs validation for required fields based on the `EmergencyRepairRequest` schema before submission, preventing submission if required fields are missing or invalid.
onSubmit: If validation passes, calls `submitEmergencyRepairRequest` with the current form values and sets a loading state while awaiting the response.
onSubmitSuccess: Receives the created `EmergencyRepairRequest` and `message`, forwards them to `form-submission-status`, optionally locks or resets the form, and clears the loading state.
onSubmitError: Handles errors from the submit interface, updates `form-submission-status` with an error message, and clears the loading state for corrections and resubmission.

components.functionality: Coordinates deep-linking and focus behavior using the `action` URL parameter. Once the relevant service data and any required options are loaded, it scrolls to and focuses the appropriate form (quote or request) when applicable.
event_handlers: onUrlParamChange: Reads `action` from the URL whenever it changes and stores the desired action intent.

onServiceAndOptionsReady: Triggered when both the `Service` and any service-specific options are fully loaded; decides whether to scroll/focus the laser cutting quote form or the emergency repair request form based on `Service.service_id` and the `action` value.
onCTAInvoked: Listens for CTA click events from `service-summary` and triggers the same scroll/focus behavior as if the matching `action` parameter was set.

components.functionality: Displays submission feedback for either the laser cutting quote form or the emergency repair request form. It shows success confirmations or errors using the `message` and relevant parts of the returned request objects.
event_handlers: onSubmissionResult: Listens for success or error events from both forms and updates its state to display the appropriate confirmation or error details.
onDismiss: Allows the user to dismiss the status message, clearing the component state while preserving the underlying request data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page content sits entirely below an existing full‑width industrial hero and gray divider bar. Under that divider, the page uses a two‑column content band: a narrow left sidebar (global site navigation, handled elsewhere) and a wider right main content column that holds all Service Detail components. Within the right column, the flow is strictly top‑to‑bottom: breadcrumb navigation at the very top of the column, followed by the service summary as the primary page heading block, then the detailed service content, and finally the service‑specific quote/request form area. Data loaders and the focus manager are non‑visual and either overlay their target region with a simple loading state or do not affect layout at all.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page content sits entirely below an existing full‑width industrial hero and gray divider bar. Under that divider, the page uses a two‑column content band: a narrow left sidebar (global site navigation, handled elsewhere) and a wider right main content column that holds all Service Detail components. Within the right column, the flow is strictly top‑to‑bottom: breadcrumb navigation at the very top of the column, followed by the service summary as the primary page heading block, then the detailed service content, and finally the service‑specific quote/request form area. Data loaders and the focus manager are non‑visual and either overlay their target region with a simple loading state or do not affect layout at all.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central content container is constrained to roughly 1040–1200px and centered on the page. The left sidebar occupies about 20–25% of this width, and the right main column occupies about 75–80%. Inside the main column, the breadcrumb navigation uses minimal vertical height; the service summary and service detail content together take up the upper half of the initial viewport of the content area, and the lower portion of the column is dedicated to forms and feedback. Vertical spacing between these blocks follows the 8px scale, mainly using 24px–32px gaps between major sections and 16px between related items inside a section.
layout_strategies.content_density: The right column is moderately dense, echoing the mockup’s functional corporate feel. Text blocks (breadcrumb, summary, details) are reasonably tight, with 16px–24px internal padding and 24px–32px separation between sections, avoiding large, editorial whitespace. The forms are somewhat denser than the text sections, stacking fields with 8px–16px vertical gaps, and using 24px–32px internal padding for their container. Loaders and status messages appear inline in the same dense rhythm, without adding large empty spaces.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention follows the F/Z pattern from the mockup: the eye arrives from the hero at the top left of the content band, landing first on the breadcrumb navigation, then moves down to the bold service summary heading as the main anchor. From there it flows down through the service detail text, and finally into the lower form area where call‑to‑action elements stand out. The left sidebar remains a vertical anchor but is visually quieter than the right column’s heading and form CTAs. When a form is submitted, the feedback/status message appears immediately above or within the form, momentarily becoming the first element encountered when the user’s gaze returns to the form area.

layout.overall_description: Below the full‑width industrial hero and strong horizontal divider, the page resolves into a centered two‑column band: a narrow left sidebar for site navigation and a dominant right main content column. At the top of the right column, a slim breadcrumb row runs horizontally, hugging the top edge of the content area. Directly beneath it, the service summary block presents a large, bold service title and key information, acting as the visual anchor at the top of the content band. Below that, a vertically stacked service detail section provides descriptive text and supporting details in a moderately dense, utilitarian layout. The lower portion of the right column is reserved for the service‑specific quote or request form, styled as a clear card‑like section with tight but readable spacing, aligned to the same left margin as the text above. Any loading states briefly occupy the same regions where their respective content will appear, and submission feedback appears immediately above or inside the relevant form. Everything scrolls together in a straightforward top‑to‑bottom flow, maintaining the practical, slightly old‑school corporate feel of the original mockup while clearly guiding the user from understanding the service to taking action.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-detail-loader is logically positioned at the very top of the right main content column, just below the page’s gray divider bar and above the breadcrumb navigation; when active, it temporarily occupies the vertical space where the breadcrumb, service summary, and detail content will appear, spanning the full width of the main column (approximately 75–80% of the total content width) with a compact vertical height (for example a single line skeleton or spinner centered vertically within 48–80px of height) and 24px top and bottom margins so it feels integrated into the column’s rhythm; when inactive, it takes no visible space and does not disrupt the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; when active it replaces the top content stack in the main column, when inactive it collapses and yields its space to subsequent components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-breadcrumb-nav sits at the top of the right main content column, directly under the gray divider bar (or under the service-detail-loader if that is showing), aligned flush with the left edge of the main column and spanning its full width; it occupies a relatively shallow horizontal strip—roughly 40–56px tall—with 16px–24px vertical padding, and has a 24px gap below it before the service summary begins, giving it clear separation without dominating the page; its text is left-aligned and flows horizontally, following the initial left-to-right eye movement before attention drops down to the main service heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the main column; it does not stick or float, simply maintaining its position as the first visible content element at the top of the column when the user is at the start of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-summary block is placed immediately below the breadcrumb navigation, separated by about 24px of vertical space, and spans the full width of the right main content column; it contains the primary service title in a large, bold type centered on the column’s left alignment, with any supporting meta information or key highlights arranged below it in a vertical stack or tight grid, all contained within a rectangle that has about 24px–32px internal padding on all sides; the block’s height naturally expands with content but visually feels like a strong header section occupying roughly the upper quarter of the visible main column area, and there is a clear 32px gap below it before the detailed service content begins, anchoring the user’s gaze before they move down into the denser details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column, forming the primary visual anchor for the page; it scrolls normally with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-detail-content section sits directly below the service summary, separated by approximately 32px of vertical space, and continues to use the full width of the right main column; its text and any inline media are left-aligned to match the service-summary, with internal padding of about 16px–24px and paragraphs stacked with 16px vertical spacing, giving a moderately dense reading experience; this section extends down as needed, becoming the central informational band of the page, and finishes with a 32px–40px vertical gap before the first service-specific form block, visually transitioning the user from reading about the service into taking action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and fully integrated into the document flow; it expands vertically with content and pushes the form section further down while maintaining consistent left/right margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When active for a laser-cutting service, the laser-cutting-options-loader appears in the lower portion of the right main content column, directly above the laser-cutting-quote-form and below the service-detail-content, occupying the same horizontal width as the main column; it takes the form of a compact loading strip or small skeleton state roughly 48–72px tall with centered feedback, and is separated from the detail content above by about 24px and from the form below by about 16px, so it feels like a brief preparatory step before the form fields; when inactive, it collapses completely and the form shifts up to sit closer to the service details.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline; appears only when data is loading for the laser-cutting form and otherwise occupies no space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The laser-cutting-quote-form lives in the lower part of the right main content column, directly beneath the service-detail-content (or beneath the laser-cutting-options-loader if it is visible), and is styled as a card-like block that spans roughly 80–90% of the main column’s width, centered on the column’s left alignment with equal left/right padding so its edges align with the text above; the form has generous internal padding of about 24px–32px on all sides, with fields stacked vertically using 8px–16px gaps, and any section labels or subtitles appearing at the top with 16px spacing before the first field; the whole form block is separated from the content above by about 32px–40px and from any content or forms below by at least 32px, making it a clearly defined action area that occupies most of the lower viewport once the user scrolls to it.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main column; it scrolls into view below the service details and remains in its position, expanding vertically as form fields or validation messages appear.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency-repair-options-loader is positioned in the same lower main-column region as the laser-cutting loader, but specifically tied to the emergency repair context; when active, it appears directly above the emergency-repair-request-form and below the service-detail-content (or below other form blocks if both types of forms are present), spanning the full width of the right column in a compact 48–72px-tall strip with centered loading feedback and 24px spacing from the content above, providing a visual cue that emergency repair options are being prepared before the user can fill out the form; when inactive, it collapses and leaves no gap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline, only occupying space while loading emergency repair options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The emergency-repair-request-form sits in the lower section of the right main content column, immediately beneath the emergency-repair-options-loader if present, or directly under the service-detail-content if this is the only form for the service; it mirrors the laser-cutting form’s card-like treatment, spanning about 80–90% of the main column’s width with 24px–32px of internal padding, and fields stacked vertically with 8px–16px gaps; if both the laser-cutting and emergency repair forms are present, they are stacked one after another with a 32px–40px vertical gap between them, creating a clear separation between the two action types while preserving a consistent left alignment and width; the form occupies the lower viewport area and becomes the primary focus once the user scrolls past the descriptive content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow; it expands vertically with form content and potential validation feedback, pushing subsequent elements (such as global feedback or footer content) further down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-action-focus-manager has no direct visual presence in the layout; it operates behind the scenes within the right main content column, programmatically shifting focus to key elements such as the active form heading or the form-submission-status region without occupying any physical space or altering the spacing between components.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; it does not consume layout space and does not affect the document flow, only adjusting focus and scroll position as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The form-submission-status component appears inline within the right main content column, closely associated with whichever form was just submitted; visually, it is a horizontal notification band that spans the full width of the active form (about 80–90% of the main column width), positioned either immediately above the form’s title area or flush at the top inside the form container, with 12px–16px vertical padding and a 16px gap to the adjacent form content; there is a 24px–32px space between this status band and any content above it (such as the end of the service-detail-content), ensuring that it reads as part of the form context rather than as general page content; on success or error, it becomes momentarily prominent in the lower viewport while preserving the compact, utilitarian spacing characteristic of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline; it appears and disappears within the form’s vertical space without shifting the broader page structure, simply expanding the vertical height of the form area when visible.

name: Product Configurator
title: Product Configurator
description: Configure a metal cabinet (dimensions, color, shelves, locking, and optional features), see real-time pricing, and add the configured unit to the cart.

core_features: Select a base cabinet product to configure, using data from getCabinetBaseProducts or the baseProductId URL parameter.
Load allowed configuration ranges and options for the selected base cabinet via getCabinetConfigurationOptions.
Provide a configuration form for height, width, depth, color, number of shelves, lockable door, and optional features, constrained by the options returned from getCabinetConfigurationOptions.
Preview and validate a cabinet configuration in real time using previewCabinetConfiguration, showing whether the configuration is valid, any validation errors, and the estimated unit price.
Allow the user to set an optional target unit-price budget and highlight when the estimated price from previewCabinetConfiguration exceeds this budget to help the user adjust options.
Create a configured cabinet instance and add it to the cart in one action via createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart, with quantity control and feedback.
Provide navigation to view the shopping cart (cart.html) and to return to the cabinet listing (products.html?category=cabinets).
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Configure from a specific cabinet product (baseProductId provided):
1. User opens configurator.html with a baseProductId URL parameter.
2. Page reads baseProductId from the URL.
3. Page optionally calls getCabinetBaseProducts to populate a base-cabinet selector but preselects the product matching baseProductId.
4. Page calls getCabinetConfigurationOptions with baseProductId to obtain allowed dimension ranges, available colors, shelf options, lock availability, and optional features.
5. Configuration form initializes its fields (units, allowed ranges, selectable options) based on the response from getCabinetConfigurationOptions and any configuration values present in URL parameters.
6. When the user adjusts any configuration field (dimensions, color, shelves, lock, optional features), the page calls previewCabinetConfiguration with the current configuration.
7. The preview result updates the estimated unit price, validation status, and configuration summary; validation messages are shown if configuration_valid is false.
8. Optionally, the user enters a desired budget limit; the page compares the estimated_unit_price from previewCabinetConfiguration with this limit and visually indicates if the configuration exceeds the budget.
9. Once the configuration is valid and acceptable to the user, they enter a quantity and submit the Add to Cart form.
10. The page calls createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart with the selected configuration parameters and quantity.
11. On success, the page updates any in-page cart indicator (if present in global layout) and shows a confirmation with options to stay on the configurator or go to cart.html.

Workflow 2 – Start from configurator without a preselected product (no baseProductId):
1. User opens configurator.html without a baseProductId URL parameter.
2. Page calls getCabinetBaseProducts and displays a list or dropdown of configurable cabinet base products.
3. User selects a base cabinet; the page updates the URL with baseProductId and resets any configuration-related URL parameters.
4. The page calls getCabinetConfigurationOptions with the chosen baseProductId.
5. Steps 5–11 from Workflow 1 apply: user configures, previews via previewCabinetConfiguration, optionally adds a budget, and finally uses createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart to add the configuration to the cart.

Workflow 3 – Resume or share a configuration via URL parameters:
1. User returns to a previously shared or bookmarked configurator.html URL that includes baseProductId and some configuration-related parameters (for example, dimension values, color, shelves, lock, selected optional features, quantity, and budget).
2. Page reads baseProductId and other configuration parameters from the URL on load.
3. Page calls getCabinetConfigurationOptions with baseProductId and initializes the configuration form with the provided URL values, ensuring they are clamped or validated against the allowed ranges/options.
4. After the configuration form is initialized, the page automatically calls previewCabinetConfiguration with the current configuration state.
5. The user sees the restored configuration, real-time pricing, and budget indication, and can further adjust the configuration or proceed directly to Add to Cart using createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart.
interactions: Base product selection: User chooses a base cabinet from a list or dropdown, triggering a call to getCabinetBaseProducts on page load (if needed) and getCabinetConfigurationOptions when a base product is selected or when baseProductId changes.

Configuration editing: User inputs or changes height, width, depth (within the min/max from getCabinetConfigurationOptions), selects a color from available_colors, selects shelves_count from shelf_count_options, toggles has_lockable_door when lockable_door_available is true, and selects or deselects optional_features based on the options returned.

Real-time preview: On any configuration change, a debounced call to previewCabinetConfiguration runs; the UI updates configuration_valid, validation_errors, estimated_unit_price, currency, and configuration_summary.

Budget control: User enters or edits an optional budget limit; the component compares this value with estimated_unit_price and clearly indicates whether the current configuration meets or exceeds the budget.

Add to cart: User sets quantity (respecting any constraints derived from the selected base Product, such as min_order_quantity and max_order_quantity if available from getCabinetBaseProducts) and submits the Add to Cart form; this calls createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart and, on success, offers a link or button to go to cart.html.
Navigation: User can click a 'Back to cabinets' control that navigates to products.html?category=cabinets, or a 'View cart'/'Go to cart' control that navigates to cart.html.

URL-based state updates: When the user selects a base product or changes configuration fields or budget/quantity, the page updates corresponding URL parameters so that the current state is represented in the URL and can be reloaded or shared.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page uses URL parameters as the single source of persistent page state and does not rely on localStorage. Key parameters include: baseProductId for the selected base cabinet; dimension-related parameters for the current configuration (for example, h, hUnit, w, wUnit, d, dUnit); colorCode for the selected color; shelvesCount for number of shelves; lock for lockable door (such as lock=true/false); options for selected optional feature codes (for example, a comma-separated list); qty for the desired quantity; and budget for the optional target unit price. On page load, the state manager component parses all relevant URL parameters. If baseProductId is present, it initiates getCabinetConfigurationOptions and pre-fills the configuration form with values from the URL (subject to validation against the allowed ranges/options). If baseProductId is absent, the page calls getCabinetBaseProducts and waits for the user to select a base product; when they do, baseProductId is written to the URL and the configuration is reset. After configuration state is initialized, the page automatically calls previewCabinetConfiguration with the current configuration values from the form (which reflect the URL state). As the user changes configuration fields, budget, or quantity, event handlers update both the in-memory state and the URL query string (using history.pushState or similar) so that the URL always matches the current state. The decision to show the base product selection view vs. the configuration view is driven by whether a valid baseProductId is present in the URL and whether getCabinetConfigurationOptions has successfully returned for that ID.

components.functionality: Loads and presents all configurable cabinet base products and manages selection of the base cabinet. When no baseProductId is present in the URL at load time, this component shows the list or dropdown of cabinets. When baseProductId is present, it uses the list to highlight the selected cabinet and allows the user to change it if desired.
event_handlers: onInit: If baseProductId is not present in the URL, call getCabinetBaseProducts and render the selectable list of cabinet base products.

onInitWithBaseProductId: If baseProductId is present, call getCabinetBaseProducts to populate the selector, preselect the matching Product, and then emit a 'baseProductSelected' event for downstream components.

onBaseProductChange: When the user selects a different base cabinet, update the baseProductId URL parameter, reset configuration-related URL parameters, and emit a 'baseProductSelected' event with the chosen Product.

onBaseProductsLoaded: After getCabinetBaseProducts returns, cache the products in memory so other components (such as quantity handling) can read Product-level constraints like min_order_quantity and max_order_quantity.

components.functionality: Fetches configuration constraints and options for the selected base cabinet using getCabinetConfigurationOptions and provides them to the configuration form and related components.
event_handlers: onBaseProductSelected: When base-product-selector emits a selection, call getCabinetConfigurationOptions with the selected baseProductId.

onOptionsLoaded: After getCabinetConfigurationOptions returns, provide the height, width, depth ranges, units, available_colors, shelf_count_options, lockable_door_available, and optional_features to the configuration form; also trigger initial configuration hydration from URL parameters.
onReloadRequested: If the user explicitly changes the baseProductId via URL or page reload, re-call getCabinetConfigurationOptions for the new baseProductId.

components.functionality: Provides input controls for all configurable cabinet parameters (dimensions, color, shelves, lock, optional features). It enforces ranges and available options from configuration-options-loader and keeps the in-memory configuration in sync with the URL and the preview component.
event_handlers: onInitFromOptions: After configuration-options-loader provides ranges and options, initialize form controls with units and allowed values and, if present, prefill with values from URL parameters (clamping or correcting values that are outside allowed ranges).

onHeightChange: When the user edits height, validate against min/max from getCabinetConfigurationOptions, update the internal configuration state and corresponding URL parameters, and emit a 'configurationChanged' event.
onWidthChange: Same as onHeightChange but for width.
onDepthChange: Same as onHeightChange but for depth.
onColorChange: When the user selects a color, update color_code (and any related display name if needed) in the configuration state and URL, then emit 'configurationChanged'.
onShelvesChange: When shelves_count is changed, ensure the value is one of the shelf_count_options, update state and URL, and emit 'configurationChanged'.
onLockToggle: When the lockable door checkbox is toggled (if lockable_door_available is true), update has_lockable_door in state and URL and emit 'configurationChanged'.
onOptionalFeatureToggle: When an optional feature is selected or deselected, update the list of selected optional_feature_codes in state and URL and emit 'configurationChanged'.

onResetConfiguration: Provide a way to reset the configuration to a neutral or API-defined default state by clearing configuration-related URL parameters and reinitializing from configuration-options-loader.

components.functionality: Consumes the current configuration state and calls previewCabinetConfiguration to obtain validation, pricing, and a configuration summary. Displays estimated unit price, currency, validity, and any validation errors.
event_handlers: onConfigurationChanged: When cabinet-configuration-form emits a configuration change, debounce and then call previewCabinetConfiguration with baseProductId and the current configuration parameters (height, width, depth with units, color_code, shelves_count, has_lockable_door, optional_feature_codes).

onPreviewResponse: When previewCabinetConfiguration returns, update the in-memory configuration preview state with configuration_valid, validation_errors, estimated_unit_price, currency, and configuration_summary, and emit a 'previewUpdated' event.
onForcePreview: Allow the user to manually trigger a preview (e.g., via a 'Recalculate' button) which directly calls previewCabinetConfiguration without waiting for debounce.
onInvalidConfiguration: If configuration_valid is false, surface validation_errors to the validation-messages-panel and disable or warn on the Add to Cart action.

components.functionality: Allows the user to specify an optional target unit-price budget and compares it to the estimated price from configuration-preview-panel to indicate whether the current configuration is within budget.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Read the budget parameter from the URL on load, if present, and initialize the budget input field accordingly.
onBudgetChange: When the user changes the budget value, validate the input, update the budget URL parameter, and emit a 'budgetChanged' event.

onPreviewUpdated: When configuration-preview-panel emits a previewUpdated event, compare estimated_unit_price to the current budget value (if any) and update the visual status (for example, a warning state when estimated_unit_price exceeds the budget).

components.functionality: Displays validation errors returned by previewCabinetConfiguration and contextual hints to guide the user to adjust the configuration.
event_handlers: onPreviewUpdated: When configuration-preview-panel emits previewUpdated with validation_errors, display the errors in a list and optionally link them to the corresponding fields in cabinet-configuration-form.
onFieldFocus: When the user focuses a configuration field associated with a known validation error, highlight or scroll the relevant message for clarity.

components.functionality: Handles quantity input and submits the configured cabinet to the cart using createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart. It is enabled only when the current configuration is valid based on the latest previewCabinetConfiguration result.
event_handlers: onInitFromURL: Read the qty parameter from the URL (if present) and initialize the quantity field, applying constraints derived from the selected base Product (min_order_quantity, max_order_quantity) if available in the Product data loaded by base-product-selector.
onQuantityChange: When the user changes the quantity, validate against any known min/max constraints, update the qty URL parameter, and enable/disable submission accordingly.
onPreviewUpdated: When configuration-preview-panel reports configuration_valid=false, disable the Add to Cart button and show a message requiring correction; enable it when configuration_valid=true.

onSubmit: When the user submits the form, call createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart with baseProductId, all current configuration parameters (height_value, height_unit, width_value, width_unit, depth_value, depth_unit, color_code, color_name if selected from available colors, shelves_count, has_lockable_door, selected optional_feature_codes), and quantity.

onAddToCartSuccess: When createConfiguredCabinetAndAddToCart returns configured_product, cart, and cart_item, display a confirmation message and emit a 'cartUpdated' event which other global layout components can consume; offer actions to stay on configurator.html or navigate to cart.html.
onAddToCartError: If the API call fails or returns errors, display appropriate feedback and keep the configuration state intact so the user can try again.

components.functionality: Shows key information about the currently selected base cabinet (e.g., name, material, size_label, and any relevant notes) to give context while configuring.
event_handlers: onBaseProductSelected: When a base Product is selected, pull its attributes from the list loaded by base-product-selector and render a concise textual summary.
onConfigurationChanged: Optionally, append a short textual representation of the current configuration (using configuration_summary from previewCabinetConfiguration when available).

components.functionality: Provides functional navigation controls to go back to the cabinet listing or view the cart.
event_handlers: onBackToCabinetsClick: Navigate to products.html?category=cabinets without altering any cart or configuration state.
onViewCartClick: Navigate to cart.html, allowing the user to review their cart including the newly added configured cabinet.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL parameters that represent the configurator state, ensuring that all components stay synchronized and that the URL fully represents the current configuration, budget, and quantity.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse URL parameters including baseProductId, dimension values and units, colorCode, shelvesCount, lock, options, qty, and budget; expose this parsed state to other components (base-product-selector, cabinet-configuration-form, budget-control, add-to-cart-form).

onBaseProductChange: When base-product-selector changes the baseProductId, update the URL accordingly and clear configuration-specific parameters to avoid invalid state carry-over between base products.
onConfigurationFieldChange: When cabinet-configuration-form updates any field, update the corresponding URL parameter in a controlled way (e.g., debounced or batched updates).
onBudgetChange: When budget-control updates the budget, write it to the URL.
onQuantityChange: When add-to-cart-form updates quantity, write it to the URL.
onPopState: When the browser back/forward navigation changes the URL, re-parse parameters and broadcast state updates to relevant components so the UI reflects the new URL state.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero at the very top (brand + industrial image + page title), followed immediately by a strong horizontal divider. Below the divider, a centered content container runs the full width but is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main column. The left sidebar holds the navigation-links in a vertical list. The right column holds the configurator experience: at the top, a prominent base-product-summary-header and base-product-selector, followed by a two-column sub-layout where the cabinet-configuration-form sits on the left and the configuration-preview-panel (with budget-control) sits on the right. Beneath these, validation-messages-panel and add-to-cart-form complete the flow in a stacked arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width hero at the very top (brand + industrial image + page title), followed immediately by a strong horizontal divider. Below the divider, a centered content container runs the full width but is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main column. The left sidebar holds the navigation-links in a vertical list. The right column holds the configurator experience: at the top, a prominent base-product-summary-header and base-product-selector, followed by a two-column sub-layout where the cabinet-configuration-form sits on the left and the configuration-preview-panel (with budget-control) sits on the right. Beneath these, validation-messages-panel and add-to-cart-form complete the flow in a stacked arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero block occupies roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height with a full-bleed background image. Below that, the main content container is centered with a maximum width around a typical desktop width (e.g., 1040–1200px) and 24px horizontal padding. Inside this container, the left navigation sidebar takes about 20–25% of the width, while the right main content column uses the remaining 75–80%. Within the main column, the internal two-column split gives roughly 60–65% of the width to the configuration form and 35–40% to the preview/price panel. Vertical spacing between major blocks (hero to divider, section headers to content, form to CTA) follows a 32–48px rhythm, with 16–24px padding inside cards and panels.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero area is visually open: large image, bold centered title, and generous breathing room. Below the divider, density increases to a functional, moderately compact layout. Navigation links in the left sidebar are relatively close vertically with 8–12px gaps. The right column uses stacked sections with clear separation: 24–32px between major components (summary header, selector, form, preview, CTA), and 16–24px between internal elements. The overall effect is utilitarian and efficient rather than airy, aligning with a corporate, slightly old-school template feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Eyes land first on the hero’s centered page title, then move down to the horizontal divider. Below that, attention is pulled to the large heading and summary in the base-product-summary-header in the right column. From there, the gaze moves down to the base-product-selector, then into the main working area where the left-side configuration form establishes the primary reading column. The configuration-preview-panel on the right acts as a secondary focal point in peripheral vision, reinforcing the F/Z pattern as users glance across to see visual and price updates. The left navigation sidebar serves as a vertical anchor for orientation, attracting attention when users look for other sections or want to jump around. The flow ends in the lower right with validation feedback and the add-to-cart-form as the final action point.

layout.overall_description: The page opens with a wide, full-bleed industrial metal fabrication photo spanning edge-to-edge across the top 35–40% of the viewport. Centered over this image is a bold, utilitarian title like “Product Configurator” in a large, high-contrast type, with a simple subtitle line beneath it explaining that users can configure cabinets and see real-time pricing. Below the hero, a solid gray horizontal divider bar runs the full width, clearly separating the hero from the working area. Under this divider, the content sits inside a centered container with modest side margins. This container is split into two columns: on the left, a narrow sidebar holds a vertical list of navigation links for different configurator sections; on the right, a wider main column contains the configurator itself. At the top of the main column, a strong page-level summary header introduces the selected base cabinet and shows key specs, followed by a base product selector. Below, the main column itself is divided into a left sub-column with the configuration form fields and a right sub-column with a compact preview/price panel that stays visible as the user scrolls the form. Beneath this two-column work area, validation messages and the add-to-cart controls are stacked, finishing the page with a clear, linear path from understanding, to configuration, to confirmation, to purchase. The spacing and structure echo a straightforward corporate template: everything is aligned to a simple grid, with moderate gaps and an emphasis on clarity and function over visual flair.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The base-product-selector is placed in the right main content column, directly below the base-product-summary-header and above the detailed cabinet-configuration-form. It spans nearly the full width of the right column (approximately 90–100% of the main column width), with 24px of top margin from the summary header and 24–32px of bottom margin before the form begins. Visually, it sits as a horizontal band or compact card aligned to the right column’s left edge, with 16–24px internal padding and its content (dropdowns or tile options) arranged in one or two rows depending on available width. It does not extend into the left sidebar; it stays fully inside the right column, maintaining the clear separation between navigation (left) and configuration (right).
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally in the document within the right main column; it expands vertically as needed for options but remains constrained to the main column width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration-options-loader has no persistent visible presence but appears contextually over the central working area when loading base product or option data. When active, it overlays the right main column content area that includes the base-product-selector, cabinet-configuration-form, and configuration-preview-panel. The overlay begins just below the horizontal divider and within the right column’s boundaries, with a subtle semi-transparent background. A small spinner or loading indicator is centered horizontally and vertically within this right column region, ensuring it is clearly visible without obscuring the left sidebar navigation-links. When inactive, it is completely hidden and does not occupy space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily positioned as an absolute/overlay layer anchored to the right main content column; appears and disappears over other components without changing their layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cabinet-configuration-form is the dominant element in the right main content column’s working area. It sits directly below the base-product-selector, aligned to the left edge of the right column, and occupies roughly the left 60–65% of the right column’s width. The right 35–40% of that column is reserved for the configuration-preview-panel, creating a clear two-column internal layout. The form starts around 24–32px below the selector and runs downward, forming the main vertical reading path. Form sections (e.g., dimensions, color, shelves, locking, accessories) are stacked vertically with 24–32px separation between section headers and 16–24px between individual field rows. There is a 32px horizontal gap between the rightmost edge of the form and the left edge of the configuration-preview-panel, reinforcing the two-column separation. The bottom of the form stops slightly above the validation-messages-panel, leaving around 24–32px of whitespace before that next block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in normal document flow within the right main column and forms the primary left sub-column of a two-column configuration area; height grows with content and can scroll while the preview panel remains partially in view.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The configuration-preview-panel is positioned to the right of the cabinet-configuration-form within the main content column. It is aligned so its top edge matches, or sits just slightly below (8–16px), the top of the form, establishing a clear visual pair. The panel occupies about 35–40% of the right column’s width and is separated from the form by a consistent 32px horizontal gap. It appears as a card-like block with 16–24px internal padding, showing a cabinet preview image or diagram at the top, followed by current configuration summary, pricing, and key attributes. The height is moderate, roughly matching the top portion of the form so users can see both simultaneously. As the user scrolls down past the form, the panel remains visible near the top of the viewport to keep price and preview in constant view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves like a sticky panel within the right main column: anchored to the right side and becoming sticky to the top of the viewport (with a small 16–24px offset) once the user scrolls past its initial position, while remaining bounded by the height of the main content section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The budget-control is nested inside the configuration-preview-panel as part of the pricing area. Inside that panel, it is positioned below the primary price display and any quick summary text, with about 16–24px of vertical spacing above and below. It spans almost the full internal width of the panel (allowing for standard 16–24px padding from the panel edges), forming a single horizontal row of input elements (for example, a numeric input and helper text) that align with the preview panel’s left edge. It appears in the mid-to-lower section of the panel, so the preview image and headline price remain the first things seen when glancing to the right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained entirely within the configuration-preview-panel and moves with it; its position is fixed relative to the panel’s internal layout and does not independently scroll or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The validation-messages-panel is located in the right main content column directly beneath the cabinet-configuration-form and roughly aligned with the left edge of that form. It uses the same width as the form (about 60–65% of the right column) and sits approximately 24–32px below the last form section, leaving a clear gap so it feels like a separate but related block. When there are messages, it appears as a compact stack of message rows within a lightly bordered or shaded panel, with 8–12px vertical spacing between individual messages, and 16–24px padding inside. The panel’s right edge aligns with the form’s right edge and does not extend into the preview panel’s zone. Its bottom edge sits 24–32px above the add-to-cart-form, ensuring that error or warning messages are visually encountered before the user reaches the call-to-action area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally in the document, appearing and disappearing without shifting the two-column structure above; it expands vertically when multiple messages are shown.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-to-cart-form is the final major element in the right main content column, placed directly below the validation-messages-panel. It begins about 24–32px beneath the messages panel (or beneath the configuration form if there are no messages visible) and is aligned to the same left edge as the form and messages. It spans a medium width—roughly 60–70% of the right column—ensuring that quantity input fields and the primary add-to-cart button sit on a single horizontal line while still leaving some whitespace to the right before the preview panel’s edge. The CTA button is on the right side of this row, visually anchoring the end of the reading flow. The vertical space below the form to the footer or page bottom is kept moderate (32–48px) to clearly mark the end of the configurator section.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in normal document order as the last element in the right main column’s stack; it remains stationary while the preview panel may still be partially visible on the right if the viewport permits.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The base-product-summary-header is the first configurator-specific element the user encounters after the hero and divider. Within the main content container, it sits in the right column directly opposite the top portion of the left sidebar. It is positioned 32–40px below the horizontal divider bar, aligned to the left edge of the right column, and spans the full width of that right column (before it splits into form/preview). The header includes the page-level or base-product title in a large, bold font, with supporting lines summarizing the cabinet’s key dimensions and features underneath, laid out in one or two lines of copy. Internal padding is around 16–24px, and there is 24–32px of space below it before the base-product-selector appears. This header establishes the main focal point of the content area and mirrors the mockup’s large heading plus short descriptive text pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows normally at the top of the right main column, acting as the anchor for all subsequent configurator components; it does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links component occupies the entire left sidebar beneath the hero’s divider. The sidebar begins approximately 32–40px below the divider, with its top aligning roughly with the base-product-summary-header in the right column. The navigation column uses about 20–25% of the total content container width and stretches vertically down the page alongside the main content. Within this sidebar, links are arranged in a simple vertical list with each item taking a full row, left-aligned, and separated by 8–12px of vertical space. Typical section labels (e.g., “Overview”, “Dimensions”, “Doors & Locks”, “Interior & Shelves”, “Accessories”, “Review & Cart”) are stacked to give users section anchors. The sidebar’s right edge is separated from the right main content column by a consistent 32–40px gutter, reinforcing the two-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Configured as a sticky sidebar within the content area: it remains visible near the top-left of the viewport as the user scrolls the right main content column, while respecting the top offset created by the hero and divider; it is fixed within the vertical bounds of the main content section.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager has no visible representation on the page. It operates behind the scenes, synchronizing the current configuration (selected base product, dimensions, options, budget, and other form values) with the URL. Conceptually, it is associated with the right main content column, affecting how the base-product-selector, cabinet-configuration-form, configuration-preview-panel, and add-to-cart-form are initialized and updated, but it does not occupy any visual space.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-spatial; functions purely as logic tied to the page state without affecting layout or occupying any area in the grid.

name: About Us
title: About Us
description: Page that loads and displays dynamic company information (history, mission, certifications, and key statistics) and provides navigation to related facility and factory tour pages.

core_features: Fetch and display structured About Us content (headline, body text, certifications, key statistics) via the getAboutPageContent interface.
Allow users to navigate between logical subsections (overview, certifications, key statistics) using URL parameters to control which section is emphasized.
Provide contextual navigation links from the About Us content to the facilities page (facilities.html) and factory tours page (factory_tours.html).
Support deep-linking into specific parts of the About Us content (e.g., directly to certifications or to the factory tour section) using URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View general company overview
1) User opens about.html without URL parameters.
2) The page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent.
3) Once data is returned, the main overview component displays the headline and body.
4) The default subsection (e.g., overview) is treated as active because no URL parameters override the state.
5) User reads content and can scroll through the page.

Workflow: Focus on certifications section via URL parameter
1) User opens about.html with a URL parameter such as ?section=certifications.
2) The page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent.
3) After the data is loaded, the section state logic reads the section URL parameter.
4) The certifications subsection is marked as active: the view scrolls or focuses on the certifications list component and visually indicates it as the current section (implementation-specific styling).
5) User reviews the list of certifications, each with dynamically loaded name and description.

Workflow: Focus on key statistics via URL parameter
1) User opens about.html with a URL parameter such as ?section=statistics.
2) The page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent.
3) After data is loaded, the state logic detects the requested section value.
4) The statistics component is emphasized (e.g., scrolled into view and flagged as the active subsection).
5) User reviews the key statistics, each showing the label and value returned by getAboutPageContent.

Workflow: Navigate from About Us to Our Facilities
1) User is on about.html with content already loaded via getAboutPageContent.
2) User locates the facilities-related navigation element within the About page (e.g., within the body content or a dedicated related-links component).
3) User clicks the facilities navigation element.
4) The click handler triggers a navigation to facilities.html (optionally preserving or adding URL parameters if needed by facilities.html).
5) User arrives at the facilities page to view detailed information about the company’s physical facilities.

Workflow: Navigate from About Us to Schedule a Factory Tour
1) User is on about.html with content loaded via getAboutPageContent.
2) User locates the factory tour call-to-action element in the About page (e.g., within the overview text or a dedicated tours section).
3) User clicks the tour call-to-action.
4) The click handler navigates to factory_tours.html, optionally with URL parameters to indicate the user originated from about.html.
5) On factory_tours.html, the user can view tour information or submit a tour request.

Workflow: Deep-link directly to tour-related content on About Us
1) User opens about.html with a URL parameter such as ?focus=tour.
2) The page initializes and calls getAboutPageContent.
3) After data is loaded, the state logic reads the focus parameter.
4) The page scrolls or focuses to a tour-related subsection within the main About body (if defined by the implementation) and emphasizes the tour call-to-action.
5) User can then click the tour call-to-action to navigate to factory_tours.html.
interactions:
Interaction: Initial data load
- Trigger: Page load or URL change to about.html.
- Action: Invoke getAboutPageContent without parameters.
- Result: Store the returned object in page state and re-render all bound components (headline, body, certifications, statistics, and related-links components).

Interaction: Section tab or in-page navigation click
- Trigger: User clicks an in-page navigation control (e.g., a tab or link) that represents a subsection such as overview, certifications, or statistics.
- Action: Update the browser URL query parameter (e.g., section=certifications) using history APIs without full page reload, and update internal section state.
- Result: The corresponding subsection component becomes active and is scrolled into view or otherwise emphasized.

Interaction: Click link to Our Facilities
- Trigger: User clicks the dedicated facilities navigation element on the About page.
- Action: Navigate the browser to facilities.html, optionally appending context parameters (e.g., from=about).
- Result: User is taken to the facilities page, leaving the About page.

Interaction: Click call-to-action to schedule a factory tour
- Trigger: User clicks the tour call-to-action element on the About page.
- Action: Navigate the browser to factory_tours.html, optionally appending context parameters (e.g., from=about).
- Result: User is taken to the factory tours page.

Interaction: URL parameter change (manual or via in-page controls)
- Trigger: User changes the URL query string manually, or the page updates it when a subsection is selected.
- Action: A URL/state synchronization handler parses the current query parameters (e.g., section, focus) and updates in-page active section state.
- Result: The matching content section is highlighted or focused without re-calling getAboutPageContent, unless a full page reload occurs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
State is controlled primarily via URL query parameters and the response from getAboutPageContent. The page does not rely on localStorage.

1) Data state:
- On every load of about.html, the page calls getAboutPageContent.
- The returned object is stored in an in-memory state object (e.g., aboutPageState) containing fields such as headline, body, certifications, and key_statistics.
- All components that need About Us data read from this shared state; they do not maintain separate copies.

2) Section state via URL parameters:
- Query parameter `section` determines which logical sub-section of the About page is currently active.
- Expected values are implementation-defined (e.g., overview, certifications, statistics). No values are hardcoded in this specification; the implementation defines valid options.
- On page load or when the URL changes, a state synchronization routine reads `section` and updates a `currentSection` variable in memory.
- Components responsible for layout and navigation use `currentSection` to decide which block to emphasize or scroll into view.

3) Focus state via URL parameters:
- Query parameter `focus` can be used to request emphasis on a specific in-page topic such as tours or facilities. Valid values are implementation-defined.
- After content is loaded, if `focus` is present, the page attempts to locate the matching subsection or call-to-action within the rendered content and scroll or focus to it.
- This does not trigger additional API calls; it only affects in-page behavior.

4) Navigation context via URL parameters:
- When navigating from about.html to facilities.html or factory_tours.html, the page may append context parameters (e.g., `from=about`, or a copy of the current `section`) so target pages can adjust their behavior.
- These parameters are added to the outgoing URL during click handling; their exact names and values are implementation-defined and not hardcoded here.

5) Reload behavior:
- If the user reloads about.html with section or focus parameters in place, the page re-runs getAboutPageContent, restores the data state, and then re-applies section/focus selection based solely on the current URL parameters.

components.functionality: Responsible for calling getAboutPageContent on page initialization, storing the result in shared state, and triggering render updates for dependent components.

Reads URL parameters (e.g., section, focus) and maintains in-memory state for which subsection of the About page is active and/or focused. Notifies content components when the URL-derived state changes.

Displays the main About Us headline using the headline field from getAboutPageContent. Optionally shows an in-page navigation control for switching between subsections (overview, certifications, statistics), which updates the URL section parameter.
Renders the primary body text of the About Us content from the body field in getAboutPageContent. Acts as the default section when no specific section is requested via URL parameters.

Displays a list of the company’s certifications. Each list item is generated from the certifications array returned by getAboutPageContent, showing the dynamically loaded name and description for each certification.
Shows key company statistics from the key_statistics array returned by getAboutPageContent. Each item displays the dynamic label and value fields without hardcoded content.

Provides navigation elements within the About page that link to the facilities page (facilities.html) and the factory tours page (factory_tours.html). The visible link or button text is sourced from the implementation’s localization or configuration layer, not hardcoded in this design.

Applies additional scrolling or focus behavior for specific topics within the About content based on the focus URL parameter (e.g., emphasizing tour information within the body). It coordinates with rendered sections once getAboutPageContent data is available.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width industrial hero (handled elsewhere in the system) sits at the very top. Immediately below the hero is a strong horizontal divider bar spanning the entire viewport. Under this divider, all About Us content is placed inside a centered content container. This container is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for section/navigation links (about-related-navigation) and a wider right column for the actual About Us content (about-main-header, about-overview-body, about-statistics-summary, about-certifications-list). Non-visual logic components (about-content-loader, about-section-state-manager, about-focus-handler) are structurally positioned at the top of the About Us content area but do not break or visually interrupt this two-column arrangement.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width industrial hero (handled elsewhere in the system) sits at the very top. Immediately below the hero is a strong horizontal divider bar spanning the entire viewport. Under this divider, all About Us content is placed inside a centered content container. This container is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for section/navigation links (about-related-navigation) and a wider right column for the actual About Us content (about-main-header, about-overview-body, about-statistics-summary, about-certifications-list). Non-visual logic components (about-content-loader, about-section-state-manager, about-focus-handler) are structurally positioned at the top of the About Us content area but do not break or visually interrupt this two-column arrangement.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero above consumes roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height. Below the divider, the central content container is constrained to a comfortable maximum width (around 1040–1200px) and centered with equal left/right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar occupies about 20–25% of the width, while the right main content column takes the remaining 75–80%. Vertically, the right column stacks the main header at the top, followed by overview text, then a band of key statistics, and finally the certifications list. Each major block is separated by 24–40px of vertical space, aligned to an 8px-based rhythm.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density matches the mockup’s functional, slightly compact feel. The hero remains visually open, but the two-column content band is denser: sidebar navigation items are relatively close together with 8–12px between items, while main content uses 16–24px padding inside each block. Paragraph spacing is modest (around 12–16px between paragraphs), and list items in statistics and certifications sections are compact but comfortably readable. This delivers a utilitarian, information-first layout without feeling cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows an F/Z pattern: the user’s eye starts at the centered hero title and imagery, moves down to the divider bar, then into the content band, where the right column’s large About Us heading immediately draws attention. From there the eye moves down through the overview text, then into the more scannable statistics band, and finally to the certifications.
