The left sidebar navigation sits as a stable vertical reference; it’s noticed primarily when users look for ways to navigate to related pages (facilities, factory tour) or when they scan back up the left edge. Non-visual components support this flow by ensuring the right column’s main header is the first focus point for keyboard navigation and that switching sections feels seamless.

layout.overall_description: Below a full-width industrial hero image and a strong gray horizontal divider, the About Us page content sits in a centered, two-column band that echoes a classic Google Sites corporate layout. The left fifth of the band is a narrow vertical sidebar containing stacked navigation links to related pages like facilities and factory tours; links are compactly spaced, with minimal decoration, reinforcing a no-nonsense, utilitarian tone. To the right, occupying the remaining width, the main content column begins with a large, left-aligned “About Us” heading aligned horizontally with the top of the sidebar navigation. Immediately beneath this title, a block of overview text runs across the full width of the right column, with moderate line length and modest paragraph spacing. Below the overview, a band of key statistics appears as a row or grid of compact stat tiles that span the right column, providing scannable numbers and labels. Beneath the stats, the certifications section continues the vertical flow with a slightly denser grid or list of certification items, badges, or logos, still aligned to the main column’s left edge. All visible blocks sit on an 8px-based spacing system, with 24–40px vertical separation between major sections, resulting in a practical, image-supported corporate layout where the hero sets the tone and the two-column band organizes navigation and content into a clear, predictable structure.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-content-loader is positioned logically at the top of the About Us page’s main content structure, just below the global hero-and-divider region and above both the left sidebar and right content column in the DOM order. It spans the full width of the central content container but remains visually invisible, taking up effectively zero visible height once loaded. Its role is to prepare and feed data to the visible About Us sections without altering the two-column layout; as soon as data is available, the sidebar navigation and right-column sections render in their designated areas beneath the divider bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, full-width logical container that scrolls with the page; it does not reserve visible space and does not affect layout beyond controlling when child components can render.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-section-state-manager is embedded near the top of the About Us content area, adjacent in structure to the content loader and conceptually sitting above the visible two-column band. It does not render any visible UI; instead, it governs which subsections within the right main content column are currently active or emphasized (e.g., history, mission, statistics, certifications) and synchronizes active states with the left sidebar navigation. Visually, the page appears as a normal two-column layout; the state manager only influences which content blocks in the right column are shown, collapsed, or highlighted at any given time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller bound to the entire About Us content container; scrolls naturally with the page and does not occupy dedicated visual space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-main-header is the first clearly visible element in the right main content column. It is positioned directly below the horizontal divider, with a vertical offset of approximately 32–40px inside the central content container. Horizontally, it aligns flush with the left edge of the right column, which starts roughly 20–25% in from the container’s left side (to accommodate the sidebar). The header text (e.g., “About Us”) spans the full width of the right column but is left-aligned, set in a large, bold type that visually anchors the page’s content area. There is about 16–24px of space beneath this header before the overview body text begins, giving it clear prominence while maintaining the moderately compact vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the right column; it scrolls with the content and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-overview-body sits immediately below the about-main-header in the right content column, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the right column, with text lines limited naturally by that column’s width (approximately 75–80% of the central content container). Paragraphs are left-aligned and stacked vertically, with 12–16px between paragraphs to maintain legibility within the slightly compact density. The top edge of this text block visually aligns with the first navigation item in the left sidebar, reinforcing the horizontal banding implied by the mockup. Below the last paragraph of this overview, there is a 24–32px gap before the statistics summary begins, visually distinguishing introduction text from more structured data that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level content that scrolls with the page; it expands vertically based on text length but remains confined to the width of the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-certifications-list is placed in the lower portion of the right main content column, directly beneath the statistics summary and separated from it by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the right column, aligning with the same left and right edges as the main header and overview body. Within this area, certifications are presented as a moderately dense vertical list or simple grid: on larger screens, items can appear in two columns (e.g., certification name and issuing body or logo) with 16–24px padding inside each item and 12–16px gaps between rows; on narrower screens, items collapse into a single column list. The visual styling remains straightforward and utilitarian—minimal iconography, with emphasis on clearly reading certification names. Additional 32–40px of space below the final certification item gives a clean visual end to the About Us content, before any page footer begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable block bound to the right column, expanding vertically based on the number of certifications; it does not fix or overlay other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-statistics-summary appears directly below the overview body within the right main content column, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the right column and presents key company numbers (e.g., years in operation, facilities, employees, production capacity) in a compact grid of stat tiles. On desktop widths, 3–4 tiles are laid out in a single row or two rows, each tile using 16–24px of internal padding and separated horizontally and vertically by 16px, generating a dense but orderly band of data. Each tile aligns within the right column’s bounds, with its top edge forming a strong horizontal line that roughly matches the midpoint of the sidebar navigation. Below the last row of tiles, another 24–32px of space introduces the certifications list, keeping sections visually distinct while maintaining the overall compact rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Responsive grid container within the right column; it reflows tiles as the viewport narrows but always stays within the right column and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-related-navigation occupies the left sidebar of the content band, directly below the horizontal divider and parallel in vertical position to the right column’s main header. It spans roughly 20–25% of the central content container’s width and extends downward as a vertical stack of navigation items (e.g., About Us, Facilities, Factory Tour, Certifications). Each nav item is left-aligned, with 12–16px of vertical padding and about 16–24px of horizontal padding from the sidebar’s left edge, resulting in a compact column of links. Items are spaced with 8–12px between them, echoing the slightly old-school, utilitarian navigation style seen in Google Sites-like layouts. The active link corresponding to the current page (About Us) is visually distinguished through a bolder weight or background highlight that still feels restrained. The sidebar’s top aligns horizontally with the top of the main About Us header, and its bottom can extend beyond the right column sections if there are more links, but it always stays narrower and visually subordinate to the main content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sidebar block that scrolls with the page; optionally configured as a sticky element that remains fixed within the viewport once scrolled a short distance (e.g., sticks 80–96px below the top), maintaining persistent access to navigation on taller pages.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The about-focus-handler is logically attached near the top of the main content area, just before the about-main-header in the DOM order. It manifests as invisible focusable anchors or skip targets that do not occupy visible width or height. From a spatial standpoint, it coexists at the very top of the right column’s content stack, ensuring that keyboard users and assistive technologies can bypass the hero and navigation and land directly on the About Us heading and key content sections. Although it has no visible footprint, its placement mirrors the starting point of visual content: immediately after the global layout’s divider and just before the main header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, focus-management layer attached to the right column’s top; it scrolls with the document and only becomes apparent in keyboard focus order.

name: Our Facilities
title: Our Facilities
description: Functional page that loads and displays structured information about the company’s manufacturing facilities, equipment, and safety standards, and provides navigation to factory tour scheduling and related company information.

core_features: Fetch facilities content (headline, body, highlights) via the getFacilitiesPageContent interface on page load.
Display the facilities headline and main descriptive body content returned by the interface.
List key facility highlights (e.g., production lines, equipment, safety procedures) using the highlights array returned by the interface.
Allow users to focus on a specific highlight using a URL parameter to control which highlight is considered selected.

Provide a functional call-to-action navigation element that leads to the factory_tours.html page, optionally including URL parameters that reflect the user’s current context (e.g., selected highlight).
Provide a functional navigation control that sends users back to about.html, including URL parameters that indicate that the user came from the facilities page.
Synchronize page state (selected highlight, expanded/collapsed detail view) with URL parameters so the state is shareable and restorable on reload.
user_workflows:
Workflow: View general facilities overview
1. User navigates to facilities.html (optionally with no URL parameters).
2. On initial load, the page-level loader component calls getFacilitiesPageContent.
3. Once data is returned, the headline is displayed in the page header and the body text is rendered in the main content section.
4. The highlights list component renders each highlight from the returned highlights array.
5. If no highlight-related URL parameter is present, the first highlight in the array (or another default determined at runtime) is considered selected for more detailed emphasis.
6. User reads the overview body and scans the highlights list.

Workflow: Deep-link to a specific highlight
1. User follows a link to facilities.html with a URL parameter, e.g., facilities.html?highlightIndex={n}.
2. On load, the loader component calls getFacilitiesPageContent.
3. When the data is available, the highlights list is rendered.
4. The highlight-detail component reads the highlightIndex parameter from the URL.
5. If highlightIndex corresponds to a valid index in the highlights array, that highlight is marked as the selected highlight and its details are emphasized; otherwise a default highlight is used.
6. The page updates the selection state without additional API calls.

Workflow: Change selected highlight via interaction
1. User is on facilities.html with facilities content already loaded.
2. User clicks a highlight item in the highlights list.
3. The click handler updates the selected highlight ID/index in the page state.
4. The handler also updates the URL (e.g., using pushState/replaceState) to set or change the highlightIndex parameter to the newly selected highlight’s index.
5. The highlight-detail component re-renders to show the newly selected highlight.
6. The Factory Tour call-to-action component updates any context it derives from the selected highlight (e.g., passes the selected index or title into the target URL as a parameter).

Workflow: Navigate to Factory Tours from facilities context
1. User reviews facilities information and decides to learn more about tours.
2. User activates the call-to-action control (e.g., a button or link) managed by the factory-tour-cta component.
3. The click handler constructs a navigation URL to factory_tours.html, including query parameters such as source=facilities and, if a highlight is selected, highlightIndex and/or highlightTitle (based only on the current dynamic data).
4. Browser navigates to factory_tours.html with those parameters, allowing that page to interpret the context (e.g., which part of the facility interested the user).

Workflow: Return to About Us
1. User is on facilities.html and wants to go back to broader company information.
2. User activates the back-to-about link component.
3. The click handler navigates to about.html, appending a URL parameter such as from=facilities (and optionally any other context parameters, like lastHighlightIndex).
4. Browser loads about.html, whose logic can optionally use these parameters for contextual behavior.

Workflow: Share or bookmark facilities state
1. User navigates to facilities.html, selects a specific highlight, and possibly changes a detail view mode (e.g., full vs. summary view).
2. Each interaction updates the URL parameters (e.g., highlightIndex and viewMode) without reloading the page.
3. User copies the URL or bookmarks the page.
4. Later, when the URL is opened again, the loader component calls getFacilitiesPageContent and restores the selected highlight and detail view mode based on the URL parameters.
interactions:
Interaction: Page load
- Trigger: Browser loads facilities.html.
- Behavior: The page-level loader component invokes getFacilitiesPageContent with no parameters. When the promise resolves, it stores the response object in page state and passes the relevant fields (headline, body, highlights) as props to child components. URL parameters are read after data is available to determine selected highlight and any view modes.

Interaction: Highlight selection
- Trigger: User clicks a highlight entry in the highlights list.
- Behavior: The click handler reads the clicked item’s index in the highlights array (or another runtime-derived identifier) and updates the selected highlight state. It also updates the URL parameter (e.g., highlightIndex) to reflect this new selection. The highlight-detail component re-renders to display the selected highlight, and the CTA component updates any target URL parameters derived from this selection.

Interaction: Change detail view mode
- Trigger: User toggles a control in the detail area (e.g., a control to switch between compact/summary and full detail view of the selected highlight or body content). The labels for the control are provided externally (e.g., by server-side configuration or translation) rather than hardcoded in this specification.
- Behavior: The detail component updates an internal state variable indicating the chosen view mode and synchronizes it with a URL parameter such as viewMode=summary or viewMode=full. On subsequent reloads, this parameter is used to restore the same view mode.

Interaction: Navigate to Factory Tours
- Trigger: User clicks the CTA element generated by the factory-tour-cta component.
- Behavior: The component constructs a URL to factory_tours.html. It always includes a source=facilities parameter. If a valid selected highlight index exists, it also appends highlightIndex={currentIndex}. Optionally, it can append highlightTitle using a safely encoded version of the selected highlight’s title. The browser then performs a standard navigation to the constructed URL.

Interaction: Navigate back to About Us
- Trigger: User clicks the back-to-about navigation control.
- Behavior: The component constructs a URL to about.html with a from=facilities parameter and, if available, lastHighlightIndex={currentIndex}. The browser navigates to this URL.

Interaction: URL parameter change without full reload
- Trigger: Internal navigation within the page (e.g., highlight selection, detail view toggle) when JavaScript history manipulation is available.
- Behavior: The page uses history.pushState or history.replaceState to update the query string parameters (highlightIndex, viewMode, etc.) without requesting the page from the server again. Components listen to state changes maintained in memory rather than directly listening to URL changes, but they ensure that URL and internal state remain synchronized.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The facilities.html page uses URL parameters exclusively (no localStorage) to represent restorable state:

- Parameter: highlightIndex
  - Type: non-negative integer in string form.
  - Purpose: identifies which element in the highlights array returned by getFacilitiesPageContent is considered the selected highlight.
  - On initial load: after getFacilitiesPageContent resolves, the loader parses highlightIndex from the URL. If it is a valid index within the array bounds, that index is stored as the selected highlight. If it is missing or invalid, a default index (e.g., 0) is used, and the URL may be updated to reflect the actual selected index.
  - On interaction: when the user selects a different highlight, the page updates this parameter using pushState/replaceState.

- Parameter: viewMode
  - Allowed values: implementation-defined fixed set such as 'summary' or 'full'. These literals are structural, not content, and do not conflict with dynamic data.
  - Purpose: indicates how the body and/or highlight details should be displayed (e.g., truncated vs. full-length text). The specific behavior is implemented by the detail component based on this value.
  - On initial load: if viewMode is present and matches a known mode, that mode is applied; otherwise, a default mode is assumed.
  - On interaction: toggling the detail mode updates viewMode in the URL without reloading.

- Parameter: source (used for outbound links only)
  - For outbound navigation to factory_tours.html and about.html, the page sets source=facilities or from=facilities on the target URLs. These are not read by facilities.html itself but are part of the contract with the target pages.

The page calls getFacilitiesPageContent only once per load, independent of URL parameter changes. Subsequent changes to highlightIndex or viewMode update only client-side state and URL parameters; they do not trigger additional API calls. All derived data (selected highlight object, selected title for CTA parameters, etc.) is computed from the single response object from getFacilitiesPageContent.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by calling getFacilitiesPageContent, storing the returned headline, body, and highlights in page-level state, and coordinating initial state derivation from URL parameters (such as highlightIndex and viewMode). It passes structured props to presentation-focused functional components (header, body, highlights list, detail view, and CTA/navigation components).

Displays the main headline of the facilities page and provides immediate context to the user using the headline field returned by getFacilitiesPageContent. Also can include a contextual subtitle or breadcrumb provided externally (not hardcoded here).

Renders the primary descriptive body text about the facilities, including layout, machinery overview, and safety standards, using the body field returned by getFacilitiesPageContent. It uses the viewMode state (derived from URL) to decide whether to show a summary or full text.

Displays a list of facility highlights based on the highlights array from getFacilitiesPageContent. Each list item is interactive and, when activated, updates the selected highlight index, both in internal state and in the URL (highlightIndex). This component does not store static label text; all per-item titles and descriptions come from the API response.

Shows the details of the currently selected highlight (e.g., more descriptive text about a specific production line, machine group, or safety process). It reads the current selected index from state (which is synchronized with URL.query.highlightIndex) and uses it to select the appropriate element from the highlights array.

Provides a functional control for users to proceed to the factory_tours.html page to learn more about tours or schedule a visit. The component constructs the target URL dynamically, embedding source=facilities and, if available, the current highlightIndex and an encoded representation of the selected highlight’s title as query parameters. Button or link text is sourced externally, not hardcoded in this specification.

Renders a functional link that navigates back to about.html. When triggered, it includes from=facilities and optionally lastHighlightIndex derived from the current selected highlight in the query string of the target URL. Link label text is provided externally and not hardcoded here.

Centralizes synchronization between internal page state (selected highlight index, view mode) and the URL query parameters. When components change these states (e.g., user selects a highlight or toggles view mode), this controller updates the URL (highlightIndex, viewMode) with pushState or replaceState. On initial load, it parses these parameters and exposes normalized values to other components.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page lives inside the existing site template: a full-width industrial hero at the very top with centered site title and global navigation, followed by a thick horizontal divider bar. All facilities-related components sit below this divider inside a central content container. Within this container, the layout follows a two-column structure: a narrow left sidebar for facilities navigation and meta links, and a wider right column for the page title, descriptive copy, CTA, and the currently selected facility highlight detail. The arrangement is linear top-to-bottom within each column but supports side-to-side scanning between sidebar items and main content.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page lives inside the existing site template: a full-width industrial hero at the very top with centered site title and global navigation, followed by a thick horizontal divider bar. All facilities-related components sit below this divider inside a central content container. Within this container, the layout follows a two-column structure: a narrow left sidebar for facilities navigation and meta links, and a wider right column for the page title, descriptive copy, CTA, and the currently selected facility highlight detail. The arrangement is linear top-to-bottom within each column but supports side-to-side scanning between sidebar items and main content.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The content band below the hero is constrained to a centered container (e.g., ~1,120–1,200px max width) with modest left/right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar occupies roughly 22–25% of the width, while the right main content column takes 75–78%. Vertical spacing between major blocks (header, body text, CTA, highlight detail) follows the 8px grid with 24–40px gaps. Each component respects internal padding of 16–24px and aligns to the implicit 12-column-like grid, keeping everything blocky and utilitarian rather than overly airy.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is moderate-to-compact. The right-column header and introductory text have comfortable 24–32px vertical spacing, while lists and nav items are more tightly packed, using 8–16px vertical gaps between items. The highlight detail area and CTA use clear bounding boxes or visual groupings with 16–24px padding to avoid clutter. Overall, the layout favors clarity and scannability over white-space-heavy minimalism, consistent with an industrial, corporate site.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves top-to-bottom and left-to-right in an F/Z pattern. First, users see the site hero (outside this component set), then drop to the horizontal divider that frames the content band. Within the band, attention goes to the large page header in the right column, then flows down into the descriptive facilities copy. From there, the bold factory tour CTA and the highlight detail area draw the eye further down. The left sidebar list of facilities acts as a vertical anchor; when users move their gaze left, they see the navigation items that control which highlight detail is visible on the right. On reloads or deep links, state-controlled selections ensure the same flow is preserved by showing the relevant highlight detail immediately.

layout.overall_description: Below the full-width industrial hero image and a solid gray horizontal divider, a centered content band spans the width of the page with modest side margins. This band is split into a narrow left sidebar and a wider right content area, echoing a classic Google Sites–style two-column layout. At the top of the right column, a bold 'Our Facilities' header sits flush left, aligned horizontally with the top of the left sidebar list. A concise block of descriptive text follows, explaining the company’s facilities, equipment, and safety standards. Just beneath this, a prominent factory tour call-to-action bar stretches across most of the right column, clearly separated by 24–32px of vertical space. Below that, the main highlight detail area fills the remaining right column height, showing imagery and structured information about the currently selected facility or capability. On the left, a compact vertical list of facility highlights runs from the top of the content band downward, giving users quick navigation between different areas (e.g., manufacturing floor, quality lab, warehouse). At the bottom of this sidebar, separated by a small gap, a quieter 'Back to About' link provides a way to return to broader company information. During loading, a subtle overlay with a centered indicator temporarily sits above both columns. The result is a straightforward, utilitarian page where the hero, clear headings, left navigation, and right-side details work together in a familiar, corporate-feeling layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-page-loader is a non-permanent overlay that appears within the central content band directly under the hero divider when the page is fetching data. It covers both the left sidebar area and the right main content column, matching the width of the constrained content container and extending just enough vertically to span all visible facilities components in the viewport. The overlay uses a light, semi-opaque background so the underlying two-column structure is hinted but visually suppressed. At the center of the overlay (both horizontally and vertically relative to the content band), a simple loading indicator and short status label appear, aligned to the 8px grid. When loading completes, the overlay disappears entirely, allowing the left navigation and right content to be fully visible and interactable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlay relative to the content band; positioned on top of all facilities components, blocking interaction while visible; otherwise absent from the layout and not occupying space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-header sits at the top of the right main content column, just below the thick horizontal divider that caps the content band. It aligns flush left with the right column’s content edge, starting approximately 32px below the divider. Horizontally, it spans nearly the full width of the right column (around 90–100% of that column), leaving standard inner padding (16–24px) if rendered as a header block. The text is set in a large, bold style, standing visually taller than surrounding text and anchoring the right side of the F-pattern. Its top baseline aligns roughly with the top of the first item in the left facilities-highlights-list, ensuring the two columns feel horizontally aligned. Below the header, a 16–24px vertical gap separates it from the facilities-body-content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block element within the right column; scrolls with the page and does not stick or overlap other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-body-content is positioned immediately beneath the facilities-header in the right main column, separated by a modest vertical gap of about 16–24px. It occupies the full usable width of the right column (minus standard side padding), forming a rectangular text block that can accommodate several paragraphs. The first line of text is left-aligned with the header above, creating a clean vertical alignment. Internally, paragraphs are spaced with 8–16px vertical gaps, following the moderate density of the mockup. The bottom of this text block sits approximately 24–32px above the factory-tour-cta, clearly grouping the header and descriptive content together as a conceptual unit before introducing interactive elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow content; expands vertically depending on text length and pushes subsequent components (CTA and highlight detail) downward accordingly.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-highlights-list forms the backbone of the left sidebar navigation. It begins at the top of the content band, about 32px below the horizontal divider, so that its first item aligns horizontally with the facilities-header text across the page. The list occupies the full width of the left column (roughly 22–25% of the total content band width) and runs vertically down the page. Each list item (representing a facility area or highlight) is left-aligned, with 12–16px vertical spacing between items and 8–16px of horizontal padding inside the clickable area, giving a slightly compact, utilitarian feel. The list has 16–24px of padding at the top and bottom of its container to keep the items from touching the edges. Visually, it appears as a vertical menu or list of text links, possibly with a subtle background tone or left border to distinguish it as navigation. When a highlight is selected, that item appears visually emphasized (e.g., bold text or a darker background) while still maintaining the same spatial footprint. The bottom of the list leaves enough vertical room (24–32px) above the back-to-about-link.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow left column content; in desktop layouts it remains a distinct vertical sidebar. On smaller screens, it can stack above the right column content but maintains its role as a vertical navigation list.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-highlight-detail component sits in the right main column below the factory-tour-cta, separated by a 32px gap to signal a new content block. It spans the full width of the right column, forming the dominant lower section of the page. The top of this block starts slightly higher than the mid-height of the left facilities-highlights-list items, so the eye naturally travels from a selected item on the left to its detailed information on the right. Internally, this block can be structured like a card or mini-layout that combines an image area (e.g., top or left) with textual details and specifications (e.g., right or bottom), but it remains contained by 16–24px padding on all sides. Vertically, it can extend significantly depending on content length and imagery, pushing the page’s scroll and defining the bulk of the page’s informational area. At the bottom of the highlight detail, a 32–40px margin separates it from any subsequent page-level content (such as a global footer), preserving the sense that it is the final major section within the facilities page body.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block that grows vertically with content; its presence and content are controlled by navigation state (which highlight is selected), but spatially it always occupies the main lower portion of the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The factory-tour-cta is positioned in the right main column directly beneath the facilities-body-content, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It appears as a horizontally oriented CTA bar or card that spans roughly 70–100% of the right column width, aligned to the same left edge as the header and body text. Inside the CTA block, there is 16–24px padding on all sides. The main call-to-action text is left-aligned, with a prominent button or link positioned either to the right within the same row (on wider screens) or stacked below on narrower ones. The CTA’s background may be slightly more pronounced (e.g., a muted accent color or a light gray panel) to distinguish it from regular text, but it remains consistent with the corporate, no-nonsense style. Vertically, it is compact—around 64–96px tall—so that it is clearly visible without overwhelming the highlight detail below. There is a 32px gap separating the bottom of the CTA from the top of the facilities-highlight-detail, creating a clear hierarchy: intro text → CTA → detailed content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block in the right column; it does not stick to the viewport but remains near the top of the main content area, just above the highlight detail. On smaller screens, its inner layout may shift from a horizontal alignment to a stacked vertical arrangement.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-about-link lives in the left sidebar, directly beneath the facilities-highlights-list. It is aligned to the left edge of the list items and uses the same overall width as the sidebar (22–25% of the content band). There is a 24–32px vertical gap between the bottom of the last facilities list item and this link, visually separating it from the main navigation items and signaling that it leads to a broader section of the site. The link itself is compact, with minimal padding (8–12px) and styled more subtly than the highlight list items—e.g., regular-weight text, smaller size, or lower contrast. Its vertical placement is typically near the lower third of the sidebar, depending on the number of navigation items, so it feels like a secondary, utility-style option rather than a primary focus. It does not extend below the bottom edge of the highlight detail on the right, maintaining a tidy horizontal baseline when both columns end.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow element within the left sidebar; scrolls with the page and does not stay fixed. On mobile/stacked layouts, it appears below the highlights list but above any global footer elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer is non-visual and does not occupy any visible space on the page. Conceptually, it sits at the page-level container for the facilities area and governs which facilities-highlight-detail is shown and which item in the facilities-highlights-list appears active, based on the URL or navigation interactions. Its effect is seen indirectly: when the page first loads or the URL changes, the appropriate left-hand list item is highlighted and the corresponding right-hand detail section is rendered in the facilities-highlight-detail area without any abrupt layout shifts beyond the usual content changes. The spatial structure—the two columns and the positions of all visual components—remains stable regardless of state changes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; does not render within the layout tree and does not affect spacing. It only updates the state of visual components that already have defined spatial positions.

name: Factory Tours
title: Factory Tours
description: Page for viewing factory tour information and submitting tour booking requests, with optional prefilled details from URL parameters.

core_features: Load and display factory tour information (intro text, tour types, safety requirements, typical schedule) using getFactoryToursInfo.
Provide a clear call-to-action that moves the user to the tour request form (scrolling within the page or reloading with a form anchor in the URL).
Render a factory tour request form that collects preferred date, preferred time, group size, contact name, contact email, and an optional message.
Prefill the factory tour request form using incoming URL parameters preferred_date, preferred_time, and group_size when present and valid.
Validate tour request data on the client (e.g., required fields, date format, business rules about allowed dates and times) before submission.
Submit the tour request via submitFactoryTourRequest and display success or error feedback based on the response.
Provide navigation links to related pages (About Us and Facilities) based on the defined outgoing connections.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View tour information
1. User navigates to factory_tours.html (directly or from the footer link).
2. On page load, the page-init controller component calls getFactoryToursInfo.
3. The intro text, tour types list, safety requirements list, and typical schedule text returned by getFactoryToursInfo are passed to the respective display components.
4. User reviews the information and decides whether to book a tour.
5. User can click navigation links to about.html or facilities.html if they want more context about the company or facilities.

Workflow 2: Book a tour directly on the page
1. User navigates to factory_tours.html without URL parameters.
2. Page-init controller calls getFactoryToursInfo and populates informational components.
3. User clicks the primary “Book a Tour” call-to-action button.
4. The page scrolls to the tour request form or appends an anchor (e.g., #tour-request) to the URL and focuses the first editable field.
5. User fills in: preferred date, preferred time, group size, contact name, contact email, and optional additional message.
6. On form submission, the form component validates all fields, including the preferred date and group size according to business rules.
7. If validation passes, the form component calls submitFactoryTourRequest with the captured values.
8. On successful response, the feedback component displays the confirmation message returned in the response (response.message) and may show a read-only summary using the returned FactoryTourRequest object.
9. If the API returns an error or the call fails, an error state is displayed and the user can correct the form and resubmit.

Workflow 3: Book a tour with prefilled details from other pages
1. User is on facilities.html or about.html and clicks an action such as “Request a Factory Tour”, which links to factory_tours.html with URL query parameters preferred_date, preferred_time, and/or group_size.
2. User lands on factory_tours.html?preferred_date=...&preferred_time=...&group_size=....
3. On page load, the page-init controller parses these URL parameters.
4. The parsed values are validated for format and business rules (e.g., preferred_date not in the past and meeting weekday/future-window constraints; group_size within allowed bounds).
5. Valid parameter values are used as initial values in the tour request form fields; invalid or missing values leave the corresponding fields empty for user input.
6. User reviews or adjusts the prefilled date, time, and group size, and completes remaining required fields (contact name and email).
7. User submits the form; submitFactoryTourRequest is called with the final values.
8. Feedback component displays success or error state based on the API response.

Workflow 4: Navigate to related information
1. While viewing the Factory Tours page, the user clicks the breadcrumb or link labeled for About Us.
2. Browser navigates to about.html via a standard URL change.
3. Alternatively, user clicks the “Learn more about our facilities” contextual link.
4. Browser navigates to facilities.html via a standard URL change.
5. Those pages can optionally link back to factory_tours.html with prefilled parameters if the user initiates a tour request from there.
interactions:
On page load (factory_tours.html):
- The page-init controller parses the current URL for query parameters preferred_date, preferred_time, and group_size.
- It then triggers getFactoryToursInfo with no parameters.
- When getFactoryToursInfo resolves, its response is stored in page state and passed to the information components (intro, tour types, safety requirements, typical schedule).
- If an anchor such as #tour-request is present, the page scrolls to the tour request form component.

Click interaction: Book a Tour CTA
- User clicks the main call-to-action button in the info area.
- Event handler either scrolls smoothly to the tour request form or updates the URL to include an anchor (e.g., #tour-request) and then focuses the first form field.
- No API call is made at this step.

Form entry interactions: tour request form fields
- When the form is initialized, it checks the parsed URL parameters and sets initial values of the preferred date, preferred time, and group size fields where valid.
- On user input (onChange) for each field, the component updates its internal form state, performs real-time validation where appropriate (e.g., date format, numeric group size, email format), and updates inline error messages.

Form submission interaction
- User clicks the form submission button.
- The onSubmit handler prevents the default browser submission behavior, then runs validation across all fields:
  - Ensures preferred date is present and in an accepted format, and adheres to business rules (e.g., not in the past, allowed weekday rules, allowed future window).
  - Ensures preferred time is present and matches an accepted time format.
  - Ensures group size is present, numeric, and within the allowed range for tours.
  - Ensures contact name and contact email are present, and email format is valid.
- If any validation fails, the form state is updated with error messages and no API call is made.
- If validation passes, the handler calls submitFactoryTourRequest with preferred_date, preferred_time, group_size, additional_message, contact_name, and contact_email from the form state.
- While waiting for the response, the form may enter a submitting state, disabling inputs and submit button to prevent duplicates.

API response handling interaction
- On successful submitFactoryTourRequest response, the tour-request-feedback component receives the response.request (FactoryTourRequest object) and response.message.
- The component displays response.message and, if configured, a summary of the submitted request (read-only values such as preferred_date, preferred_time, and group_size from response.request).
- The form component may clear its fields, mark itself as submitted, or switch to a read-only/acknowledged mode based on page logic.
- On API error (e.g., network failure or server validation error), the feedback component shows an error state, and the form component re-enables inputs and allows resubmission.

Navigation interactions
- Clicking the About Us breadcrumb or link updates window.location to about.html, performing a full page navigation.
- Clicking the Learn more about our facilities link updates window.location to facilities.html, performing a full page navigation.
- These navigations do not call any interfaces from the Factory Tours page; they rely on standard link behavior.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses the URL (query and optional hash/anchor) as the main external state carrier, not localStorage. Query parameters:
- preferred_date (string): When present, on page load the page-init controller attempts to parse this as an ISO-like date string. If parsing and validation (format, not in the past, within the allowed range, and satisfying business rules such as weekday-only policies) succeed, this value is assigned as the initial value of the preferred date field in the tour request form. If invalid, it is ignored and the field remains empty for the user to fill.
- preferred_time (string): When present, the controller uses it as the initial value for the preferred time field, after checking it matches a recognized time format. Invalid values are ignored.
- group_size (number): When present, the controller converts it to a number and checks that it is within the allowed range for group sizes. Valid values are used to initialize the group size field; invalid values are ignored.
These parameters only affect the initial form state; subsequent edits by the user are stored in component state and are not written back into the URL automatically.

Hash / anchor logic:
- If the page URL includes an anchor fragment that targets the tour request form (for example, factory_tours.html#tour-request), the page-init controller scrolls the viewport to the form on load and focuses the first available field.
- When the user clicks the Book a Tour CTA, the handler may update the URL to include the same anchor fragment using pushState/replaceState or a standard anchor link. This ensures that if the user refreshes the page, the browser can automatically return to the form section.

API-driven state:
- The result of getFactoryToursInfo is transient page state used to populate information components and is not serialized into the URL.
- The submitFactoryTourRequest response is also held in page state. The FactoryTourRequest returned in response.request can be used to display a confirmation summary. This state is not persisted across navigations unless the containing application decides to encode it in the URL or another mechanism; the Factory Tours page itself does not add custom parameters for it.

Error and loading state:
- Loading, error, and success states for both getFactoryToursInfo and submitFactoryTourRequest are held in component-specific state and are not reflected in URL parameters.
- If desired, the application could add a simple status parameter (e.g., tour_status) to the URL to reflect a successful or failed submission, but this is optional and not required for the core functionality described here.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial data loading and URL parameter parsing for the Factory Tours page. On load, it parses query parameters (preferred_date, preferred_time, group_size), calls getFactoryToursInfo, and initializes shared page state used by other components, including initial form values and informational content.
Displays the high-level factory tours information, including intro text and typical schedule, as returned by getFactoryToursInfo.
Renders the list of available tour types, each with its name and description, using the tour_types array from getFactoryToursInfo.
Displays the safety requirements for factory visits as a list, using the safety_requirements array from getFactoryToursInfo.

Provides a prominent action control (e.g., button or link) labeled for initiating a tour booking. When activated, it scrolls the page to the tour request form or updates the URL with an anchor to focus the form.

Collects user input required to request a factory tour: preferred date, preferred time, group size, contact name, contact email, and an optional additional message. Initializes its fields based on validated preferred_date, preferred_time, and group_size values from URL parameters parsed by the page-init controller. Performs client-side validation and, on success, calls submitFactoryTourRequest.

Displays feedback messages related to tour request submission, including success messages (using response.message) and optional summaries based on the returned FactoryTourRequest object, as well as error messages if submission fails.
Indicates the loading state while getFactoryToursInfo is in progress and optionally displays errors if the call fails.
Manages and displays the loading or disabled state of the tour request form while submitFactoryTourRequest is in progress to prevent duplicate submissions.
Displays a breadcrumb navigation trail that includes a link to about.html, allowing users to navigate back to the About Us page.
Provides a contextual navigation link to facilities.html, typically near tour description content, so users can learn more about the facilities before or after requesting a tour.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width hero (provided by the site template) at the very top, immediately followed by a strong horizontal divider. Beneath this divider sits a centered content container split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for contextual navigation and a wider right column for the Factory Tours content. All provided components live within this band: the facilities-context-link in the left sidebar, and breadcrumb, tour info, lists, CTA, form, and states in the right main column. Components in the right column are stacked vertically in the order: breadcrumb → tour info → tour types → safety requirements → booking CTA → form (with feedback and loading states).

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the mockup’s linear flow: a full-width hero (provided by the site template) at the very top, immediately followed by a strong horizontal divider. Beneath this divider sits a centered content container split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for contextual navigation and a wider right column for the Factory Tours content. All provided components live within this band: the facilities-context-link in the left sidebar, and breadcrumb, tour info, lists, CTA, form, and states in the right main column. Components in the right column are stacked vertically in the order: breadcrumb → tour info → tour types → safety requirements → booking CTA → form (with feedback and loading states).
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly 35–40% of the initial viewport height and spans the full browser width. Below it, the central content container spans about 80–90% of the page width, centered, and is visually divided into a left sidebar (approx. 20–25% width, akin to 3/12 columns) and a right content area (75–80% width, about 9/12 columns). Within the right column, each section (info, lists, CTA, form) uses the full column width. Vertical spacing between major sections is in the 32–48px range, while spacing between closely related elements (breadcrumb to heading, heading to paragraph, paragraph to list) uses 16–24px spacing, all on an 8px grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: Above the divider, the hero is visually open, dominated by imagery and a bold title with ample breathing room. Below the divider, density increases: the left sidebar links are relatively tight vertically with about 8–16px between items, and the right column stacks several text blocks, lists, and a form. Content remains readable but compact: lists are not overly spaced out, the form fields are grouped with efficient spacing, and the CTA is close to the form. This matches a functional, slightly utilitarian corporate aesthetic rather than an airy editorial feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye starts at the center of the hero (site or section title), then moves down to any hero subtext and across to any top navigation, following an F/Z-like pattern. After scrolling past the hero, the strong horizontal divider acts as a visual reset. The gaze then goes to the right column: first to the breadcrumb at the top left of that column, then down to the Tour Info heading and introductory copy. From there, the eye flows downward through the tour types list, then into the safety requirements, and is finally drawn to the prominent booking CTA button and the tour request form beneath it. Throughout, the left sidebar—with the facilities-context-link—serves as a vertical anchor that is re-noticed when scanning back up or when considering navigation to related pages.

layout.overall_description: The Factory Tours page appears as a straightforward, corporate web page with a slightly old-school Google Sites feel: a large industrial hero image fills the top of the viewport, with a bold centered site or section title overlaid. Directly beneath this, a solid horizontal divider bar stretches across the full width, clearly separating the hero from the main content. Below the divider, a central content container is centered on the page and split into two distinct columns. On the left, a narrow sidebar column holds simple vertical navigation, anchored by a contextual link back to the broader facilities content. On the right, a wider main column contains the Factory Tours experience: a breadcrumb at the top, followed by a large “Factory Tours” heading and descriptive text. Scrolling down this column reveals a structured sequence: a tour types list laid out as simple cards or rows, then a compact safety requirements list, and finally a prominent booking call-to-action leading directly into a full-width tour request form. Loading indicators briefly replace or overlay their respective sections without breaking the overall structure, and status feedback appears just above the form. The result is a functional, image-driven layout that is easy to scan top-to-bottom and left-to-right, with clear segmentation and moderate, 8px-based spacing throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This controller component is non-visual and effectively sits at the very top of the Factory Tours page structure, immediately after the page shell loads and before any content sections. It does not occupy any visible space in the hero, divider, or content band. In the DOM, it appears above the sidebar and main content container, initializing data and URL parameter handling so that when the user sees the page, the hero and the two-column layout can populate correctly. There is no height, width, or padding allocated to it; it only influences when downstream components (tour-info-section, lists, form) render populated states versus loading states.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller; does not render in the layout and has no spatial footprint. It operates in the background and does not affect spacing other than controlling when other components become visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is positioned at the top of the right main content column, directly beneath the full-width horizontal divider that separates the hero from the content area. Within the central content container, it aligns to the left edge of the right column and spans roughly 60–70% of that column’s width, leaving comfortable breathing room to the right. There is about 24px of top margin from the divider to the breadcrumb and about 8–16px of bottom margin before the main Factory Tours heading appears. The breadcrumb text is relatively small and low-key, forming a thin horizontal strip that acts as a subtle wayfinding element without overshadowing the main heading below. It sits flush with the top of the right column while the left sidebar (with the facilities-context-link) begins vertically aligned in its own column to the left.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element within the normal document flow of the right column. It scrolls away with the page content and does not stick to the top once the user scrolls down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The facilities-context-link appears in the left sidebar column of the main content band, starting near the top of that column, horizontally aligned with the breadcrumb in the right column. The sidebar occupies approximately 20–25% of the total content container width (similar to 3 of 12 grid columns). The link is presented as the first item in a vertical list of navigation items, with about 24–32px of top margin from the horizontal divider to create a small header zone for the sidebar. The link itself has a tap/click area padded by about 12–16px vertically and horizontally, forming a simple full-width row within the sidebar. Additional contextual or section links (if present) would stack below it with 8–16px gaps, creating a compact vertical nav pattern reminiscent of standard Google Sites side navigation. Overall height is minimal, but the sidebar column is visually tall, acting as a continuous vertical strip alongside the right content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the left sidebar column. The sidebar may optionally use a sticky behavior on larger screens—keeping its top region (including this link) visible with a top offset of around 64–80px as the user scrolls—but the link itself does not float independently; it remains aligned within its column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-info-section sits in the right main content column immediately below the breadcrumb navigation. It begins with a large page title such as “Factory Tours,” left-aligned to the column’s edge and spanning nearly the full width of this column (approx. 90–100% of the right column width, which is itself about 75–80% of the page’s central container). The top margin from the breadcrumb to the heading is about 16–24px, creating a clear but not excessive separation. Below the heading, one or more paragraphs of descriptive copy flow in a single column, with 8–16px spacing between paragraphs. The entire info section is vertically contained within about 25–35% of the visible area of the content band before scrolling, making it the first substantial block of information the user encounters. There is roughly 24–32px of bottom margin after the description to separate it from the tour-types-list that follows. This section is visually dominant within the right column in terms of typography size, anchoring the page’s purpose immediately after the user passes the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static content block within the right column, participating in normal document flow. It expands vertically as needed based on text length but always maintains the same horizontal alignment and margins.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-types-list is placed directly beneath the tour-info-section within the right main column, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the right column (about 75–80% of the main container) and is organized so that each tour type appears as a clear visual item. On larger screens, these items can be arranged in a simple grid of two columns of cards (each card spanning roughly 48–50% of the right column width with 16–24px horizontal gaps), echoing the product grid seen in the mockup. Each card has 16–24px internal padding, a straightforward rectangular outline, and 16–24px vertical spacing between rows. On narrower viewports, the list collapses to a single vertical stack, with each item spanning 100% of the column width and 16–24px vertical gaps between items. The list’s top is aligned with the left edge of the right column, and the overall block consumes a significant portion of the mid-page area, visually acting as the main “examples” section of what tours are available. There is about 24–32px of bottom margin before the safety-requirements-list begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical section in the right column. Its internal layout is responsive: two-column card grid on wider screens, single-column stack on narrower screens, but it always remains confined to the width of the right column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The safety-requirements-list appears beneath the tour-types-list in the right main column, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical spacing. It is left-aligned to the right column’s edge and uses the full width of that column. At its top, it may include a smaller section heading such as “Safety Requirements,” sized smaller than the main page title and separated from its bullet or numbered items by about 8–16px. The list items are stacked in a single column with compact spacing (8–12px between lines or bullets), reinforcing the practical, information-dense character. This block is visually shorter than the tour types section—perhaps 20–30% of its height—so that users can quickly scan requirements before proceeding to booking. There is roughly 24–32px of bottom margin after the last safety requirement, creating clear separation before the booking-cta-button that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right main column, fully within the normal document flow. It grows vertically with additional requirements but maintains consistent left alignment and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The booking-cta-button is positioned immediately below the safety-requirements-list in the right main column, with about 24–32px of top margin to visually distinguish it as the next action after reading safety details. The button aligns with the left edge of the right column content but is large enough to be visually prominent—its label is clearly legible, and the clickable area has around 12–16px of vertical padding and 24–32px of horizontal padding. On wider screens, it may appear as a single primary button aligned left, with sufficient whitespace on its right side to maintain the column’s simple, utilitarian feel. The bottom margin beneath the button (before the tour-request-form) is about 16–24px, making it feel connected to the form below: users see the button and almost immediately see the beginning of the form, understanding that clicking or scrolling continues the booking process.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static interactive element within the right column. It remains in place in the flow and does not stick or float; on click, it may scroll to or focus the form, but the button’s physical position in the layout does not change.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-request-form occupies a substantial block beneath the booking-cta-button in the right main column. It starts approximately 16–24px below the button, maintaining visual continuity. The form spans nearly the full width of the right column (about 90–100% of that column), with its fields arranged in a straightforward vertical stack. Related fields can appear in two-column rows where screen width allows (e.g., date and time side-by-side, each taking about 48–50% of the right column width with a 16–24px gap), echoing the implicit 12-column grid. Each field has 12–16px vertical spacing from the next, and groups of fields (e.g., contact details vs. visit details) are separated by 24–32px to keep the layout clear but compact. Internal padding around the form’s outer border is about 24px on all sides, forming a simple rectangular form panel. The tour-request-feedback status message appears directly above the first field (see that component), ensuring that success/error messages are immediately noticeable. The form block is tall enough that on most screens it requires some scrolling to see all fields and the submit button, anchoring the lower part of the page’s visual flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static form panel within the right column, scrolling with the page. When the form is in a loading/submitting state, the tour-form-loading-state may overlay or appear inline within this block, but the form’s spatial footprint remains consistent.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-request-feedback component is located inside the right main column as part of the tour-request-form area. It sits directly above the first form field, within the same overall form width, aligned to the left edge. When visible, it appears as a horizontal message bar or panel spanning nearly the full width of the form (90–100% of the right column width), with 8–16px of internal padding and 8–16px of vertical margin separating it from both the heading/intro of the form (if any) and the first input field. Its height is modest—just enough for one or two lines of text—so it does not dominate the layout but remains immediately noticeable. When no status is present, the space can collapse or be reserved as a thin placeholder line so that the form below does not jump drastically, preserving layout stability consistent with the utilitarian feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and inline within the form area. It appears and disappears within the normal flow of the form, pushing content down when visible. It does not overlay other components and does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-info-loading-state occupies the same horizontal region as the tour-info-section (and, if needed, temporarily stands in for the tour-types-list and safety-requirements-list) within the right main column. When active, it appears directly beneath the breadcrumb, aligned with the left edge of the right column. It may be rendered as a centered spinner with one or two skeleton lines that mimic the future positions of the heading and text, contained within a block that spans roughly 60–80% of the right column width and has about 24–32px of top and bottom padding. This loading block extends down far enough (approximately the combined height where the tour info and first part of the lists would appear) to communicate that the main content is being loaded, but it preserves the same general vertical footprint so that when real content replaces it, the page does not shift dramatically. The left sidebar with the facilities-context-link remains visible, providing a stable vertical anchor while the right column content is loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the right column where the tour info and lists will render. It temporarily replaces those components and scrolls with the page. Once loading is complete, it is removed from the DOM, freeing the same space for the actual content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tour-form-loading-state is associated with the tour-request-form area at the lower part of the right main column. When active (e.g., while submitting a booking request or pre-filling details from URL parameters), it appears in the same rectangular region as the form. On desktop, it may overlay the form with a subtle dimmed background and a centered loading spinner or progress indicator, covering approximately 40–60% of the visible height of the form block while respecting its width (about 75–80% of the main container). On simpler implementations, it may temporarily replace the form’s fields with a loading indicator aligned to the left but still padded inside the form panel by about 24px. In either case, the form’s overall block height and position relative to the booking-cta-button and page bottom remain visually consistent, preserving the vertical flow of the page even during loading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Tied to the form’s spatial footprint. It either overlays the form content in a centered fashion (absolute positioning within the form container) or appears inline in place of the fields. It does not move independently of the form and scrolls with the page content.

name: Wishlist
title: Wishlist
description: Page for viewing and managing saved product lists (wishlists/project lists), including switching between lists, creating new lists, and moving saved items into the cart.

core_features: Load all wishlists for the current user using getWishlists and determine which wishlist to display based on the wishlistId URL parameter or the default wishlist flag.
Display items for the currently selected wishlist using getWishlistItems, including product name, size, material, price, thumbnail, and notes.
Allow users to switch between existing wishlists (for example, different project lists) and update the URL wishlistId parameter to reflect the selected list.
Allow users to create a new wishlist/project list by submitting a name, optional description, and default-flag via createWishlist, then immediately display the new (empty) list.
Enable users to move individual wishlist items into the cart by specifying a quantity and calling moveWishlistItemToCart; then refresh the displayed wishlist items.
Enable users to remove items from a wishlist by calling removeWishlistItem and updating the item list after successful deletion.
Provide an empty-state view when the selected wishlist has no items, including navigation to products.html so users can start adding products.

Support list selection and optional new-list naming when saving products (used primarily from product pages) via addProductToWishlist, ensuring items are saved into the chosen or newly created wishlist.
user_workflows:
View the default wishlist:
1. User opens wishlist.html without specifying a wishlistId in the URL.
2. Page controller calls getWishlists.
3. If one or more wishlists exist, the controller selects the wishlist marked is_default. If none are marked as default, it selects the first wishlist returned.
4. The controller updates the URL to wishlist.html?wishlistId=<selectedWishlistId> if needed.
5. The controller calls getWishlistItems(selectedWishlistId).
6. The wishlist items table displays all items for that wishlist, or the empty-state component displays if there are no items.

Open a specific project list:
1. User navigates to wishlist.html?wishlistId=<someId> from another page or bookmark.
2. Page controller calls getWishlists.
3. If a wishlist with id matching the wishlistId parameter exists, it is set as the selected wishlist; otherwise, the controller falls back to the default wishlist selection logic (default list or first list).
4. The controller calls getWishlistItems(selectedWishlistId).
5. Selected wishlist name is highlighted in the list selector, and its items are shown in the items table.

Create a new wishlist/project list:
1. User is on wishlist.html viewing any list and clicks a 'New list' control.
2. The wishlist-create-form is shown, allowing the user to enter a wishlist name, optional description, and whether this list should be the default (is_default flag).
3. User submits the form; the form component calls createWishlist with the entered values.
4. On success, the new Wishlist returned by createWishlist is appended to the list of wishlists in memory, and its id is set as the current selected wishlist.
5. The URL is updated to wishlist.html?wishlistId=<newWishlistId>.
6. The page controller calls getWishlistItems(newWishlistId). The items table shows an empty list with the empty-state component.

Switch between wishlists:
1. User sees multiple wishlists in the wishlist selector (e.g., different project lists).
2. User clicks a wishlist entry (tab/button) corresponding to a different wishlist id.
3. The selector component notifies the page controller of the newly selected wishlistId.
4. The page controller updates the URL wishlistId parameter and calls getWishlistItems(newWishlistId).
5. The items table updates to show items for the newly selected wishlist.

Move an item from wishlist to cart:
1. User views items in the selected wishlist and identifies an item to move to the cart.
2. User enters or confirms a quantity for this item (for example, via a quantity input associated with the item row).
3. User clicks the 'Move to cart' action for that item.
4. The item-actions component calls moveWishlistItemToCart with wishlistItemId for that row and the specified quantity.
5. On success, the component triggers a refresh of the current wishlist items by instructing the page controller to call getWishlistItems(currentWishlistId) again, so the moved item no longer appears.
6. Optionally, a confirmation message is shown and the user can click a 'View cart' link, which navigates to cart.html.

Remove an item from a wishlist:
1. User identifies an item that should be removed (not purchased) from a wishlist.
2. User clicks a 'Remove' action for that item.
3. The item-actions component may ask for confirmation, then calls removeWishlistItem with the wishlistItemId for that row.
4. On success (success = true), the component instructs the page controller to call getWishlistItems(currentWishlistId) again or removes the item from its in-memory list.
5. If no items remain, the empty-state component is shown instead of the items table.

Handle an empty wishlist state:
1. After loading wishlist items for the selected wishlist, getWishlistItems returns an empty items array.
2. The items table component stays hidden, and the empty-state component is displayed.
3. The empty-state component shows context about the selected wishlist and includes a 'Browse products' link.
4. When the user clicks 'Browse products', the browser navigates to products.html.

Save a product to a wishlist (cross-page flow using the same interfaces):
1. On product.html, the user clicks a 'Save to wishlist' control for a product.
2. A reusable wishlist-add-to-list-modal component (defined using the interfaces for this page) opens and calls getWishlists to display existing lists.
3. The user chooses an existing wishlist or types a new list name.
4. The modal calls addProductToWishlist with productId, either wishlistId of the selected list or newListName if creating one, and optional notes.
5. The server may create a new wishlist if newListName is provided and return the created Wishlist and WishlistItem.
6. When the user later visits wishlist.html?wishlistId=<thatListId>, the page controller uses getWishlistItems to show the product that was saved.
interactions: On page load, the page controller reads the wishlistId URL parameter (if present), calls getWishlists, determines the selected wishlist, optionally updates the URL, and then calls getWishlistItems for the selected wishlist.
When the user clicks a wishlist in the wishlist selector, the selected wishlistId state is updated, the URL wishlistId parameter is changed, and getWishlistItems is called for the new wishlist.
When the user clicks the 'New list' control, the wishlist-create-form becomes visible, allowing the user to enter a name, description, and default flag.

When the user submits the wishlist-create-form, the form calls createWishlist; on success, the new wishlist is added to the list of wishlists, set as selected, the URL is updated, and getWishlistItems is called for the new list.
For each wishlist item row, clicking the product name or image navigates to product.html?id=<product_id> without additional API calls.
For each wishlist item row, changing the quantity input updates local component state for that row but does not trigger any API calls until the user decides to move the item to the cart.

Clicking 'Move to cart' for a wishlist item calls moveWishlistItemToCart with the wishlistItemId and current quantity; on success, the items list is refreshed by calling getWishlistItems again, and a 'View cart' navigation control becomes available.
Clicking 'Remove' for a wishlist item calls removeWishlistItem with the wishlistItemId; on success, the item is removed from the UI (or the list is reloaded via getWishlistItems).
If getWishlistItems returns an empty items array, the empty-state component is shown; when the user clicks 'Browse products' in that state, the browser navigates to products.html.

When the reusable wishlist-add-to-list-modal is used (primarily from product.html), opening it triggers getWishlists; selecting an existing list or typing a new list name and submitting triggers addProductToWishlist, and the modal then closes upon success.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The primary state for this page is the currently selected wishlist, identified by the wishlistId URL parameter.

1) URL parameter handling:
- The page reads wishlistId from the query string (wishlist.html or wishlist.html?wishlistId=<id>).
- After calling getWishlists, the page validates wishlistId against the list returned:
  • If wishlistId matches one of the returned wishlists, that wishlist is selected.
  • If wishlistId is missing or does not match any wishlist, the page selects the wishlist where is_default is true (if any).
  • If no wishlist has is_default set, the first wishlist in the getWishlists result is selected.
- If the selected wishlist's id differs from the wishlistId in the URL, the page updates the URL to wishlist.html?wishlistId=<selectedWishlistId> using browser history APIs, without reloading the page.

2) Data loading sequence:
- On initial load or whenever the selected wishlist changes, the controller calls getWishlistItems(selectedWishlistId).
- The result (wishlist metadata and items array) is stored in page state and bound to the relevant components.

3) State updates after createWishlist:
- When createWishlist succeeds, the new Wishlist object is appended to the in-memory list of wishlists from getWishlists.
- The new wishlist id is set as selectedWishlistId.
- The URL wishlistId parameter is updated to the new id.
- getWishlistItems(newWishlistId) is called to get items for that list (typically empty initially).

4) State updates after moveWishlistItemToCart:
- moveWishlistItemToCart takes wishlistItemId and quantity from the current UI state and returns updated Cart, CartItem, and Wishlist data.
- Regardless of the Wishlist object returned, the items for the currently selected wishlist are refreshed by calling getWishlistItems(selectedWishlistId), ensuring the UI reflects that the item has been moved.
- Cart state is not persisted on this page beyond optional display; navigation to cart.html is done via link, without passing cart parameters.

5) State updates after removeWishlistItem:
- When removeWishlistItem returns success = true, the controller either removes the corresponding item from its local items array or, for simplicity and consistency, calls getWishlistItems(selectedWishlistId) again.
- If the refreshed items array is empty, the empty-state component is shown.

6) Interaction with addProductToWishlist (cross-page but same interfaces):
- When used in the wishlist-add-to-list-modal, the modal maintains transient state for the selected wishlistId or the newListName, plus optional notes.
- Submitting the modal calls addProductToWishlist with productId (provided by the host page, typically product.html), the chosen wishlistId or newListName, and notes.
