- The Wishlist and WishlistItem returned do not immediately change state on wishlist.html unless the user navigates to wishlist.html?wishlistId=<thatWishlistId>, at which point the standard getWishlists/getWishlistItems flow loads the updated contents.

No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used; all persistent state is driven by the URL parameters and the data returned from the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Central orchestration component for wishlist.html. On page load, it reads the wishlistId URL parameter, calls getWishlists, determines the selected wishlist, updates the URL if needed, and then calls getWishlistItems for the selected wishlist. It manages in-memory state for the list of wishlists, the currently selected wishlist, and the items belonging to that wishlist. It also responds to child component events (switching lists, creating new lists, moving items to cart, removing items) and triggers the appropriate API calls.

Displays all wishlists for the current user (as returned by getWishlists) and allows the user to switch between them. It highlights the currently selected wishlist and exposes a 'New list' action. When the user selects a different wishlist, it notifies the page controller so the controller can update the URL wishlistId parameter and reload the selected wishlist items.

Allows the user to create a new wishlist or project list. The form collects the wishlist name, optional description, and a boolean indicating whether this list should be the default. On submission, it calls createWishlist with these values. On success, it passes the created Wishlist back to the page controller, which updates the wishlists collection, selects the new wishlist, updates the URL, and loads its items.

Displays the items returned by getWishlistItems for the selected wishlist. Each row shows product-related data (product_name, size_label, material, unit_price, currency, thumbnail_image_url, notes) along with controls for navigating to the product detail page, setting quantity, moving the item to the cart, and removing the item from the wishlist. It delegates per-row actions to the wishlist-item-actions component and displays or hides itself based on whether the items array is empty.

Encapsulates the behavior of the action controls for a single wishlist item row. It manages the quantity input for that item, triggers moveWishlistItemToCart when the user chooses to move the item to the cart, and triggers removeWishlistItem when the user removes the item. After each API call, it notifies the page controller so the list can be refreshed or updated. It also exposes a control to navigate to the product detail page via product.html?id=<product_id>.

Shown when getWishlistItems returns an empty items array for the selected wishlist. It communicates that the selected wishlist has no saved items and offers a 'Browse products' navigation control to direct the user to products.html so they can start saving items. It updates automatically when the page controller detects that the wishlist items array transitions between empty and non-empty.

Reusable modal component (primarily invoked from product pages) that allows a user to save a product into a wishlist. When opened, it calls getWishlists to retrieve existing wishlists, displays them for selection, and also provides an input to specify a new list name. On submit, it calls addProductToWishlist with the provided productId, either the selected wishlistId or a newListName, and optional notes. If newListName is provided, the backend may create a new wishlist and then add the product to it. The component closes upon success and returns the resulting Wishlist and WishlistItem to the host page, so that wishlist.html can later show the updated contents via its normal loading logic.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page inherits the site-wide pattern: a full-width industrial hero at the very top (global template), followed by a solid horizontal divider bar. Directly under the divider sits the main content band, constrained in a central container. This band is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for wishlist navigation and management, and a wider right column for the active wishlist details. In the right column, the page controller (title + description + top-level actions) sits at the top, followed by either the wishlist items table or an empty state message. Inline item actions live inside the table rows, and the add-to-list modal appears as a centered overlay on top of this structure when invoked.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page inherits the site-wide pattern: a full-width industrial hero at the very top (global template), followed by a solid horizontal divider bar. Directly under the divider sits the main content band, constrained in a central container. This band is split into two columns: a narrow left sidebar for wishlist navigation and management, and a wider right column for the active wishlist details. In the right column, the page controller (title + description + top-level actions) sits at the top, followed by either the wishlist items table or an empty state message. Inline item actions live inside the table rows, and the add-to-list modal appears as a centered overlay on top of this structure when invoked.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero consumes roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport height and spans the full width. The main content container below is centered and capped at around 1120–1200px. Inside this, the left sidebar occupies about 22–25% of the container width, while the right main column uses the remaining 75–78%. Vertical spacing between page controller, table/empty state, and sidebar sections follows a 32–40px rhythm, with component padding mostly in 16–24px increments. The modal overlay is slightly narrower than half the viewport on desktop (about 480–640px wide) and vertically centered.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero stays open with a large image and bold central title. Below, the body is moderately dense: sidebar list items are stacked with tight vertical rhythm (8–16px between items), and the table rows are compact but legible, with about 56–64px row height. Internal padding for cards, table cells, and form fields follows a 16–24px standard, avoiding excessive whitespace. Copy blocks under headings have comfortable but not airy spacing, matching a utilitarian, functional corporate aesthetic.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The user’s eye is drawn first to the hero title in the global template, then down to the horizontal divider. From there, attention moves immediately to the right-column page controller heading (“Wishlists” / current list name). After reading the heading and its short description, the eye drops down into the main wishlist content: either the items table or the empty state. The left sidebar acts as a secondary vertical anchor; when users want to change lists, their gaze shifts to the left, scans down the list selector and creation form, then returns to the right column where the items update. Within the table, the eye scans rows left-to-right, ending on the inline item actions on the far right. When the add-to-list modal is open, it briefly becomes the focal point by being centered and dimming the rest of the layout.

layout.overall_description: The Wishlist page sits beneath the site’s standard full-width hero: a large industrial photo with a centered, bold company or site title. A solid gray horizontal divider runs the full width directly under the hero, clearly separating the hero from the functional content. Below this divider, a centered content container appears, with modest side margins to create a subtle frame. Inside this container, the layout splits into two columns: a narrow left sidebar and a wider right main panel. The left sidebar is a vertical control center for lists, containing the wishlist list selector at the top and a compact create-new-list form beneath it, all stacked and aligned to the left with moderate but efficient spacing. The right main panel starts with the wishlist page controller: a strong page heading aligned to the left, optionally accompanied by a short description and high-level actions aligned along the same horizontal band. Beneath this, the available vertical space is devoted either to a dense wishlist items table (if the active list has items) or to a centered empty state block if it doesn’t. The table spans nearly the full width of the right column with rows that feel functional and data-driven, and the inline item actions sit at the far right of each row, keeping operational controls close to the edge. When the user triggers a cross-list action, a mid-sized modal appears centered on the screen, overlaying a dimmed version of this entire two-column layout. The result is a straightforward, industrial-leaning page that mirrors the original mockup’s hero-plus-two-column pattern and supports a clear flow from high-level context to detailed list management.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-page-controller is placed at the top of the right main content column, directly below the horizontal divider and aligned with the right column’s left edge. It spans the full width of the right column (approximately 75–78% of the central content container’s width). The controller is structured as a compact header block: a large, left-aligned page title (e.g., the active wishlist name or simply 'Wishlists') sits at the top of this block, using a bold type size that visually anchors the content area. Directly under the title, separated by about 8–12px, sits a single short paragraph of descriptive copy or helper text, spanning at most 70% of the right column’s width to avoid overly long line lengths. To the right of the title (in the same horizontal band on larger screens), there is room for primary page-level actions (such as 'Move all to cart' or 'Delete list'), right-aligned with 16–24px padding from the column’s right edge, forming a subtle top utility row. The entire controller block has about 24–32px of vertical padding above and below, creating a clear break between the global divider above and the items table or empty state below. This section is visually prominent and is the first element in the main content area that users engage with after the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element anchored to the top of the right content column; it scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick or overlap other sections.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-list-selector occupies the upper portion of the left sidebar column, starting just below the horizontal divider and aligned flush with the left edge of the central content container. The sidebar column itself takes up roughly 22–25% of the container width. Inside this column, the list selector is a vertical stack of elements: a small section heading ('Your lists' or similar) at the top, followed by the list of saved wishlists. Each wishlist entry appears as a horizontally full-width row within the sidebar, with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding on both sides, constrained by the 8px grid. Items are separated by either a subtle dividing line or 4–8px of vertical spacing, creating a compact but scannable list. The active list is highlighted with a stronger background or left border to reinforce its role as the current 'section' in this navigation-like sidebar. The list selector extends downwards as far as needed, but roughly the top 40–50% of the sidebar height is reserved for it before the create form begins. If there are many lists, the inner region of this selector can scroll independently within the sidebar while the section heading stays pinned at the top, maintaining the sense of a fixed vertical navigation in line with the mockup’s left nav pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Occupies the top of the left sidebar and can use a sticky inner container: the section header remains pinned to the top of the sidebar as the list of wishlists scrolls, giving a persistent navigation feel while the main page scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-create-form is positioned directly below the wishlist-list-selector within the left sidebar, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space to signal a change from navigation to creation. It spans the full width of the sidebar column, maintaining the same left and right margins as the list selector for visual alignment. At the top of this form block, a small heading or label ('Create new list') and possibly a brief helper line of text are left-aligned, with 8–12px spacing between them. Below, the main form controls (e.g., a single-line text input for the list name and a primary 'Create' button) are stacked vertically, each using 16–24px vertical padding and aligning to the 8px grid. If an inline trigger is used instead (for example, a 'New list' link or button), it appears left-aligned and compact, with the full form expanding below it when activated but still staying within the sidebar width. Overall, the form takes up roughly the bottom 40–60% of the sidebar height depending on content volume, but it visually remains a secondary panel relative to the list selector and the main right-column content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element stacked in the left sidebar beneath the list selector; it scrolls with the sidebar content. If the sidebar itself becomes scrollable, the form scrolls as part of that column rather than sticking.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-items-table sits in the right main content column directly beneath the wishlist-page-controller, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans nearly the full width of the right column, maintaining consistent left and right padding (16–24px) inside a rectangular table container to align with the rest of the layout. The table header row appears at the top, with column labels (e.g., Product, Specification, Quantity, Notes, Actions) left-aligned except for the actions column, which is right-aligned and narrower. The table body fills the remaining vertical space, with each row having a height of roughly 56–64px to balance readability with moderate density. Rows extend horizontally from the left padding of the right column to near the right edge, leaving a small 16px inner margin so it doesn’t feel edge-to-edge cramped. There is 8–12px vertical spacing between rows or a very subtle horizontal rule for separation. On tall screens, the table can occupy more than half the viewport height, encouraging vertical scrolling when there are many items. This component is visually dominant in the right column and acts as the main content area into which the user’s attention flows after the page controller.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the right column that scrolls naturally with the main page content. The table can internally scroll if constrained by a fixed-height container in specific implementations, but conceptually it is a standard scrolling list of rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-item-actions component is embedded within each row of the wishlist-items-table as the rightmost column. In every row, this actions cell aligns flush with the table’s right padding, occupying about 15–20% of the row width. The actions themselves (e.g., 'Add to cart', 'Move', 'Remove') are arranged horizontally within this cell, right-aligned, with 8–12px spacing between individual buttons or links. Each action is compact—text buttons or small icon-plus-label controls—designed to feel functional and slightly dense rather than overly padded. On hover, each action stands out with a simple underline or background change, but visually they remain tertiary to the main product information in the left columns. Vertical alignment ensures the actions sit centered within the row height, aligning with the text baseline of the item name for a clean, industrial look. This keeps the operational controls consistently located at the far right edge of the user’s scanning path for each row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element anchored in the rightmost cell of each table row; it moves along with the row as the table scrolls. No sticky or floating behavior—controls remain within their respective rows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-empty-state occupies the same spatial region as the wishlist-items-table within the right main content column and appears only when the active wishlist contains no items. It sits directly beneath the wishlist-page-controller, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px to give it a bit more breathing room than the denser table. The empty state is centered within the width of the right column but left-anchored content-wise: a simple illustration or icon (if used) appears at the upper center of the block, with the main empty-state headline and a short explanatory paragraph aligned slightly below it. The text is horizontally centered relative to the right column but keeps line lengths moderate (no more than 60–70 characters wide). Below the text, a primary call-to-action button (e.g., 'Browse products' or 'Add items to this list') is centered, with about 16–24px vertical spacing from the body copy. The block uses generous internal padding—about 40–48px top and bottom—to visually distinguish it from the more data-dense table view, yet it still adheres to the functional corporate feel by avoiding overly decorative elements. This component visually fills the central vertical band of the right column, drawing attention without overshadowing the left sidebar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that conditionally replaces the wishlist-items-table within the right column. It scrolls with the main content and has no sticky or overlay behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The wishlist-add-to-list-modal appears as an overlay on top of the entire page when triggered, dimming the background hero, sidebar, and main content with a semi-transparent dark layer. The modal itself is centered both horizontally and vertically within the viewport on desktop, occupying roughly 40–50% of the viewport width (about 480–640px) and auto-sized in height based on content, up to around 60–70% of viewport height before its interior becomes scrollable. It has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides, aligned with the site’s 8px spacing system. At the top of the modal, a clear, left-aligned title ('Add to list' or 'Move to wishlist') appears, with an optional short explanatory line below it spaced by 8–12px. The main body of the modal contains a vertical list selector for available wishlists (similar in style to the sidebar list but more compact), potentially with radio buttons or highlight states, and might include a small inline form element (like a quick-create new list field) beneath it. At the bottom, a horizontal action row runs full width: primary action button on the right, secondary/cancel action to its left, with 8–16px spacing. The modal uses a simple rectangular shape with subtle shadow, consistent with the utilitarian, slightly old-school aesthetic rather than a highly polished dialog.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, sitting above a dimmed, non-interactive background. It remains in place while the underlying page does not scroll (or scroll is locked) until the modal is dismissed. If its content exceeds available height, the modal body scrolls internally.

name: Policies
title: Policies
description: Displays shipping, returns, warranty, terms of use, and privacy policies, with deep-linking via URL parameters and optional navigation back to checkout.

core_features: Load and display all shipping methods and free-shipping conditions using getShippingPolicies.
Load and display all policy documents (shipping, returns, warranty, terms of use, privacy) using getPolicyDocuments.
Highlight and scroll to a specific policy section based on the doc URL parameter.
Provide an in-page table of contents for quick navigation between policy sections.
Conditionally show a 'Back to Checkout' navigation link when the user arrives from checkout, preserving their checkout context via URL parameters.
Allow users to update the focused policy section by clicking table-of-contents links, updating the doc URL parameter accordingly.
user_workflows: Viewing all policies from the footer: 1) User clicks the 'Policies' footer link (without parameters). 2) policies.html loads and the controller component calls getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments in parallel. 3) The table of contents shows all available policy documents. 4) The shipping section displays all shipping methods and free-shipping conditions. 5) Policy document sections are rendered below with their titles and text from the API. 6) User scrolls or uses the table of contents to read specific sections.

Deep-link from another page to a specific policy: 1) Another page links to policies.html?doc=<policy_code> (for example one of the codes exposed by getPolicyDocuments). 2) On load, the controller reads the doc parameter and stores it as activeDocCode. 3) The page calls getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments. 4) After data loads, the deep-link manager locates the section whose code matches activeDocCode. 5) The page scrolls to that section and visually marks it as focused. 6) The corresponding entry in the table of contents is also marked as active.

Arriving from checkout to review a policy: 1) checkout.html links to policies.html with doc=<policy_code> and an additional source=checkout parameter. 2) The controller reads both doc and source from the URL. 3) As above, the page loads policy data and focuses the requested section. 4) Because source=checkout, the 'Back to Checkout' link is rendered. 5) User reviews the required policy. 6) User clicks 'Back to Checkout'; the navigation component sends the user back to checkout.html, optionally forwarding any relevant URL parameters that were present (such as doc or a checkout session identifier supplied by checkout.html).

Switching policy sections using the table of contents: 1) User is on policies.html with data loaded. 2) User clicks a different policy item in the table of contents. 3) The click handler updates the doc URL parameter via history.pushState (or a full reload) to the clicked policy’s code. 4) activeDocCode is updated and the deep-link manager scrolls to the corresponding section. 5) The table of contents updates the active state to the newly selected policy.

Reviewing shipping methods and free-shipping conditions: 1) User opens policies.html (from any source). 2) The controller calls getShippingPolicies and receives a list of ShippingMethod records and free_shipping_conditions text. 3) The shipping section lists each shipping method with its name, description, price, currency, delivery window fields, and regional availability, as provided. 4) The section also displays the free_shipping_conditions string. 5) If the user changes the focused policy via the table of contents to the shipping policy code, the page scrolls back to this shipping section.
interactions: Page load triggers calls to getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments; once resolved, all dependent components render from the returned data.
Parsing of URL parameters (doc and optional source) on initial load and on URL changes to determine the active policy section and whether to show the 'Back to Checkout' link.
Clicking a table-of-contents item updates the doc URL parameter, sets the active policy code, scrolls to the target section, and updates active highlighting.

Clicking the 'Back to Checkout' link navigates to checkout.html, optionally re-attaching any parameters originally provided by checkout.html (for example, a checkout session identifier if present in the URL).

Scrolling the page updates the internal notion of which section is currently in view (optional enhancement), which can be used to synchronize the active table-of-contents entry without extra API calls (no local storage).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and API responses. On load, the controller parses the query string. The doc parameter, if present, is treated as the code of the policy that should be focused (matching one of the codes returned in getPolicyDocuments.documents[].code, such as 'shipping_policy', 'returns_policy', 'warranty_policy', 'terms_of_use', or 'privacy_policy'). The controller stores this as activeDocCode. After getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments resolve, a deep-link manager uses activeDocCode to scroll to and highlight the corresponding section (for shipping_policy, this points to the shipping section; for other codes, to their respective document sections). When the user clicks a table-of-contents item, the click handler updates the doc query parameter (e.g., ?doc=returns_policy) via navigation, and the controller updates activeDocCode accordingly, re-running the highlight/scroll behavior. To distinguish visits from checkout, checkout.html can append an optional source=checkout parameter when linking to policies.html (e.g., policies.html?doc=shipping_policy&source=checkout). When source=checkout is present, the 'Back to Checkout' component renders and uses the original URL (including any additional parameters supplied by checkout.html) to navigate back. No persistent state is stored in localStorage; all navigational state is reconstructed from the URL and fresh calls to getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments.

components.functionality: Initializes the Policies page, parses URL parameters, calls getShippingPolicies and getPolicyDocuments, and distributes the resulting data and active policy code to child components.

Displays shipping policy information using the shipping methods and free-shipping conditions returned by getShippingPolicies. It maps ShippingMethod fields to a structured list (name, description, price, currency, min/max delivery days, regions, and free/eligible flags) and presents the free_shipping_conditions text. It exposes an anchor ID associated with the 'shipping_policy' code so that deep-links and the table of contents can scroll to it.

Iterates over all policy documents returned by getPolicyDocuments and renders one policy-detail-section component per document. Each section is associated with an anchor ID derived from documents[].code so that it can be targeted by the doc URL parameter and the table of contents.

Represents a single policy document section (such as returns, warranty, terms of use, or privacy). Uses the document.title and document.content_snippet fields to render the section’s heading and body. Associates the section with an anchor ID derived from document.code, and applies a focused state when document.code matches activeDocCode.

Displays a list of links to each policy section, including the shipping section and all documents returned by getPolicyDocuments. Clicking a link updates the doc URL parameter to the corresponding code and scrolls to the associated section. The currently active policy (based on activeDocCode) is visually distinguished.

Coordinates deep-linking behavior for the doc URL parameter. On initialization and whenever the URL or activeDocCode changes, it scrolls the page to the section whose anchor ID corresponds to activeDocCode and updates the browser history when the user selects a different policy in the table of contents.

Conditionally renders a 'Back to Checkout' link when the optional source=checkout URL parameter is detected. When clicked, navigates to checkout.html, optionally preserving any parameters originally passed through (for example, a checkout or session identifier) without making additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit 12-column-like grid with a full-width hero and a two-column body (narrow sidebar + wider content). Spacing and alignment suggest a standard 8px-based layout typical of Google Sites templates.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom flow: a full-width hero (handled by the global template) with an industrial image and a centered "Policies" hero title, followed by a bold horizontal divider. Directly beneath this, the policies content lives in a two-column band inside a centered content container: a narrow left sidebar for the policies-table-of-contents navigation list and a wider right column for all policy content. Within the right column, a large page title and brief explanatory copy (template) appear first, followed by the back-to-checkout-link aligned to the top-right, then the shipping-policy-section as the first detailed policy, the policy-documents-section-list as a scannable index/grid of policies, and finally the policy-detail-section as the main deep content area. Non-visual components (policies-page-controller, policy-deeplink-manager) sit behind everything, managing state but not affecting the visual flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s top-to-bottom flow: a full-width hero (handled by the global template) with an industrial image and a centered "Policies" hero title, followed by a bold horizontal divider. Directly beneath this, the policies content lives in a two-column band inside a centered content container: a narrow left sidebar for the policies-table-of-contents navigation list and a wider right column for all policy content. Within the right column, a large page title and brief explanatory copy (template) appear first, followed by the back-to-checkout-link aligned to the top-right, then the shipping-policy-section as the first detailed policy, the policy-documents-section-list as a scannable index/grid of policies, and finally the policy-detail-section as the main deep content area. Non-visual components (policies-page-controller, policy-deeplink-manager) sit behind everything, managing state but not affecting the visual flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The hero occupies roughly the top 35–40% of the initial viewport height and spans the full width. Below the divider, the central content container is constrained (e.g., ~1100–1200px max-width) and centered with equal left and right margins. Inside this container, the left sidebar (policies-table-of-contents) uses about 20–25% of the content width, while the right main content column occupies the remaining 75–80%. Vertical spacing between major content blocks in the main column (heading, shipping-policy-section, policy-documents-section-list, policy-detail-section) is in the 32–40px range, while internal padding within each section is around 16–24px. All spacing adheres to an 8px base grid.
layout_strategies.content_density: The hero is visually open with a large image and a prominent centered title. Below the divider, the density increases to a functional, slightly compact level. The left table of contents shows links stacked closely with 8–12px vertical gaps and modest padding, emphasizing utility. In the main column, body text is readable with 16–24px line spacing and section padding, but there is not excessive whitespace. The policy-documents-section-list echoes the product-grid feel from the mockup: a moderately dense list or grid of policy items with 16–24px gaps between items. The policy-detail-section uses readable but compact text layout, suitable for longer legal content while still respecting margins.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts in the center of the hero at the large "Policies" hero title, drops down to the smaller location/context text and any top navigation, then is led to the bold horizontal divider. From there, the eye moves into the two-column band: it first registers the large page title in the right column, then notices the back-to-checkout-link on the upper right. After scanning the introductory text, the viewer’s gaze continues down to the prominent shipping-policy-section, then down again into the policy-documents-section-list, which acts like a visual index. As the user focuses on specifics, attention shifts horizontally to the left sidebar (policies-table-of-contents) to jump between policies; selecting a link brings focus back to the right, where the policy-detail-section expands or updates. The table of contents acts as an anchored vertical reference that the user can quickly glance at when deciding what to read next.

layout.overall_description: From top to bottom, the page feels like a straightforward corporate site with an industrial, image-driven hero spanning the full browser width. A large, centered "Policies" title sits over a broad factory or metalworking image, framed by ample top and bottom padding so it feels strong but not fancy. Directly below, a solid gray horizontal rule runs edge to edge, clearly separating the hero from the main content. Beneath this, the content is pulled into a centered container with modest left and right margins. The container is split into two vertical columns: on the left, a slim sidebar with a vertical list of policy links; on the right, a wider main column containing the page title, a short explanatory paragraph, and a right-aligned "Back to checkout" link near the top. Scrolling down the right column, the user first encounters a clearly labeled shipping policy block, then a structured list or grid of policy documents, and lastly a deeper policy detail panel that changes based on what is selected. The left-hand table of contents remains visually steady along the side, roughly aligned with the top of the main column and tracking the user’s scroll, making the whole experience feel anchored and utilitarian, very much in line with a no-nonsense, Google-Sites-like corporate template.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-page-controller is a non-visual wrapper that sits at the root of the Policies page content area, just below the global hero and horizontal divider. It spans the full width of the central content container and extends from the top of the two-column band down to the bottom of all policy content, conceptually enclosing the sidebar and main content columns. It does not render any visible UI; instead, it governs data loading and routing for all policy-related components within this structured layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered structural controller that occupies no visual space but is scoped to the entire scrollable area of the Policies page content band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the right main content column, the shipping-policy-section is the first substantial content block after the page title, intro text, and the back-to-checkout link. It is aligned to the left edge of the main column and spans 100% of the main column’s width (approximately 75–80% of the inner content container). There is a clear vertical gap of about 32px below the introductory paragraph before this section begins, reinforcing that it is the primary policy users care about when arriving from checkout. The section itself is card-like: it has internal padding of about 16–24px on all sides, with its heading near the top left and body text flowing beneath. Its bottom edge sits roughly 32px above the next block (policy-documents-section-list), creating a distinct yet compact separation. When the user selects "Shipping" in the table of contents, the page scrolls so that this section’s heading aligns near the top of the viewport, making it feel like the anchored top of the detail view.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width block within the main column; may act as an anchor target for in-page navigation but does not float or stick.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-documents-section-list is placed directly beneath the shipping-policy-section in the right main content column, separated by approximately 32px of vertical whitespace. It extends the full width of the main column and is visually framed as a distinct band of content, echoing a product grid. At the top of this band, a small heading or label (e.g., "All Policies" or "Policy Documents") aligns with the main column’s left edge. Below that, individual policy entries are arranged in a structured layout: on larger screens, they can appear as a two-column grid of compact cards, each about 45–48% of the main column’s width with 16–24px horizontal and vertical gaps between them; on smaller screens, they stack in a single column. Each card uses 16–20px internal padding, with the policy title at the top and a brief summary or metadata below, delivering a moderately dense but orderly index. The bottom of this section is separated by roughly 32–40px from the policy-detail-section that follows, making it clear that the list is for navigation/overview while the detail below is for in-depth reading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, grid- or list-style block that expands vertically as items are added; aligns with the main column and scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-detail-section occupies the lower portion of the right main content column, directly below the policy-documents-section-list, with approximately 32–40px of vertical space between them. It spans the full width of the main column and is designed for longer-form text: a policy title at the top left, followed by structured paragraphs, subsections, and possibly subheadings. The section’s internal padding is about 24px on all sides, slightly more generous than the list above to support comfortable reading of dense legal text. The body text is left-aligned and uses consistent spacing between paragraphs (around 16px), producing a compact but readable block. As the user selects different items in the policies-table-of-contents or in the policy-documents-section-list, this section’s content updates while its physical location remains fixed in the page layout, creating a stable reading area near the bottom of the main column that can extend far down the page as needed. The top of this section can also serve as an anchor, so that deep links scroll the user to this area with the relevant policy title visible near the top of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content area within the main column that updates its content based on selection and deep-link state but does not change its positional footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policies-table-of-contents forms the left sidebar navigation within the two-column band. It begins near the top of the content band, vertically aligned with the baseline of the main column’s page title. The sidebar occupies roughly 20–25% of the inner content container’s width and stretches downward alongside the main column content. Within this sidebar, a simple heading (e.g., "Policies") appears at the top left, followed by a vertical list of navigation links such as Shipping, Returns, Warranty, Terms of Use, and Privacy. Each link is left-aligned, with 8–12px of vertical spacing between items and 16–20px padding from the sidebar’s edges, resulting in a compact, functional column of text. There is limited decorative styling; the emphasis is on clear readability and quick scanning. Even as the main content on the right extends well below the fold, this sidebar remains visually distinct, providing a consistent vertical reference and making it easy to jump to different policy sections.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Sticky within the viewport on larger screens: the sidebar remains fixed relative to the top of the viewport after the user scrolls past the hero, with a small top offset (e.g., 64–80px) so it stays visible while the main column scrolls independently. On smaller screens, it collapses into a non-sticky block above the main content and scrolls normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The policy-deeplink-manager is an invisible layer that sits alongside the policies-page-controller, conceptually anchored to the entire policy content area rather than a specific visual region. It occupies the same horizontal span as the full content container and tracks the full vertical range of the page content. While it does not present any UI directly, it coordinates the state of the policies-table-of-contents, the policy-documents-section-list, the shipping-policy-section, and the policy-detail-section so that URL parameters and in-page interactions scroll or focus the user on the correct section within the existing layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered state manager scoped to the full policies page; has no spatial footprint and does not affect alignment or spacing of visible components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-checkout-link is positioned in the upper right area of the main content column, just beneath the horizontal divider and aligned on the same vertical band as the page title and introductory copy. In a typical desktop layout, the page title sits flush left at the top of the main column, with the back-to-checkout-link flush right on the same horizontal line or slightly below it, separated from the title by a flexible gap of 40–60% of the main column’s width. There is about 16–24px of vertical space between the divider and this row so it does not feel cramped. The link itself is styled as a subtle text link or small button, not larger than a single line of text, with minimal padding (8–12px horizontal, 4–8px vertical) so it is discoverable but not dominant. A 16–24px gap separates this row from the introductory paragraph below. On smaller screens, the link wraps beneath the title but remains right-aligned within the main column, maintaining its role as an optional, secondary action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned inline element within the top section of the main column; it scrolls away with the content and is not sticky.
